					<div id="mw-content-text" class="mw-body-content"><div class="mw-subjectpageheader">
</div><div class="mw-content-ltr mw-parser-output" lang="en" dir="ltr"><div class="shortdescription nomobile noexcerpt noprint searchaux" style="display:none">Country in South Asia</div>
<style data-mw-deduplicate="TemplateStyles:r1320445320">.mw-parser-output .hatnote{font-style:italic}.mw-parser-output div.hatnote{padding-left:1.6em;margin-bottom:0.5em}.mw-parser-output .hatnote i{font-style:normal}.mw-parser-output .hatnote+link+.hatnote{margin-top:-0.5em}@media print{body.ns-0 .mw-parser-output .hatnote{display:none!important}}</style><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">This article is about the country. For other uses, see <a href="/wiki/India_(disambiguation)" class="mw-disambig" title="India (disambiguation)">India (disambiguation)</a>.</div>
<p class="mw-empty-elt">

        </div>
        <div style="display:table-cell; vertical-align:middle; padding: 0px 5px;">
            <div style="padding-bottom:3px;"><span class="skin-invert-image" typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:Emblem_of_India.svg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/Emblem_of_India.svg/60px-Emblem_of_India.svg.png" decoding="async" width="60" height="96" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/Emblem_of_India.svg/120px-Emblem_of_India.svg.png 1.5x" data-file-width="550" data-file-height="877" /></a></span></div>
            <div><a href="/wiki/State_Emblem_of_India" title="State Emblem of India">State emblem</a></div>
        </div>
    </div></td></tr><tr><td colspan="2" class="infobox-full-data"><b>Motto:&#160;</b><span title="Sanskrit-language text"><i lang="sa-Latn"><a href="/wiki/Satyameva_Jayate" title="Satyameva Jayate">Satyameva Jayate</a></i></span>&#160;<span class="languageicon" style="font-size:100%; data-mw-deduplicate="TemplateStyles:r1239334494">@media screen{html.skin-theme-clientpref-night .mw-parser-output div:not(.notheme)>.tmp-color,html.skin-theme-clientpref-night .mw-parser-output p>.tmp-color,html.skin-theme-clientpref-night .mw-parser-output table:not(.notheme) .tmp-color{color:inherit!important}}@media screen and (prefers-color-scheme:dark){html.skin-theme-clientpref-os .mw-parser-output div:not(.notheme)>.tmp-color,html.skin-theme-clientpref-os .mw-parser-output p>.tmp-color,html.skin-theme-clientpref-os .mw-parser-output table:not(.notheme) .tmp-color{color:inherit!important}}</style><span class="tmp-color" style="color:forestgreen">low inequality</span></span></td></tr><tr><th scope="row" class="infobox-label"><a href="/wiki/Human_Development_Index" title="Human Development Index">HDI</a>&#160;<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r886047488" /><span class="nobold">(2023)</span></th><td class="infobox-data"><span typeof="mw:File"><span title="Increase"><img alt="Increase" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/Increase2.svg/20px-Increase2.svg.png" decoding="async" width="11" height="11" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/Increase2.svg/40px-Increase2.svg.png 2x" data-file-width="300" data-file-height="300" /></span></span>&#160;0.685<sup id="cite&#95;ref-UNHDR&#95;26-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-UNHDR-26"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>18<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><br /><span class="nowrap"><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1239334494" /><span class="tmp-color" style="color:orange">medium</span></span>&#160;(<a href="/wiki/List_of_countries_by_Human_Development_Index" title="List of countries by Human Development Index">130th</a>)</td></tr><tr><th scope="row" class="infobox-label">Currency</th><td class="infobox-data"><a href="/wiki/Indian_rupee" title="Indian rupee">Indian rupee</a> (<a href="/wiki/%E2%82%B9" class="mw-redirect" title="₹">₹</a>) (<a href="/wiki/ISO_4217" title="ISO 4217">INR</a>)</td></tr><tr><th scope="row" class="infobox-label">Time zone</th><td class="infobox-data"><span class="nowrap"><a href="/wiki/Coordinated_Universal_Time" title="Coordinated Universal Time">UTC</a>+05:30</span> (<a href="/wiki/Indian_Standard_Time" title="Indian Standard Time">IST</a>)</td></tr><tr><th scope="row" class="infobox-label">Date format</th><td class="infobox-data"><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1126788409" /><div class="plainlist"><ul><li><span class="nowrap"><abbr title="day">dd</abbr>-<abbr title="month">mm</abbr>-<abbr title="year">yyyy</abbr></span><sup id="cite&#95;ref-27" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-27"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>i<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></li></ul></div></td></tr><tr><th scope="row" class="infobox-label"><a href="/wiki/Telephone_numbers_in_India" title="Telephone numbers in India">Calling code</a></th><td class="infobox-data"><a href="/wiki/Telephone_numbers_in_India" title="Telephone numbers in India">+91</a></td></tr><tr><th scope="row" class="infobox-label"><a href="/wiki/ISO_3166" title="ISO 3166">ISO 3166 code</a></th><td class="infobox-data"><a href="/wiki/ISO_3166-2:IN" title="ISO 3166-2:IN">IN</a></td></tr><tr><th scope="row" class="infobox-label"><a href="/wiki/Country_code_top-level_domain" title="Country code top-level domain">Internet TLD</a></th><td class="infobox-data"><a href="/wiki/.in" title=".in">.in</a> (<a href="/wiki/.in#Internationalised_domain_names_and_country_codes" title=".in">others</a>)</td></tr></tbody></table>
<p><b>India</b>, officially the <b>Republic of India</b>,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-29" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-29"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>j<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-30" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-30"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>20<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> is a country in <a href="/wiki/South_Asia" title="South Asia">South Asia</a>.  It is the <a href="/wiki/List_of_countries_and_dependencies_by_area" title="List of countries and dependencies by area">seventh-largest country by area</a>; the <a href="/wiki/List_of_countries_and_dependencies_by_population" title="List of countries and dependencies by population">most populous country</a> since 2023;<sup id="cite&#95;ref-31" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-31"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>21<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> and, since its independence in 1947, the world's most populous democracy.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-32" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-32"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>22<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-stein-arnold&#95;33-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-stein-arnold-33"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>23<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEFisher2018184–185&#95;34-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFisher2018184–185-34"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>24<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Bounded by the <a href="/wiki/Indian_Ocean" title="Indian Ocean">Indian Ocean</a> on the south, the <a href="/wiki/Arabian_Sea" title="Arabian Sea">Arabian Sea</a> on the southwest, and the <a href="/wiki/Bay_of_Bengal" title="Bay of Bengal">Bay of Bengal</a> on the southeast, it shares land borders with <a href="/wiki/Pakistan" title="Pakistan">Pakistan</a> to the west;<sup id="cite&#95;ref-36" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-36"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>k<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> <a href="/wiki/China" title="China">China</a>, <a href="/wiki/Nepal" title="Nepal">Nepal</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Bhutan" title="Bhutan">Bhutan</a> to the north; and <a href="/wiki/Bangladesh" title="Bangladesh">Bangladesh</a> and <a href="/wiki/Myanmar" title="Myanmar">Myanmar</a> to the east. In the <a href="/wiki/Indian_Ocean" title="Indian Ocean">Indian Ocean</a>, India is near <a href="/wiki/Sri_Lanka" title="Sri Lanka">Sri Lanka</a> and the <a href="/wiki/Maldives" title="Maldives">Maldives</a>; its <a href="/wiki/Andaman_and_Nicobar_Islands" title="Andaman and Nicobar Islands">Andaman and Nicobar Islands</a> share a <a href="/wiki/Maritime_boundary" title="Maritime boundary">maritime border</a> with Myanmar, <a href="/wiki/Thailand" title="Thailand">Thailand</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Indonesia" title="Indonesia">Indonesia</a>.
</p><p><a href="/wiki/Modern_humans" class="mw-redirect" title="Modern humans">Modern humans</a> arrived on the <a href="/wiki/Indian_subcontinent" title="Indian subcontinent">Indian subcontinent</a> from <a href="/wiki/Africa" title="Africa">Africa</a> no later than 55,000 years ago.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-PetragliaAllchin&#95;37-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-PetragliaAllchin-37"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>26<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-Dyson2018p1&#95;38-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Dyson2018p1-38"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>27<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-Fisher2018p23&#95;39-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Fisher2018p23-39"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>28<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Their long occupation, predominantly in isolation as hunter-gatherers, has made the region highly diverse.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Dyson2018-28a&#95;40-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Dyson2018-28a-40"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>29<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Neolithic" title="Neolithic">Settled life</a> emerged on the subcontinent in the western margins of the <a href="/wiki/Indus_River" title="Indus River">Indus</a> <a href="/wiki/River_basin" class="mw-redirect" title="River basin">river basin</a> 9,000 years ago, evolving gradually into the <a href="/wiki/Indus_Valley_Civilisation" title="Indus Valley Civilisation">Indus Valley Civilisation</a> of the third millennium BCE.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Combined-2&#95;41-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Combined-2-41"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>30<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> By 1200&#160;<span title="Before Common Era">BCE</span>, an <a href="/wiki/Proto-language" title="Proto-language">archaic form</a> of <a href="/wiki/Sanskrit" title="Sanskrit">Sanskrit</a>, an <a href="/wiki/Indo-European_language" class="mw-redirect" title="Indo-European language">Indo-European language</a>, had <a href="/wiki/Trans-cultural_diffusion" class="mw-redirect" title="Trans-cultural diffusion">diffused</a> into India from the northwest.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-lowe1&#95;42-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-lowe1-42"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>31<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-Combined-4-Rigveda&#95;43-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Combined-4-Rigveda-43"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>32<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Rig_Veda" class="mw-redirect" title="Rig Veda">Its hymns</a> recorded the <a href="/wiki/Historical_Vedic_religion" title="Historical Vedic religion">early dawnings</a> of <a href="/wiki/Hinduism" title="Hinduism">Hinduism</a> in India.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Combined-3&#95;44-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Combined-3-44"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>33<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> India's pre-existing <a href="/wiki/Dravidian_languages" title="Dravidian languages">Dravidian languages</a> were supplanted in the northern regions.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Combined-4&#95;45-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Combined-4-45"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>34<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> By 400&#160;<span title="Before Common Era">BCE</span>, <a href="/wiki/Caste" title="Caste">caste</a> had emerged within Hinduism,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Dyson2018-16a&#95;46-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Dyson2018-16a-46"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>35<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> and <a href="/wiki/History_of_Buddhism_in_India" title="History of Buddhism in India">Buddhism</a> and <a href="/wiki/History_of_Jainism" title="History of Jainism">Jainism</a> had arisen, proclaiming <a href="/wiki/Social_order" title="Social order">social orders</a> unlinked to heredity.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Fisher2018-59&#95;47-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Fisher2018-59-47"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>36<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Early political consolidations gave rise to the loose-knit <a href="/wiki/Maurya_Empire" title="Maurya Empire">Maurya</a> and <a href="/wiki/Gupta_Empire" title="Gupta Empire">Gupta Empires</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Combined-5&#95;48-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Combined-5-48"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>37<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> This era was noted for creativity in art, architecture, and writing,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Combined-6&#95;49-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Combined-6-49"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>38<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> but the status of women declined,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Combined-7&#95;50-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Combined-7-50"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>39<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> and <a href="/wiki/Untouchability" title="Untouchability">untouchability</a> became an organised belief.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-52" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-52"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>l<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEKulkeRothermund2016122–123&#95;51-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKulkeRothermund2016122–123-51"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>40<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In <a href="/wiki/South_India" title="South India">South India</a>, the <a href="/wiki/Middle_kingdoms_of_India#The_Deccan_plateau_and_South" title="Middle kingdoms of India">Middle kingdoms</a> exported Dravidian language scripts and religious cultures to the kingdoms of <a href="/wiki/Southeast_Asia" title="Southeast Asia">Southeast Asia</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-AsherAsher2006-17&#95;53-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-AsherAsher2006-17-53"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>41<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>In the <a href="/wiki/1st_millennium" title="1st millennium">1st millennium</a>, <a href="/wiki/Islam" title="Islam">Islam</a>, <a href="/wiki/Christianity" title="Christianity">Christianity</a>, <a href="/wiki/Judaism" title="Judaism">Judaism</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Zoroastrianism" title="Zoroastrianism">Zoroastrianism</a> became established on India's southern and western coasts.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Combined-8&#95;54-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Combined-8-54"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>42<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In the early centuries of the <a href="/wiki/2nd_millennium" title="2nd millennium">2nd millennium</a> Muslim armies from <a href="/wiki/Central_Asia" title="Central Asia">Central Asia</a> intermittently overran India's northern plains.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Combined-13&#95;55-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Combined-13-55"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>43<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The resulting <a href="/wiki/Delhi_Sultanate" title="Delhi Sultanate">Delhi Sultanate</a> drew northern India into the cosmopolitan <a href="/wiki/Islamic_Golden_Age" title="Islamic Golden Age">networks of medieval Islam</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Combined-10&#95;56-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Combined-10-56"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>44<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In south India, the <a href="/wiki/Vijayanagara_Empire" title="Vijayanagara Empire">Vijayanagara Empire</a> created a long-lasting composite Hindu culture.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-AsherAsher2006-74&#95;57-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-AsherAsher2006-74-57"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>45<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In the <a href="/wiki/Punjab" title="Punjab">Punjab</a>, <a href="/wiki/Sikhism" title="Sikhism">Sikhism</a> emerged, rejecting institutionalised religion.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-AsherAsher2006-267&#95;58-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-AsherAsher2006-267-58"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>46<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The <a href="/wiki/Mughal_Empire" title="Mughal Empire">Mughal Empire</a> ushered in two centuries of economic expansion and relative peace,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-AsherAsher2006-152&#95;59-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-AsherAsher2006-152-59"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>47<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> and left <a href="/wiki/Mughal_architecture" title="Mughal architecture">a rich architectural legacy</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Fisher2018-106&#95;60-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Fisher2018-106-60"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>48<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEAsher1992&#91;httpsarchiveorgdetailsarchitectureofmu0000ashepage250mode2up&#95;250&#93;&#95;61-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAsher1992[httpsarchiveorgdetailsarchitectureofmu0000ashepage250mode2up_250]-61"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>49<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Gradually expanding <a href="/wiki/Company_rule_in_India" title="Company rule in India">rule of the British East India Company</a> turned India into a colonial economy but consolidated its <a href="/wiki/Sovereignty" title="Sovereignty">sovereignty</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Combined-11&#95;62-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Combined-11-62"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>50<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> <a href="/wiki/British_Raj" title="British Raj">British Crown rule</a> began in 1858. The rights promised to Indians were granted slowly,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Combined-12&#95;63-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Combined-12-63"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>51<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEPeers2013&#91;httpsbooksgooglecombooksiddyQuAgAAQBAJpgPA76&#95;76&#93;&#95;64-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPeers2013[httpsbooksgooglecombooksiddyQuAgAAQBAJpgPA76_76]-64"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>52<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> but <a href="/wiki/Industrial_Revolution" title="Industrial Revolution">technological changes</a> were introduced, and modern ideas of education and the public life took root.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-EmbreeHay1988&#95;65-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-EmbreeHay1988-65"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>53<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Indian_independence_movement" title="Indian independence movement">A nationalist movement</a> emerged in India, the first in the non-European <a href="/wiki/British_Empire" title="British Empire">British Empire</a> and an influence on other nationalist movements.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Marshall2001&#95;66-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Marshall2001-66"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>54<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-Chiriyankandath2016&#95;67-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Chiriyankandath2016-67"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>55<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Noted for nonviolent resistance after 1920,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-metcalf-metcalf-gandhi-1920&#95;68-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-metcalf-metcalf-gandhi-1920-68"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>56<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> it became the primary factor in ending British rule.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-stein-arnold-successful-nationalism&#95;69-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-stein-arnold-successful-nationalism-69"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>57<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In 1947, the <a href="/wiki/British_Raj" title="British Raj">British Indian Empire</a> was <a href="/wiki/Partition_of_India" title="Partition of India">partitioned</a> into two independent <a href="/wiki/Dominion#India_and_Pakistan" title="Dominion">dominions</a>, a Hindu-majority <a href="/wiki/Dominion_of_India" title="Dominion of India">dominion of India</a> and a Muslim-majority <a href="/wiki/Dominion_of_Pakistan" title="Dominion of Pakistan">dominion of Pakistan</a>. A large-scale loss of life and an unprecedented migration accompanied the partition.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-70" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-70"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>58<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>India has been a <a href="/wiki/Federal_republic" title="Federal republic">federal republic</a> since 1950, governed through a democratic <a href="/wiki/Parliamentary_system" title="Parliamentary system">parliamentary system</a>. It is a <a href="/wiki/Pluralism_(political_philosophy)" title="Pluralism (political philosophy)">pluralistic</a>, <a href="/wiki/Multilingualism" title="Multilingualism">multilingual</a> and <a href="/wiki/Multiculturalism" title="Multiculturalism">multi-ethnic society</a>. India's population grew from 361 million in 1951 to over 1.4 billion in 2023.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Dyson2018-219&#95;71-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Dyson2018-219-71"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>59<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> During this time, its nominal <a href="/wiki/Per_capita_income" title="Per capita income">per capita income</a> increased from US$64 annually to US$2,601, and its literacy rate from 16.6% to 74%. A comparatively destitute country in 1951,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Fisher2018-8&#95;72-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Fisher2018-8-72"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>60<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> India has become a <a href="/wiki/List_of_countries_by_real_GDP_growth_rate" title="List of countries by real GDP growth rate">fast-growing</a> major economy and <a href="/wiki/Information_technology_in_India" title="Information technology in India">a hub for information technology services</a>, with an expanding middle class.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-MetcalfMetcalf2012-265&#95;73-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-MetcalfMetcalf2012-265-73"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>61<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> India has reduced <a href="/wiki/Poverty_in_India" title="Poverty in India">its poverty rate</a>, though at the cost of increasing economic inequality.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Dyson2018-216-a&#95;74-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Dyson2018-216-a-74"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>62<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> It is a <a href="/wiki/List_of_states_with_nuclear_weapons" title="List of states with nuclear weapons">nuclear-weapon state</a> that <a href="/wiki/List_of_countries_by_military_expenditures" class="mw-redirect" title="List of countries by military expenditures">ranks high in military expenditure</a>. It has disputes over <a href="/wiki/Kashmir" title="Kashmir">Kashmir</a> with its neighbours, Pakistan and China, unresolved since the mid-20th century.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-kashmir-disputes&#95;75-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-kashmir-disputes-75"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>63<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Among the socio-economic challenges India faces are <a href="/wiki/Gender_inequality_in_India" title="Gender inequality in India">gender inequality</a>, <a href="/wiki/Malnutrition_in_India" title="Malnutrition in India">child malnutrition</a>,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-NarayanJohn2018-lead&#95;76-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-NarayanJohn2018-lead-76"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>64<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> and rising levels of <a href="/wiki/Air_pollution_in_India" title="Air pollution in India">air pollution</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-BalakrishnanDey2019-lead&#95;77-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-BalakrishnanDey2019-lead-77"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>65<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> India's land is <a href="/wiki/Megadiverse_country" class="mw-redirect" title="Megadiverse country">megadiverse</a> with four <a href="/wiki/Biodiversity_hotspots" class="mw-redirect" title="Biodiversity hotspots">biodiversity hotspots</a>. <a href="/wiki/Wildlife_of_India" title="Wildlife of India">India's wildlife</a>, which has traditionally been viewed with tolerance in <a href="/wiki/Culture_of_India" title="Culture of India">its culture</a>,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEKaranthGopal2005374&#95;78-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKaranthGopal2005374-78"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>66<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> is supported in <a href="/wiki/Protected_areas_of_India" title="Protected areas of India">protected habitats</a>.
</p>
<meta property="mw:PageProp/toc" />
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Etymology">Etymology</h2></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Names_for_India" class="mw-redirect" title="Names for India">Names for India</a></div>
<p>According to the <i><a href="/wiki/Oxford_English_Dictionary" title="Oxford English Dictionary">Oxford English Dictionary</a></i>, the English proper noun "India" derives most immediately from the <a href="/wiki/Classical_Latin" title="Classical Latin">Classical Latin</a> <i>India</i>, a reference to a loosely-defined historical region of Asia stretching from <a href="/wiki/South_Asia" title="South Asia">South Asia</a> to the borders of China. Further <a href="/wiki/Etymon" class="mw-redirect" title="Etymon">etymons</a> are: <a href="/wiki/Hellenistic_Greek" class="mw-redirect" title="Hellenistic Greek">Hellenistic Greek</a> <span title="Ancient Greek (to 1453)-language romanization"><i lang="grc-Latn">India</i></span> (<span title="Ancient Greek (to 1453)-language text"><span lang="grc">Ἰνδία</span></span>); <a href="/wiki/Ancient_Greek" title="Ancient Greek">Ancient Greek</a> <span title="Ancient Greek (to 1453)-language romanization"><i lang="grc-Latn">Indos</i></span> (<span title="Ancient Greek (to 1453)-language text"><span lang="grc">Ἰνδός</span></span>), or the River <a href="/wiki/Indus" class="mw-redirect" title="Indus">Indus</a>; Achaemenian <a href="/wiki/Old_Persian" title="Old Persian">Old Persian</a> <span title="Old Persian (ca. 600-400 B.C.)-language romanization"><i lang="peo-Latn"><a href="/wiki/Hindu" class="mw-redirect" title="Hindu">Hindu</a></i></span> (an eastern province of the <a href="/wiki/Achaemenid_Empire" title="Achaemenid Empire">Achaemenid Empire</a>); and <a href="/wiki/Sanskrit" title="Sanskrit">Sanskrit</a> <span title="Sanskrit-language romanization"><i lang="sa-Latn">Sindhu</i></span>, or "river," but specifically the Indus river, and by extension its well-settled basin.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-79" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-79"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>67<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The <a href="/wiki/Ancient_Greeks" class="mw-redirect" title="Ancient Greeks">Ancient Greeks</a> referred to South Asians as <span title="Ancient Greek (to 1453)-language romanization"><i lang="grc-Latn">Indoi</i></span>, 'the people of the Indus'.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEKuiper201086&#95;80-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKuiper201086-80"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>68<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The term <i><a href="/wiki/Names_for_India#Bharat" class="mw-redirect" title="Names for India">Bharat</a></i> (<span title="ISO 15919 Indic (Hindi language) transliteration"><i lang="hi-Latn">Bhārat</i></span>; <style data-mw-deduplicate="TemplateStyles:r1177148991">.mw-parser-output .IPA-label-small{font-size:85%}.mw-parser-output .references .IPA-label-small,.mw-parser-output .infobox .IPA-label-small,.mw-parser-output .navbox .IPA-label-small{font-size:100%}</style><span class="IPA-label IPA-label-small">pronounced</span> <span class="IPA nowrap" lang="hns-Latn-fonipa"><a href="/wiki/Help:IPA/Hindi_and_Urdu" title="Help:IPA/Hindi and Urdu">&#91;ˈbʱaːɾət&#93;</a></span> <span class="ext-phonos"><span data-nosnippet="" id="ooui-php-1" class="noexcerpt ext-phonos-PhonosButton ext-phonos-PhonosButton-emptylabel oo-ui-widget oo-ui-widget-enabled oo-ui-buttonElement oo-ui-buttonElement-frameless oo-ui-iconElement oo-ui-buttonWidget" data-ooui="{&quot;_&quot;:&quot;mw.Phonos.PhonosButton&quot;,&quot;href&quot;:&quot;\/\/upload.wikimedia.org\/wikipedia\/commons\/transcoded\/7\/79\/Hi-Bharat.ogg\/Hi-Bharat.ogg.mp3&quot;,&quot;rel&quot;:[&quot;nofollow&quot;],&quot;framed&quot;:false,&quot;icon&quot;:&quot;volumeUp&quot;,&quot;data&quot;:{&quot;ipa&quot;:&quot;&quot;,&quot;text&quot;:&quot;&quot;,&quot;lang&quot;:&quot;en&quot;,&quot;wikibase&quot;:&quot;&quot;,&quot;file&quot;:&quot;Hi-Bharat.ogg&quot;},&quot;classes&quot;:[&quot;noexcerpt&quot;,&quot;ext-phonos-PhonosButton&quot;,&quot;ext-phonos-PhonosButton-emptylabel&quot;]}"><a role="button" tabindex="0" href="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/7/79/Hi-Bharat.ogg/Hi-Bharat.ogg.mp3" rel="nofollow" aria-label="Play audio" title="Play audio" class="oo-ui-buttonElement-button"><span class="oo-ui-iconElement-icon oo-ui-icon-volumeUp"></span><span class="oo-ui-labelElement-label"></span><span class="oo-ui-indicatorElement-indicator oo-ui-indicatorElement-noIndicator"></span></a></span><sup class="ext-phonos-attribution noexcerpt navigation-not-searchable"><a href="/wiki/File:Hi-Bharat.ogg" title="File:Hi-Bharat.ogg">ⓘ</a></sup></span>), mentioned in both <a href="/wiki/Indian_epic_poetry" title="Indian epic poetry">Indian epic poetry</a> and the <a href="/wiki/Constitution_of_India" title="Constitution of India">Constitution of India</a>,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEClémentin-Ojha2014&#95;81-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClémentin-Ojha2014-81"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>69<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-82" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-82"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>70<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> is used in its variations by <a href="/wiki/Names_for_India" class="mw-redirect" title="Names for India">many Indian languages</a>. A modern rendering of the historical name <span title="Sanskrit-language romanization"><i lang="sa-Latn">Bharatavarsha</i></span>, which applied originally to <a href="/wiki/North_India" title="North India">North India</a>,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Jha2014&#95;83-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Jha2014-83"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>71<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTESingh2017&#91;httpsbooksgooglecombooksiddYM4DwAAQBAJpgPA253&#95;253&#93;&#95;84-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTESingh2017[httpsbooksgooglecombooksiddYM4DwAAQBAJpgPA253_253]-84"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>72<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> <i>Bharat</i> gained increased currency from the mid-19th century as a native name for India.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEClémentin-Ojha2014&#95;81-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClémentin-Ojha2014-81"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>69<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEBarrow2003&#95;85-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarrow2003-85"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>73<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p><i><a href="/wiki/Hindustan" title="Hindustan">Hindustan</a></i> (<span class="IPA nowrap" lang="hns-Latn-fonipa"><a href="/wiki/Help:IPA/Hindi_and_Urdu" title="Help:IPA/Hindi and Urdu">&#91;ɦɪndʊˈstaːn&#93;</a></span> <span class="ext-phonos"><span data-nosnippet="" id="ooui-php-2" class="noexcerpt ext-phonos-PhonosButton ext-phonos-PhonosButton-emptylabel oo-ui-widget oo-ui-widget-enabled oo-ui-buttonElement oo-ui-buttonElement-frameless oo-ui-iconElement oo-ui-buttonWidget" data-ooui="{&quot;_&quot;:&quot;mw.Phonos.PhonosButton&quot;,&quot;href&quot;:&quot;\/\/upload.wikimedia.org\/wikipedia\/commons\/transcoded\/4\/46\/Hindustan.ogg\/Hindustan.ogg.mp3&quot;,&quot;rel&quot;:[&quot;nofollow&quot;],&quot;framed&quot;:false,&quot;icon&quot;:&quot;volumeUp&quot;,&quot;data&quot;:{&quot;ipa&quot;:&quot;&quot;,&quot;text&quot;:&quot;&quot;,&quot;lang&quot;:&quot;en&quot;,&quot;wikibase&quot;:&quot;&quot;,&quot;file&quot;:&quot;Hindustan.ogg&quot;},&quot;classes&quot;:[&quot;noexcerpt&quot;,&quot;ext-phonos-PhonosButton&quot;,&quot;ext-phonos-PhonosButton-emptylabel&quot;]}"><a role="button" tabindex="0" href="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/4/46/Hindustan.ogg/Hindustan.ogg.mp3" rel="nofollow" aria-label="Play audio" title="Play audio" class="oo-ui-buttonElement-button"><span class="oo-ui-iconElement-icon oo-ui-icon-volumeUp"></span><span class="oo-ui-labelElement-label"></span><span class="oo-ui-indicatorElement-indicator oo-ui-indicatorElement-noIndicator"></span></a></span><sup class="ext-phonos-attribution noexcerpt navigation-not-searchable"><a href="/wiki/File:Hindustan.ogg" title="File:Hindustan.ogg">ⓘ</a></sup></span>) is a <a href="/wiki/Middle_Persian" title="Middle Persian">Middle Persian</a> name for India that became popular by the 13th century,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-86" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-86"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>74<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> and was used widely since the era of the <a href="/wiki/Mughal_Empire" title="Mughal Empire">Mughal Empire</a>. The meaning of <i>Hindustan</i> has varied, referring to a region encompassing the northern <a href="/wiki/Indian_subcontinent" title="Indian subcontinent">Indian subcontinent</a> (present-day northern India and <a href="/wiki/Pakistan" title="Pakistan">Pakistan</a>) or to India in its near entirety.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEClémentin-Ojha2014&#95;81-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClémentin-Ojha2014-81"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>69<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEBarrow2003&#95;85-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarrow2003-85"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>73<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-87" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-87"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>75<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="History">History</h2></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/History_of_India" title="History of India">History of India</a></div>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Ancient_India">Ancient India</h3></div>
<p>Based on <a href="/wiki/Coalescent_theory" title="Coalescent theory">coalescence</a> of <a href="/wiki/Mitochondrial_DNA" title="Mitochondrial DNA">Mitochondrial DNA</a> and <a href="/wiki/Y_Chromosome" class="mw-redirect" title="Y Chromosome">Y Chromosome</a> data, it is thought that the earliest extant <a href="/wiki/Lineage_(evolution)" title="Lineage (evolution)">lineages</a> of anatomically modern humans or <i><a href="/wiki/Homo_sapiens" class="mw-redirect" title="Homo sapiens">Homo sapiens</a></i> on the Indian subcontinent had reached there from Africa between 80,000 and 50,000 years ago, and with high likelihood by 55,000 years ago.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-PetragliaAllchin&#95;37-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-PetragliaAllchin-37"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>26<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-Dyson2018p1&#95;38-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Dyson2018p1-38"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>27<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-Fisher2018p23&#95;39-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Fisher2018p23-39"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>28<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-mattern-slow-moon-non-african-migration&#95;88-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-mattern-slow-moon-non-african-migration-88"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>76<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Their long occupation, initially in varying forms of isolation as hunter-gatherers, has made the region highly diverse, second only to Africa in human <a href="/wiki/Genetic_diversity" title="Genetic diversity">genetic diversity</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Dyson2018-28a&#95;40-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Dyson2018-28a-40"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>29<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> However, the earliest known modern human fossils in South Asia date to about 30,000 years ago.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Dyson2018p1&#95;38-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Dyson2018p1-38"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>27<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Evidence for the <a href="/wiki/Neolithic_period" class="mw-redirect" title="Neolithic period">neolithic period</a> in the western margins of the <a href="/wiki/Indus_River" title="Indus River">Indus</a> <a href="/wiki/River_basin" class="mw-redirect" title="River basin">river basin</a>, at <a href="/wiki/Mehrgarh" title="Mehrgarh">Mehrgarh</a> in <a href="/wiki/Balochistan,_Pakistan" title="Balochistan, Pakistan">Balochistan, Pakistan</a>, dates to after 7000&#160;<span title="Before Common Era">BCE</span>. Domestication of grain-producing plants (including <a href="/wiki/Barley" title="Barley">barley</a>) and animals (including <a href="/wiki/Zebu" title="Zebu">humped zebu</a> cattle) occurred here. These cultures gradually evolved into the <a href="/wiki/Indus_Valley_Civilisation" title="Indus Valley Civilisation">Indus Valley Civilisation</a>, which flourished during 2500–1900&#160;<span title="Before Common Era">BCE</span> in Pakistan and western India.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEWright201045–51&#95;89-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWright201045–51-89"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>77<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-Combined-2&#95;41-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Combined-2-41"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>30<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Centred around cities such as <a href="/wiki/Mohenjo-daro" title="Mohenjo-daro">Mohenjo-daro</a>, <a href="/wiki/Harappa" title="Harappa">Harappa</a>, <a href="/wiki/Dholavira" title="Dholavira">Dholavira</a>, <a href="/wiki/Ganweriwala" class="mw-redirect" title="Ganweriwala">Ganweriwala</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Rakhigarhi" title="Rakhigarhi">Rakhigarhi</a>,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEWright2010107&#95;90-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWright2010107-90"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>78<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> its characteristic features included standardised weights, <a href="/wiki/Soapstone" title="Soapstone">steatite</a> <a href="/wiki/Indus_Valley_Civilisation#Seals" title="Indus Valley Civilisation">seals</a>, a written script, urban planning, public works, and arts and crafts including pottery styles, terracotta human figures and animal statuettes.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEWright2010107&#95;90-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWright2010107-90"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>78<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Networks of towns and villages grew around the cities in a new <a href="/wiki/Agriculture" title="Agriculture">agro</a>-<a href="/wiki/Animal_husbandry" title="Animal husbandry">pastoral</a> economy.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEWright2010145–146&#95;91-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWright2010145–146-91"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>79<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Between 1500&#160;<span title="Before Common Era">BCE</span> and 1200&#160;<span title="Before Common Era">BCE</span>, an <a href="/wiki/Proto-language" title="Proto-language">archaic form</a> of <a href="/wiki/Sanskrit" title="Sanskrit">Sanskrit</a>, an <a href="/wiki/Indo-European_language" class="mw-redirect" title="Indo-European language">Indo-European language</a>, <a href="/wiki/Trans-cultural_diffusion" class="mw-redirect" title="Trans-cultural diffusion">diffused</a> into India from the northwest. Its evidence today is found in the <a href="/wiki/Rig_Veda" class="mw-redirect" title="Rig Veda">Rig Veda</a>—the oldest scripture associated with what later became <a href="/wiki/Hinduism" title="Hinduism">Hinduism</a>—which was composed by <a href="/wiki/Indo-Aryan_languages" title="Indo-Aryan languages">Indo-Aryan</a>-speaking tribes migrating east from what is today northern <a href="/wiki/Afghanistan" title="Afghanistan">Afghanistan</a> and across the <a href="/wiki/Punjab_region" class="mw-redirect" title="Punjab region">Punjab region</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-lowe1&#95;42-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-lowe1-42"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>31<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-Combined-4-Rigveda&#95;43-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Combined-4-Rigveda-43"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>32<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The settling of the <a href="/wiki/Ganges_river" class="mw-redirect" title="Ganges river">Ganges</a> <a href="/wiki/River_plain" class="mw-redirect" title="River plain">river plain</a> took place during the next millennium, when large swathes of the river system's adjoining regions were deforested, at times by setting fires, or later by employing iron implements, and prepared for agriculture. The settlement may have involved driving the preexisting people out or enslaving them.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEDyson201816,&#95;25&#95;92-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDyson201816,_25-92"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>80<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The <a href="/wiki/Dravidian_languages" title="Dravidian languages">Dravidian languages</a> of India were supplanted in the north, creating a broad <a href="/wiki/Language_family" title="Language family">language familiy</a>-divide, with the Indo-Aryan languages being spoken mainly in the <a href="/wiki/North_India" title="North India">north</a> and <a href="/wiki/West_India" class="mw-redirect" title="West India">west</a>, and the Dravidian in some parts of <a href="/wiki/East_India" title="East India">east India</a> and most of the <a href="/wiki/South_India" title="South India">south</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Combined-4&#95;45-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Combined-4-45"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>34<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Classical_Sanskrit" class="mw-redirect" title="Classical Sanskrit">Classical Sanskrit</a>, a refined and standardised grammatical form would emerge in the mid-1st millennium BCE and was codified in the <i><a href="/wiki/A%E1%B9%A3%E1%B9%AD%C4%81dhy%C4%81y%C4%AB" title="Aṣṭādhyāyī">Aṣṭādhyāyī</a></i> ('Eight chapters') of <a href="/wiki/P%C4%81%E1%B9%87ini" title="Pāṇini">Pāṇini</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-94" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-94"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>m<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The two major Sanskrit epics, the <a href="/wiki/Mah%C4%81bh%C4%81rata" class="mw-redirect" title="Mahābhārata">Mahābhārata</a> and the <a href="/wiki/R%C4%81m%C4%81ya%E1%B9%87a" class="mw-redirect" title="Rāmāyaṇa">Rāmāyaṇa</a>, however, were composed in a range of oral storytelling registers called <a href="/wiki/Epic_Sanskrit" class="mw-redirect" title="Epic Sanskrit">Epic Sanskrit</a> which was used in northern India between 400 BCE and 300 CE, and roughly contemporary with classical Sanskrit.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Lowe2017-epic&#95;95-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Lowe2017-epic-95"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>82<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>  
</p><p>A second <a href="/wiki/Urbanisation" class="mw-redirect" title="Urbanisation">urbanisation</a> had taken place in <a href="/wiki/South_Asia" title="South Asia">South Asia</a> by 400&#160;<span title="Before Common Era">BCE</span>, this time on the Ganges plain. In fortified cities, <a href="/wiki/Social_differentiation" class="mw-redirect" title="Social differentiation">social differentiation</a> by <a href="/wiki/Caste" title="Caste">caste</a>, or <a href="/wiki/Varna_(Hinduism)" title="Varna (Hinduism)">varna</a>, had emerged.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Dyson2018-16a&#95;46-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Dyson2018-16a-46"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>35<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> By the mid-millennium two new ethical and social systems had arisen: <a href="/wiki/History_of_Jainism" title="History of Jainism">Jainism</a> based on the teachings of <a href="/wiki/Mahavira" title="Mahavira">Mahavira</a> and <a href="/wiki/History_of_Buddhism_in_India" title="History of Buddhism in India">Buddhism</a> on those of <a href="/wiki/The_Buddha" title="The Buddha">the Buddha</a>. Both religions stressed non-violence and abjured animal sacrifices conducted in <a href="/wiki/Historical_Vedic_religion#Brahmanism" title="Historical Vedic religion">Brahmanism</a>,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-96" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-96"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>n<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> and birth into a fixed hereditary <i>varna</i>. By living ethically, lay people could rise socially and morally in these religions.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Fisher2018-59&#95;47-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Fisher2018-59-47"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>36<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Chronicling the life of the Buddha was central to the beginnings of recorded history in India.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEStein201021,&#95;61–62&#95;97-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStein201021,_61–62-97"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>83<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The rise of the two religions was a backdrop to the emergence of the first loose-knit geographically extensive power in South Asia, the <a href="/wiki/Maurya_Empire" title="Maurya Empire">Maurya Empire</a>. During the rule of the founder's grandson, <a href="/wiki/Ashoka" title="Ashoka">Ashoka</a> (ca. 268–232 BCE), the empire briefly controlled the major urban hubs and arteries of the subcontinent, except in the deep south.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEDyson201816–17&#95;98-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDyson201816–17-98"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>84<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-100" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-100"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>o<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-102" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-102"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>p<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The empire's period was notable for creativity in art, architecture, inscriptions, and produced texts,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTELudden201428–29&#95;103-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTELudden201428–29-103"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>87<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> but also for the declining rights of women in the mainstream <a href="/wiki/Indo-Aryan_languages" title="Indo-Aryan languages">Indo-Aryan speaking</a> regions.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEDyson201819&#95;104-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDyson201819-104"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>88<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> After the <a href="/wiki/Kalinga_War" title="Kalinga War">Kalinga War</a> in which his troops visited great violence on the region, Ashoka embraced <a href="/wiki/Buddhism" title="Buddhism">Buddhism</a> and promoted its tenets in edicts scattered across South Asia.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEStein201073&#95;105-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStein201073-105"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>89<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> As the edicts forbade both the killing of wild animals and the destruction of forests, Ashoka is seen by some modern environmental historians as an early embodiment of that ethos.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-elverskog-2020-lead-1&#95;106-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-elverskog-2020-lead-1-106"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>90<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEFisher201872&#95;107-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFisher201872-107"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>91<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>By the 4th and 5th centuries, the <a href="/wiki/Gupta_Empire" title="Gupta Empire">Gupta Empire</a> had created a complex system of administration and taxation in the greater Ganges Plain;<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEKulkeRothermund2016118,&#95;126&#95;108-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKulkeRothermund2016118,_126-108"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>92<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> this system became a model for later Indian kingdoms.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTERobb201168–69&#95;109-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTERobb201168–69-109"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>93<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Under the Guptas, a renewed Hinduism based on devotion, rather than the management of ritual, began to assert itself.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEStein201093–94&#95;110-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStein201093–94-110"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>94<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The renewal was reflected in a flowering of art, literature, and science.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEGilbert201754–55&#95;111-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGilbert201754–55-111"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>95<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEKulkeRothermund2016122–123&#95;51-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKulkeRothermund2016122–123-51"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>40<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In South India, the <a href="/wiki/Sangam_literature" title="Sangam literature">Sangam literature</a> of the <a href="/wiki/Tamil_language" title="Tamil language">Tamil language</a> reveals that, between 200&#160;<span title="Before Common Era">BCE</span> and 200&#160;<span title="Common Era">CE</span>, the southern peninsula was ruled by the <a href="/wiki/Chera_dynasty" title="Chera dynasty">Cheras</a> and the <a href="/wiki/Chola_dynasty" title="Chola dynasty">Cholas</a>, along the western and eastern plains, respectively, of the <a href="/wiki/Kaveri_river" class="mw-redirect" title="Kaveri river">Kaveri river</a> valley, and the <a href="/wiki/Pandya" class="mw-redirect" title="Pandya">Pandyas</a> farther south along the <a href="/wiki/Vaigai_river" class="mw-redirect" title="Vaigai river">Vaigai river</a> valley.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEStein201098&#95;112-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStein201098-112"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>96<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> By the sixth century, the <a href="/wiki/Pallava" class="mw-redirect" title="Pallava">Pallavas</a> had grown into a regional power. Simultaneously, Buddhism and Jainism, which had favoured a conservative <a href="/wiki/Transactionalism" title="Transactionalism">transactionalism</a>, were replaced by kingly devotion to the gods of particular places, which became a characteristic of the <a href="/wiki/Bhakti_movement" title="Bhakti movement">Bhakti movement</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEStein2010100–101&#95;113-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStein2010100–101-113"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>97<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The Pallavas, in particular, <a href="/wiki/Indo-Roman_trade_relations" title="Indo-Roman trade relations">traded extensively with the Roman Empire</a> and with <a href="/wiki/Western_Asia" class="mw-redirect" title="Western Asia">West</a> and <a href="/wiki/Southeast_Asia" title="Southeast Asia">Southeast Asia</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEStein201099&#95;114-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStein201099-114"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>98<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<ul class="gallery mw-gallery-packed">
		<li class="gallerybox" style="width: 342.66666666667px">
			<div class="thumb" style="width: 340.66666666667px;"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:Battle_at_Lanka,_Ramayana,_Udaipur,_1649-53.jpg" class="mw-file-description" title="Manuscript illustration, c. 1650, of the Sanskrit epic Ramayana, composed in story-telling fashion c. 400&#160;BCE&#160;– c. 300&#160;CE[99]"><img alt="Manuscript illustration, c. 1650, of the Sanskrit epic Ramayana, composed in story-telling fashion c. 400&#160;BCE&#160;– c. 300&#160;CE[99]" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/Battle_at_Lanka%2C_Ramayana%2C_Udaipur%2C_1649-53.jpg/960px-Battle_at_Lanka%2C_Ramayana%2C_Udaipur%2C_1649-53.jpg" decoding="async" width="341" height="200" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/Battle_at_Lanka%2C_Ramayana%2C_Udaipur%2C_1649-53.jpg/1280px-Battle_at_Lanka%2C_Ramayana%2C_Udaipur%2C_1649-53.jpg 2x" data-file-width="5100" data-file-height="2995" /></a></span></div>

<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Medieval_India">Medieval India</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Medieval_India" title="Medieval India">Medieval India</a></div>
<p>The Indian early medieval age, from 600 to 1200&#160;<span title="Common Era">CE</span>, was defined by regional kingdoms and cultural diversity.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEStein2010116&#95;116-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStein2010116-116"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>100<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> No ruler of this period was able to create an empire and consistently control lands much beyond their core region.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-118" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-118"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>q<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEStein2010115–116&#95;117-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStein2010115–116-117"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>101<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> During this time, pastoral peoples, whose land had been cleared to make way for the growing agricultural economy, were accommodated within caste society, as were new non-traditional ruling classes.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEStein2010116–117&#95;119-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStein2010116–117-119"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>102<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The caste system consequently began to show regional differences.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEStein2010116–117&#95;119-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStein2010116–117-119"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>102<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>In the 6th and 7th centuries, the first <a href="/wiki/Bhakti" title="Bhakti">devotional hymns</a> were composed in Tamil.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEStein2010118&#95;120-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStein2010118-120"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>103<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> They were imitated all over India and led to both the resurgence of Hinduism and the development of all <a href="/wiki/Languages_of_India" title="Languages of India">modern languages of the subcontinent</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEStein2010118&#95;120-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStein2010118-120"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>103<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Indian royalty, big and small, and the temples they patronised drew citizens in significant numbers to the capital cities, which became economic hubs as well.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEStein2010118–119&#95;121-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStein2010118–119-121"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>104<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Temple towns of various sizes began to appear everywhere as India underwent another urbanisation.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEStein2010118–119&#95;121-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStein2010118–119-121"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>104<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> By the 8th and 9th centuries, the effects were felt in Southeast Asia, as South Indian culture and political systems were exported to lands that became part of modern-day <a href="/wiki/Myanmar" title="Myanmar">Myanmar</a>, <a href="/wiki/Thailand" title="Thailand">Thailand</a>, <a href="/wiki/Laos" title="Laos">Laos</a>, <a href="/wiki/Brunei" title="Brunei">Brunei</a>, <a href="/wiki/Cambodia" title="Cambodia">Cambodia</a>, <a href="/wiki/Vietnam" title="Vietnam">Vietnam</a>, <a href="/wiki/Philippines" title="Philippines">Philippines</a>, <a href="/wiki/Malaysia" title="Malaysia">Malaysia</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Indonesia" title="Indonesia">Indonesia</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEStein2010121–123&#95;122-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStein2010121–123-122"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>105<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Indian merchants, scholars, and sometimes armies were involved in this transmission; Southeast Asians took the initiative as well, with many sojourning in Indian seminaries and translating Buddhist and Hindu texts into their languages.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEStein2010121–123&#95;122-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStein2010121–123-122"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>105<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>After the 10th century, Muslim Central Asian nomadic clans, using <a href="/wiki/Courser_(horse)" title="Courser (horse)">swift-horse</a> cavalry and raising vast armies united by ethnicity, repeatedly overran South Asia's north-western plains,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-124" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-124"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>r<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> leading eventually to the establishment of the Islamic <a href="/wiki/Delhi_Sultanate" title="Delhi Sultanate">Delhi Sultanate</a> in 1206.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTELudden201485&#95;125-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTELudden201485-125"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>107<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The sultanate was to control much of North India and to make many forays into South India. Although at first disruptive for the Indian elites, the sultanate largely left its vast non-Muslim subject population to its own laws and customs.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEAsherTalbot202254&#95;126-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAsherTalbot202254-126"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>108<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEMetcalfMetcalf20066&#95;127-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMetcalfMetcalf20066-127"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>109<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>By repeatedly repulsing <a href="/wiki/Mongol_Empire" title="Mongol Empire">Mongol raiders</a> in the 13th century, the sultanate saved India from the devastation visited on West and Central Asia,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTELudden201485&#95;125-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTELudden201485-125"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>107<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> setting the scene for centuries of <a href="/wiki/Human_migration" title="Human migration">migration</a> of fleeing soldiers, learned men, mystics, traders, artists, and artisans from that region into the subcontinent, thereby creating a syncretic Indo-Islamic culture in the north.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEAsherTalbot200850–51&#95;128-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAsherTalbot200850–51-128"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>110<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The sultanate's raiding and weakening of the regional kingdoms of South India paved the way for the indigenous <a href="/wiki/Vijayanagara_Empire" title="Vijayanagara Empire">Vijayanagara Empire</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEAsherTalbot200853&#95;129-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAsherTalbot200853-129"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>111<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Embracing a strong <a href="/wiki/Shaivism" title="Shaivism">Shaivite</a> tradition and building upon the military technology of the sultanate, the empire came to control much of peninsular India,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEMetcalfMetcalf200612&#95;130-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMetcalfMetcalf200612-130"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>112<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> and was to influence South Indian society for long afterwards.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEAsherTalbot200853&#95;129-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAsherTalbot200853-129"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>111<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<ul class="gallery mw-gallery-packed">
		<li class="gallerybox" style="width: 322px">
