<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Early_modern_India">Early modern India</h3></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size mw-halign-right" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Shaikh_Salim_Chishti_Ka_Maqbara.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/89/Shaikh_Salim_Chishti_Ka_Maqbara.jpg/250px-Shaikh_Salim_Chishti_Ka_Maqbara.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="167" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/89/Shaikh_Salim_Chishti_Ka_Maqbara.jpg/500px-Shaikh_Salim_Chishti_Ka_Maqbara.jpg 1.5x" data-file-width="5400" data-file-height="3600" /></a><figcaption>The <i><a href="/wiki/Dargah" title="Dargah">dargah</a></i>, or mausoleam of <a href="/wiki/Sufi" class="mw-redirect" title="Sufi">Sufi</a> saint <a href="/wiki/Salim_Chisti" class="mw-redirect" title="Salim Chisti">Salim Chisti</a>, built by <a href="/wiki/Mughal_Empire" title="Mughal Empire">Mughal emperor</a>, <a href="/wiki/Akbar" title="Akbar">Akbar</a>, in the early 17th century</figcaption></figure>
<figure class="mw-default-size mw-halign-right" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:India_1835_2_Mohurs.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/India_1835_2_Mohurs.jpg/250px-India_1835_2_Mohurs.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="127" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/India_1835_2_Mohurs.jpg/500px-India_1835_2_Mohurs.jpg 1.5x" data-file-width="5752" data-file-height="2923" /></a><figcaption>A two-<a href="/wiki/Mohur" title="Mohur">mohur</a> <a href="/wiki/Company_rule_in_India" title="Company rule in India">East India Company rule</a> gold coin, issued in 1835, the <a href="/wiki/Obverse_and_reverse" title="Obverse and reverse">obverse</a> inscribed "<a href="/wiki/William_IV" title="William IV">William IIII</a>, King"</figcaption></figure>
<p>In the early 16th century, northern India, then under mainly Muslim rulers,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTERobb201197&amp;ndash;98&#95;131-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTERobb201197&amp;ndash;98-131"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>113<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> fell again to the superior mobility and firepower of a new generation of Central Asian warriors.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEStein2010159&#95;132-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStein2010159-132"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>114<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The resulting <a href="/wiki/Mughal_Empire" title="Mughal Empire">Mughal Empire</a> did not stamp out the local societies it came to rule. Instead, it balanced and pacified them through new administrative practices<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEAsherTalbot2022136&#95;133-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAsherTalbot2022136-133"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>115<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTERobb2011108–109&#95;134-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTERobb2011108–109-134"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>116<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> and diverse and inclusive ruling elites,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEMetcalfMetcalf201215&#95;135-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMetcalfMetcalf201215-135"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>117<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> leading to more systematic, centralised, and uniform rule.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEAsherTalbot2022136,&#95;151–152&#95;136-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAsherTalbot2022136,_151–152-136"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>118<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Eschewing tribal bonds and Islamic identity, especially under <a href="/wiki/Akbar" title="Akbar">Akbar</a>, the Mughals united their far-flung realms through loyalty—expressed through a Persianised culture—to an emperor who had near-divine status.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEMetcalfMetcalf201217&#95;137-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMetcalfMetcalf201217-137"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>119<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The Mughal state's economic policies, deriving most revenues from agriculture<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEAsherTalbot2008158&#95;138-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAsherTalbot2008158-138"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>120<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> and mandating that taxes be paid in the well-regulated silver currency,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEStein2010164&#95;139-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStein2010164-139"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>121<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> caused peasants and artisans to enter larger markets.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEAsherTalbot2022187&#95;140-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAsherTalbot2022187-140"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>122<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The relative peace maintained by the empire during much of the 17th century was a factor in India's economic expansion,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEAsherTalbot2022187&#95;140-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAsherTalbot2022187-140"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>122<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> resulting in greater patronage of <a href="/wiki/Mughal_painting" title="Mughal painting">painting</a>, literary forms, textiles, and <a href="/wiki/Mughal_architecture" title="Mughal architecture">architecture</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEAsherTalbot2008186&#95;141-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAsherTalbot2008186-141"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>123<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Newly coherent social groups in northern and western India, such as the <a href="/wiki/Maratha_Empire" title="Maratha Empire">Marathas</a>, the <a href="/wiki/Rajput" title="Rajput">Rajputs</a>, and the <a href="/wiki/Sikh_Empire" title="Sikh Empire">Sikhs</a>, gained military and governing ambitions during Mughal rule, which, through collaboration or adversity, gave them both recognition and military experience.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEMetcalfMetcalf200623–24&#95;142-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMetcalfMetcalf200623–24-142"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>124<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Expanding commerce during Mughal rule gave rise to new Indian commercial and political elites along the coasts of southern and eastern India.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEMetcalfMetcalf200623–24&#95;142-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMetcalfMetcalf200623–24-142"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>124<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The empire gradually disintegrated in the late 17th and 18th centuries, enabling many among these elites were able to seek control of their own affairs.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEAsherTalbot2008256&#95;143-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAsherTalbot2008256-143"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>125<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>By the early 18th century, with the lines between commercial and political dominance being increasingly blurred, several European trading companies, including the <a href="/wiki/East_India_Company" title="East India Company">English East India Company</a>, had established coastal outposts.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEAsherTalbot2008286&#95;144-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAsherTalbot2008286-144"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>126<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEMetcalfMetcalf200644–49&#95;145-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMetcalfMetcalf200644–49-145"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>127<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The East India Company's control of the seas, greater resources, and more advanced military training and technology led it to assert its military strength increasingly and caused it to become attractive to a portion of the Indian elite; these factors were crucial in allowing the company to gain control over the <a href="/wiki/Bengal" title="Bengal">Bengal</a> region by 1765 and sideline the other European companies.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTERobb2011115–117&#95;146-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTERobb2011115–117-146"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>128<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEAsherTalbot2008286&#95;144-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAsherTalbot2008286-144"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>126<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTELudden2002128–132&#95;147-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTELudden2002128–132-147"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>129<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEMetcalfMetcalf200651–55&#95;148-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMetcalfMetcalf200651–55-148"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>130<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Its further access to the riches of Bengal and the subsequent increased strength and size of its army enabled it to annexe or subdue most of India by the 1820s.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEMetcalfMetcalf200668–71&#95;149-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMetcalfMetcalf200668–71-149"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>131<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> India was no longer exporting manufactured goods as it long had, but instead supplying the <a href="/wiki/British_Empire" title="British Empire">British Empire</a> with raw materials. Many historians consider this to be the onset of India's colonial period.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEAsherTalbot2008286&#95;144-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAsherTalbot2008286-144"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>126<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> By this time, with its economic power severely curtailed by the British parliament and having effectively been made an arm of British administration, the East India Company began more consciously to enter non-economic arenas, including education, social reform, and culture.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEAsherTalbot2008289&#95;150-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAsherTalbot2008289-150"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>132<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Modern_India">Modern India</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/History_of_India_(1947%E2%80%93present)" title="History of India (1947–present)">History of India (1947–present)</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size mw-halign-right" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Mahatma_Gandhi_leaves_Presidency_Jail_in_Calcutta.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f4/Mahatma_Gandhi_leaves_Presidency_Jail_in_Calcutta.jpg/250px-Mahatma_Gandhi_leaves_Presidency_Jail_in_Calcutta.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="182" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f4/Mahatma_Gandhi_leaves_Presidency_Jail_in_Calcutta.jpg/500px-Mahatma_Gandhi_leaves_Presidency_Jail_in_Calcutta.jpg 1.5x" data-file-width="4002" data-file-height="2913" /></a><figcaption>Mahatma Gandhi leaves the Presidency Jail in <a href="/wiki/Calcutta" class="mw-redirect" title="Calcutta">Calcutta</a> in April 1938, after interviewing political prisoners there.</figcaption></figure>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Palace_of_Assembly_Chandigarh_2006.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1a/Palace_of_Assembly_Chandigarh_2006.jpg/250px-Palace_of_Assembly_Chandigarh_2006.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="105" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1a/Palace_of_Assembly_Chandigarh_2006.jpg/500px-Palace_of_Assembly_Chandigarh_2006.jpg 1.5x" data-file-width="4760" data-file-height="1990" /></a><figcaption>The <a href="/wiki/Chandigarh_Capitol_Complex" title="Chandigarh Capitol Complex">Capitol Complex</a> in <a href="/wiki/Chandigarh" title="Chandigarh">Chandigarh</a>. Commissioned by <a href="/wiki/Jawaharlal_Nehru" title="Jawaharlal Nehru">Jawaharlal Nehru</a>, the city was built in the aftermath of India's <a href="/wiki/Partition_of_India" title="Partition of India">1947 partition</a> and independence.</figcaption></figure>
<p>The appointment in 1848 of <a href="/wiki/James_Broun-Ramsay,_1st_Marquess_of_Dalhousie" title="James Broun-Ramsay, 1st Marquess of Dalhousie">Lord Dalhousie</a> as Governor General of the East India Company set the stage for changes essential to a modern state: the consolidation and demarcation of sovereignty, the surveillance of the population, and the education of citizens. Technological changes—among them, railways, canals, and the telegraph—were introduced not long after their introduction in <a href="/wiki/Europe" title="Europe">Europe</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTERobb2011169–171&#95;151-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTERobb2011169–171-151"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>133<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEMetcalfMetcalf200694–99&#95;152-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMetcalfMetcalf200694–99-152"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>134<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEBrown199483&#95;153-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrown199483-153"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>135<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEPeers200650&#95;154-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPeers200650-154"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>136<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Disaffection with the company also grew during this time and set off the <a href="/wiki/Indian_Rebellion_of_1857" title="Indian Rebellion of 1857">Indian Rebellion of 1857</a>. Fed by diverse resentments and perceptions, including invasive British-style social reforms, harsh land taxes, and summary treatment of some wealthy landowners and princes, the rebellion rocked many regions of northern and central India and shook the foundations of Company rule.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEMetcalfMetcalf2006100–103&#95;155-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMetcalfMetcalf2006100–103-155"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>137<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEBrown199485–86&#95;156-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrown199485–86-156"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>138<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> After the rebellion was suppressed in 1858, the East India Company was disbanded, and the British government began to <a href="/wiki/British_Raj" title="British Raj">directly administer India</a>. Proclaiming a <a href="/wiki/Unitary_state" title="Unitary state">unitary state</a> and a gradual but limited British-style parliamentary system,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEStein2010227&#95;157-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStein2010227-157"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>139<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> the new rulers also protected princes and landed gentry as a feudal safeguard against future unrest.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEMetcalfMetcalf2006103–108&#95;158-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMetcalfMetcalf2006103–108-158"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>140<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In the decades following, public life gradually emerged all over India, leading eventually to the founding of the <a href="/wiki/Indian_National_Congress" title="Indian National Congress">Indian National Congress</a> in 1885.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTERobb2001183&#95;159-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTERobb2001183-159"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>141<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTESarkar19831–4&#95;160-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTESarkar19831–4-160"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>142<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTECopland2001ix–x&#95;161-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTECopland2001ix–x-161"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>143<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEMetcalfMetcalf2006123&#95;162-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMetcalfMetcalf2006123-162"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>144<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The rush of technology and the commercialisation of agriculture in the second half of the 19th century was marked by economic setbacks, and many small farmers became dependent on the whims of faraway markets.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEStein2010249&#95;163-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStein2010249-163"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>145<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> There was an increase in the number of large-scale <a href="/wiki/Famine_in_India" title="Famine in India">famines</a>,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEStein2010245&#95;164-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStein2010245-164"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>146<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> and, despite the risks of infrastructure development borne by Indian taxpayers, little industrial employment was generated for Indians.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEStein2010247–248&#95;165-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStein2010247–248-165"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>147<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> However, commercial cropping, especially in the newly canalled Punjab, led to increased food production for internal consumption.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEMetcalfMetcalf2006126&#95;166-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMetcalfMetcalf2006126-166"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>148<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The railway network provided critical famine relief,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEMetcalfMetcalf200697&#95;167-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMetcalfMetcalf200697-167"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>149<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> notably reduced the cost of moving goods,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEMetcalfMetcalf200697&#95;167-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMetcalfMetcalf200697-167"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>149<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> and helped nascent Indian-owned industry.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEMetcalfMetcalf2006126&#95;166-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMetcalfMetcalf2006126-166"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>148<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>After <a href="/wiki/World_War_I" title="World War I">World War I</a>, in which approximately <a href="/wiki/Indian_Army_during_World_War_I" title="Indian Army during World War I">one million Indians served</a>,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEMetcalfMetcalf2006163&#95;168-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMetcalfMetcalf2006163-168"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>150<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> a new period began. It was marked by <a href="/wiki/Montagu%E2%80%93Chelmsford_Reforms" title="Montagu–Chelmsford Reforms">British reforms</a> but also <a href="/wiki/Rowlatt_act" class="mw-redirect" title="Rowlatt act">repressive legislation</a>, by more strident Indian calls for self-rule, and by the beginnings of a <a href="/wiki/Nonviolent" class="mw-redirect" title="Nonviolent">nonviolent</a> movement of non-co-operation led by <a href="/wiki/Mahatma_Gandhi" title="Mahatma Gandhi">Mahatma Gandhi</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEMetcalfMetcalf2006167&#95;169-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMetcalfMetcalf2006167-169"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>151<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> During the 1930s, the British enacted slow legislative reform; the Indian National Congress won victories in the resulting elections.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEMetcalfMetcalf2006195–197&#95;170-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMetcalfMetcalf2006195–197-170"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>152<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The next decade was beset with crises: <a href="/wiki/India_in_World_War_II" title="India in World War II">Indian participation in World War&#160;II</a>, the Congress's final push for non-co-operation, and an upsurge of <a href="/wiki/Muslim_nationalism_in_South_Asia" title="Muslim nationalism in South Asia">Muslim nationalism</a>. All were capped by the advent of independence in 1947, but tempered by the <a href="/wiki/Partition_of_India" title="Partition of India">partition of India</a> into two states: India and Pakistan.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEMetcalfMetcalf2006203&#95;171-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMetcalfMetcalf2006203-171"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>153<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>India's constitution was adopted in 1950 and established a secular, democratic republic.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEMetcalfMetcalf2006231&#95;172-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMetcalfMetcalf2006231-172"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>154<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Economic liberalisation has created a large urban middle class and transformed India into a <a href="/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(real)_growth_rate" class="mw-redirect" title="List of countries by GDP (real) growth rate">fast growing economy</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-exp-middle-class&#95;173-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-exp-middle-class-173"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>155<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-MetcalfMetcalf2012-265&#95;73-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-MetcalfMetcalf2012-265-73"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>61<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> However, India has been hamstrung by persistent poverty, both rural and urban;<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEMetcalfMetcalf2006265–266&#95;174-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMetcalfMetcalf2006265–266-174"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>156<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Religious_violence_in_India" title="Religious violence in India">religious</a>- and <a href="/wiki/Caste-related_violence_in_India" title="Caste-related violence in India">caste-related violence</a>;<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEMetcalfMetcalf2006266–270&#95;175-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMetcalfMetcalf2006266–270-175"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>157<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Naxalite%E2%80%93Maoist_insurgency" title="Naxalite–Maoist insurgency">Maoist-inspired Naxalite insurgencies</a>;<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEMetcalfMetcalf2006253&#95;176-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMetcalfMetcalf2006253-176"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>158<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> and <a href="/wiki/Insurgency_in_Jammu_and_Kashmir" title="Insurgency in Jammu and Kashmir">separatism in Jammu and Kashmir</a> and <a href="/wiki/Insurgency_in_Northeast_India" title="Insurgency in Northeast India">in Northeast India</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEMetcalfMetcalf2006274&#95;177-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMetcalfMetcalf2006274-177"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>159<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> India has unresolved territorial disputes with <a href="/wiki/China%E2%80%93India_relations#1960s" title="China–India relations">China</a> and with <a href="/wiki/Indo-Pakistani_wars_and_conflicts" class="mw-redirect" title="Indo-Pakistani wars and conflicts">Pakistan</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEMetcalfMetcalf2006247–248&#95;178-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMetcalfMetcalf2006247–248-178"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>160<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Geography">Geography</h2></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">See also: <a href="/wiki/Geography_of_India" title="Geography of India">Geography of India</a></div>
<p>India accounts for the bulk of the Indian subcontinent, lying atop the <a href="/wiki/Indian_Plate" class="mw-redirect" title="Indian Plate">Indian tectonic plate</a>, and a part of the <a href="/wiki/Indo-Australian_Plate" class="mw-redirect" title="Indo-Australian Plate">Indo-Australian Plate</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEAliAitchison2005&#95;179-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAliAitchison2005-179"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>161<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> India's defining geologic processes began approximately 70&#160;million years ago, when the Indian Plate, then part of the southern supercontinent <a href="/wiki/Gondwana" title="Gondwana">Gondwana</a>, began a north-eastward <a href="/wiki/Plate_tectonics" title="Plate tectonics">drift</a> caused by <a href="/wiki/Seafloor_spreading" title="Seafloor spreading">seafloor spreading</a> to its south-west, and later, south and south-east.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEAliAitchison2005&#95;179-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAliAitchison2005-179"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>161<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Simultaneously, the vast <a href="/wiki/Tethys_Ocean" title="Tethys Ocean">Tethyan</a> <a href="/wiki/Oceanic_crust" title="Oceanic crust">oceanic crust</a>, to its northeast, began to <a href="/wiki/Subduction" title="Subduction">subduct</a> under the <a href="/wiki/Eurasian_Plate" class="mw-redirect" title="Eurasian Plate">Eurasian Plate</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEAliAitchison2005&#95;179-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAliAitchison2005-179"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>161<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The <a href="/wiki/Indian_plate" title="Indian plate">Indian continental crust</a>, however, was obstructed and was sheared horizontally. Its lower crust and mantle slid under, but the upper layer piled up in sheets (or <a href="/wiki/Nappe" title="Nappe">nappes</a>) ahead of the subduction zone.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEMolnar2015117&#95;180-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMolnar2015117-180"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>162<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> This created the <a href="/wiki/Orogeny" title="Orogeny">orogeny</a>, or process of mountain building, of the Himalayas.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEMolnar2015118&#95;181-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMolnar2015118-181"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>163<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The middle and stiffer layer continued to push into Tibet, causing <a href="/wiki/Thrust_tectonics" title="Thrust tectonics">crustal thickening</a> of the <a href="/wiki/Tibetan_Plateau" title="Tibetan Plateau">Tibetan Plateau</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEMolnar2015128&#95;182-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMolnar2015128-182"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>164<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Immediately south of the emerging Himalayas, plate movement created a vast crescent-shaped <a href="/wiki/Trough_(geology)" title="Trough (geology)">trough</a> that rapidly filled with river-borne sediment<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEDikshitSchwartzberg20237&#95;183-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDikshitSchwartzberg20237-183"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>165<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> and now constitutes the <a href="/wiki/Indo-Gangetic_Plain" title="Indo-Gangetic Plain">Indo-Gangetic Plain</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEPrakash&#95;et&#95;al.2000&#95;184-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPrakash_et_al.2000-184"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>166<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The original Indian plate makes its first appearance above the sediment in the ancient <a href="/wiki/Aravalli_range" class="mw-redirect" title="Aravalli range">Aravalli range</a>, which extends from the <a href="/wiki/Delhi_Ridge" title="Delhi Ridge">Delhi Ridge</a> in a southwesterly direction. To the west lies the <a href="/wiki/Thar_Desert" title="Thar Desert">Thar Desert</a>, the eastern spread of which is checked by the Aravallis.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-aravalli&#95;185-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-aravalli-185"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>167<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-prasad-aravalli&#95;186-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-prasad-aravalli-186"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>168<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-fisher-aravalli&#95;187-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-fisher-aravalli-187"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>169<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The remaining Indian Plate survives as <a href="/wiki/South_India" title="South India">peninsular India</a>, the oldest and geologically most stable part of India. It extends as far north as the <a href="/wiki/Satpura_Range" title="Satpura Range">Satpura</a> and <a href="/wiki/Vindhya_Range" title="Vindhya Range">Vindhya</a> ranges in central India. These parallel chains run from the Arabian Sea coast in Gujarat in the west to the coal-rich <a href="/wiki/Chota_Nagpur_Plateau" title="Chota Nagpur Plateau">Chota Nagpur Plateau</a> in Jharkhand in the east.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEDikshitSchwartzberg20238&#95;188-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDikshitSchwartzberg20238-188"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>170<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> To the south, the remaining peninsular landmass, the <a href="/wiki/Deccan_Plateau" title="Deccan Plateau">Deccan Plateau</a>, is flanked on the west and east by coastal ranges known as the <a href="/wiki/Western_Ghats" title="Western Ghats">Western</a> and <a href="/wiki/Eastern_Ghats" title="Eastern Ghats">Eastern Ghats</a>;<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEDikshitSchwartzberg20239–10&#95;189-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDikshitSchwartzberg20239–10-189"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>171<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> the plateau contains the country's oldest rock formations, some over one billion years old. Constituted in such fashion, India lies to the north of the equator between 6° 44′ and 35° 30′ north latitude<sup id="cite&#95;ref-190" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-190"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>s<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> and 68° 7′ and 97° 25′ east longitude.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEMinistry&#95;of&#95;Information&#95;and&#95;Broadcasting20071&#95;191-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMinistry_of_Information_and_Broadcasting20071-191"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>172<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Major Himalayan-origin rivers that substantially flow through India include the <a href="/wiki/Ganges" title="Ganges">Ganges</a> and the <a href="/wiki/Brahmaputra_River" title="Brahmaputra River">Brahmaputra</a>, both of which drain into the <a href="/wiki/Bay_of_Bengal" title="Bay of Bengal">Bay of Bengal</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEDikshitSchwartzberg202315&#95;192-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDikshitSchwartzberg202315-192"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>173<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Important tributaries of the Ganges include the <a href="/wiki/Yamuna" title="Yamuna">Yamuna</a> and the <a href="/wiki/Kosi_River" title="Kosi River">Kosi</a>. The Kosi's extremely low gradient, caused by long-term silt deposition, leads to severe floods and course changes.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEDuff1993353&#95;193-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDuff1993353-193"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>174<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEBasuXavier2017&#91;httpsbooksgooglecombooksidnXmLDgAAQBAJpgPA78&#95;78&#93;&#95;194-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBasuXavier2017[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidnXmLDgAAQBAJpgPA78_78]-194"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>175<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Major peninsular rivers, whose steeper gradients prevent their waters from flooding, include the <a href="/wiki/Godavari_River" title="Godavari River">Godavari</a>, the <a href="/wiki/Mahanadi_River" class="mw-redirect" title="Mahanadi River">Mahanadi</a>, the <a href="/wiki/Kaveri_River" class="mw-redirect" title="Kaveri River">Kaveri</a>, and the <a href="/wiki/Krishna_River" title="Krishna River">Krishna</a>, which also drain into the Bay of Bengal;<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEDikshitSchwartzberg202316&#95;195-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDikshitSchwartzberg202316-195"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>176<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> and the <a href="/wiki/Narmada_River" title="Narmada River">Narmada</a> and the <a href="/wiki/Tapti_River" title="Tapti River">Tapti</a>, which drain into the <a href="/wiki/Arabian_Sea" title="Arabian Sea">Arabian Sea</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEDikshitSchwartzberg202317&#95;196-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDikshitSchwartzberg202317-196"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>177<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>India's coastline measures 7,517 kilometres (4,700&#160;mi) in length; of this distance, 5,423 kilometres (3,400&#160;mi) belong to peninsular India and 2,094 kilometres (1,300&#160;mi) to the Andaman, Nicobar, and Lakshadweep island chains.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEKumarPathakPednekarRaju2006&#95;197-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKumarPathakPednekarRaju2006-197"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>178<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> According to the Indian naval hydrographic charts, the <a href="/wiki/Coastal_India" title="Coastal India">mainland coastline</a> consists of the following: 43% sandy beaches; 11% rocky shores, including cliffs; and 46% <a href="/wiki/Mudflat" title="Mudflat">mudflats</a> or marshy shores.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEKumarPathakPednekarRaju2006&#95;197-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKumarPathakPednekarRaju2006-197"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>178<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Coastal features include the marshy <a href="/wiki/Rann_of_Kutch" title="Rann of Kutch">Rann of Kutch</a> of western India and the alluvial <a href="/wiki/Sundarbans" title="Sundarbans">Sundarbans</a> delta of eastern India; the latter is shared with Bangladesh.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEDikshitSchwartzberg202312&#95;198-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDikshitSchwartzberg202312-198"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>179<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> India has two <a href="/wiki/Archipelago" title="Archipelago">archipelagos</a>: the <a href="/wiki/Lakshadweep" title="Lakshadweep">Lakshadweep</a> <a href="/wiki/Atoll" title="Atoll">coral atolls</a> off India's south-western coast; and the Andaman and Nicobar Islands, a volcanic chain in the <a href="/wiki/Andaman_Sea" title="Andaman Sea">Andaman Sea</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEDikshitSchwartzberg202313&#95;199-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDikshitSchwartzberg202313-199"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>180<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<ul class="gallery mw-gallery-packed">
		<li class="gallerybox" style="width: 862.66666666667px">
			<div class="thumb" style="width: 860.66666666667px;"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:Panorama_of_Himalayas_from_Ranikhet,_Uttarakhand,_India.jpg" class="mw-file-description" title="A panoramic view of the Garhwal and Kumaon Himalayas. 		<li class="gallerybox" style="width: 218px">

		</li>

</ul>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Climate">Climate</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Climate_of_India" title="Climate of India">Climate of India</a></div>
<p>The Indian climate is strongly influenced by the Himalayas and the Thar Desert, both of which drive the economically and culturally pivotal summer and winter <a href="/wiki/Monsoon" title="Monsoon">monsoons</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEChang1967391–394&#95;201-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChang1967391–394-201"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>182<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The Himalayas prevent cold Central Asian <a href="/wiki/Katabatic_wind" title="Katabatic wind">katabatic winds</a> from blowing in, keeping the bulk of the Indian subcontinent warmer than most locations at similar latitudes.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEPosey1994118&#95;202-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPosey1994118-202"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>183<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEWolpert20034&#95;203-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWolpert20034-203"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>184<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The Thar Desert plays a crucial role in attracting the moisture-laden south-west summer monsoon winds that, between June and October, provide the majority of India's rainfall.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEChang1967391–394&#95;201-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChang1967391–394-201"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>182<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Four major climatic groupings predominate in India: <a href="/wiki/Climate_of_India#Tropical_wet" title="Climate of India">tropical wet</a>, <a href="/wiki/Climate_of_India#Arid_and_semi-arid_regions" title="Climate of India">tropical dry</a>, <a href="/wiki/Climate_of_India#Subtropical_humid" title="Climate of India">subtropical humid</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Climate_of_India#Mountain" title="Climate of India">montane</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEHeitzmanWorden199697&#95;204-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeitzmanWorden199697-204"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>185<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Temperatures in India have risen by 0.7&#160;°C (1.3&#160;°F) between 1901 and 2018.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-205" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-205"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>186<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Climate_change_in_India" title="Climate change in India">Climate change in India</a> is often thought to be the cause. The <a href="/wiki/Retreat_of_glaciers_since_1850" title="Retreat of glaciers since 1850">retreat of Himalayan glaciers</a> has adversely affected the <a href="/wiki/Volumetric_flow_rate" title="Volumetric flow rate">flow rate</a> of the major Himalayan rivers, including the <a href="/wiki/Ganges" title="Ganges">Ganges</a> and the <a href="/wiki/Brahmaputra" class="mw-redirect" title="Brahmaputra">Brahmaputra</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-206" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-206"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>187<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> According to some current projections, the number and severity of droughts in India will have markedly increased by the end of the present century.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-207" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-207"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>188<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<ul class="gallery mw-gallery-packed">
		<li class="gallerybox" style="width: 268.66666666667px">
			<div class="thumb" style="width: 266.66666666667px;"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:Coral_Tree_Monsoon_Mallalli_Falls_Hassan_Jun24_A7CR_01575.jpg" class="mw-file-description" title="Indian coral tree in bloom in the mist of the Southwest Monsoon, Mallalli Falls, Hassan, Karnataka"><img alt="Indian coral tree in bloom in the mist of the Southwest Monsoon, Mallalli Falls, Hassan, Karnataka" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/Coral_Tree_Monsoon_Mallalli_Falls_Hassan_Jun24_A7CR_01575.jpg/500px-Coral_Tree_Monsoon_Mallalli_Falls_Hassan_Jun24_A7CR_01575.jpg" decoding="async" width="267" height="200" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/Coral_Tree_Monsoon_Mallalli_Falls_Hassan_Jun24_A7CR_01575.jpg/960px-Coral_Tree_Monsoon_Mallalli_Falls_Hassan_Jun24_A7CR_01575.jpg 1.5x" data-file-width="6711" data-file-height="5033" /></a></span></div>

		</li>
		<li class="gallerybox" style="width: 326.66666666667px">
			<div class="thumb" style="width: 324.66666666667px;"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:Dromedary_in_Thar_desert.jpg" class="mw-file-description" title="A dromedary in the Thar desert"><img alt="A dromedary in the Thar desert" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/90/Dromedary_in_Thar_desert.jpg/500px-Dromedary_in_Thar_desert.jpg" decoding="async" width="325" height="200" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/90/Dromedary_in_Thar_desert.jpg/960px-Dromedary_in_Thar_desert.jpg 1.5x, //upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/90/Dromedary_in_Thar_desert.jpg/1280px-Dromedary_in_Thar_desert.jpg 2x" data-file-width="4679" data-file-height="2883" /></a></span></div>
