</ul>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Biodiversity">Biodiversity</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main articles: <a href="/wiki/Forestry_in_India" title="Forestry in India">Forestry in India</a> and <a href="/wiki/Wildlife_of_India" title="Wildlife of India">Wildlife of India</a></div>
<p>India is a <a href="/wiki/Megadiverse_country" class="mw-redirect" title="Megadiverse country">megadiverse country</a>, a term employed for 17 countries that host high <a href="/wiki/Biological_diversity" class="mw-redirect" title="Biological diversity">biological diversity</a> and contain many species <a href="/wiki/Indigenous_(ecology)" class="mw-redirect" title="Indigenous (ecology)">indigenous</a>, or <a href="/wiki/Endemic" class="mw-redirect" title="Endemic">endemic</a>, to them.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-208" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-208"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>189<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> India is the <a href="/wiki/Habitat" title="Habitat">habitat</a> for 8.6% of all <a href="/wiki/Mammal" title="Mammal">mammals</a>, 13.7% of <a href="/wiki/Bird" title="Bird">bird</a> species, 7.9% of <a href="/wiki/Reptile" title="Reptile">reptile</a> species, 6% of <a href="/wiki/Amphibian" title="Amphibian">amphibian</a> species, 12.2% of <a href="/wiki/Fish" title="Fish">fish</a> species, and 6.0% of all <a href="/wiki/Flowering_plant" title="Flowering plant">flowering plant</a> species.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-209" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-209"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>190<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-Puri&#95;210-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Puri-210"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>191<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Fully a third of Indian plant species are endemic.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEBasak198324&#95;211-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBasak198324-211"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>192<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> India also overlaps four of the world's 34 <a href="/wiki/Biodiversity_hotspot" title="Biodiversity hotspot">biodiversity hotspots</a>,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-IUCN-India&#95;212-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-IUCN-India-212"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>193<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> or regions that display significant habitat loss in the presence of high endemism.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-214" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-214"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>t<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-SivaperumanVenkataraman2018&#95;213-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-SivaperumanVenkataraman2018-213"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>194<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>India's most dense forests, such as the <a href="/wiki/Tropical_and_subtropical_moist_broadleaf_forest" class="mw-redirect" title="Tropical and subtropical moist broadleaf forest">tropical moist forest</a> of the <a href="/wiki/Andaman_Islands" title="Andaman Islands">Andaman Islands</a>, the <a href="/wiki/Western_Ghats" title="Western Ghats">Western Ghats</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Northeast_India" title="Northeast India">Northeast India</a>, occupy approximately 3% of its land area.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Jha2018&#95;215-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Jha2018-215"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>195<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-indiaforest&#95;216-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-indiaforest-216"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>196<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> <i>Moderately dense forest</i>, whose canopy density is between 40% and 70%, occupies 9.39% of India's land area.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Jha2018&#95;215-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Jha2018-215"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>195<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-indiaforest&#95;216-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-indiaforest-216"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>196<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> It predominates in the <a href="/wiki/Temperate_coniferous_forest" title="Temperate coniferous forest">temperate coniferous forest</a> of the <a href="/wiki/Himalaya" class="mw-redirect" title="Himalaya">Himalayas</a>, the moist deciduous <i><a href="/wiki/Shorea_robusta" title="Shorea robusta">sal</a></i> forest of eastern India, and the dry deciduous <a href="/wiki/Teak" title="Teak">teak</a> forest of central and southern India.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTETritsch200111–12&#95;217-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTETritsch200111–12-217"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>197<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> India has two natural zones of <a href="/wiki/Deserts_and_xeric_shrublands" title="Deserts and xeric shrublands">thorn forest</a>, one in the <a href="/wiki/Deccan_Plateau" title="Deccan Plateau">Deccan Plateau</a>, immediately east of the Western Ghats, and the other in the western part of the Indo-Gangetic plain, now turned into rich agricultural land by irrigation, its features no longer visible.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-218" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-218"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>198<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Among the Indian subcontinent's notable indigenous trees are the <a href="/wiki/Astringent" title="Astringent">astringent</a> <i><a href="/wiki/Azadirachta_indica" title="Azadirachta indica">Azadirachta indica</a></i>, or <i>neem</i>, which is widely used in rural Indian <a href="/wiki/Herbal_medicine" title="Herbal medicine">herbal medicine</a>,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Goyal2006&#95;219-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Goyal2006-219"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>199<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> and the luxuriant <i><a href="/wiki/Ficus_religiosa" title="Ficus religiosa">Ficus religiosa</a></i>, or <i>peepul</i>,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Hughes2013&#95;220-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Hughes2013-220"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>200<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> which is displayed on the ancient seals of <a href="/wiki/Mohenjo-daro" title="Mohenjo-daro">Mohenjo-daro</a>,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-AmeriCostello2018&#95;221-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-AmeriCostello2018-221"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>201<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> and under which <a href="/wiki/Gautama_Buddha" class="mw-redirect" title="Gautama Buddha">the Buddha</a> is recorded in the <a href="/wiki/P%C4%81li_Canon" class="mw-redirect" title="Pāli Canon">Pali canon</a> to have sought enlightenment.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Gwynne2011&#95;222-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Gwynne2011-222"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>202<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Many Indian species have descended from those of <a href="/wiki/Gondwana" title="Gondwana">Gondwana</a>, the southern <a href="/wiki/Supercontinent" title="Supercontinent">supercontinent</a> from which India separated more than 100 million years ago.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTECrameOwen2002142&#95;223-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTECrameOwen2002142-223"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>203<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> India's subsequent collision with Eurasia set off a mass exchange of species. However, <a href="/wiki/Deccan_Traps" title="Deccan Traps">volcanism</a> and <a href="/wiki/Climate_variability_and_change" title="Climate variability and change">climatic changes</a> later caused the extinction of many endemic Indian forms.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEKaranth2006&#95;224-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKaranth2006-224"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>204<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Still later, mammals entered India from Asia through two <a href="/wiki/Zoogeography" title="Zoogeography">zoogeographic</a> passes flanking the Himalayas.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTETritsch200114&#95;225-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTETritsch200114-225"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>205<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> This lowered endemism among India's mammals, which stands at 12.6%, contrasting with 45.8% among reptiles and 55.8% among amphibians.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Puri&#95;210-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Puri-210"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>191<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Among endemics are the vulnerable<sup id="cite&#95;ref-226" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-226"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>206<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Nilgiri_Langur" class="mw-redirect" title="Nilgiri Langur">hooded leaf monkey</a><sup id="cite&#95;ref-itis&#95;227-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-itis-227"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>207<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> and the threatened <a href="/wiki/Duttaphrynus_beddomii" title="Duttaphrynus beddomii">Beddome's toad</a><sup id="cite&#95;ref-IUCN&#95;228-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-IUCN-228"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>208<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-229" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-229"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>209<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> of the Western Ghats.
</p><p>India contains 172 <a href="/wiki/World_Conservation_Union" class="mw-redirect" title="World Conservation Union">IUCN</a>-designated <a href="/wiki/List_of_endangered_animals_in_India" title="List of endangered animals in India">threatened animal species</a>, or 2.9% of endangered forms.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEIUCN1994xli&#95;230-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIUCN1994xli-230"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>210<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> These include the endangered <a href="/wiki/Bengal_tiger" title="Bengal tiger">Bengal tiger</a> and the <a href="/wiki/South_Asian_river_dolphin" title="South Asian river dolphin">Ganges river dolphin</a>. <a href="/wiki/Critically_endangered" title="Critically endangered">Critically endangered</a> species include the <a href="/wiki/Gharial" title="Gharial">gharial</a>, a <a href="/wiki/Crocodilian" class="mw-redirect" title="Crocodilian">crocodilian</a>; the <a href="/wiki/Great_Indian_bustard" title="Great Indian bustard">great Indian bustard</a>; and the <a href="/wiki/White-rumped_vulture" title="White-rumped vulture">Indian white-rumped vulture</a>, which has become nearly extinct by having ingested the carrion of <a href="/wiki/Diclofenac" title="Diclofenac">diclofenac</a>-treated cattle.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-LovetteFitzpatrick2016&#95;231-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-LovetteFitzpatrick2016-231"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>211<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Before they were extensively used for agriculture and cleared for human settlement, the thorn forests of Punjab were mingled at intervals with open grasslands that were grazed by large herds of <a href="/wiki/Blackbuck_Conservation_Area" title="Blackbuck Conservation Area">blackbuck</a> preyed on by the <a href="/wiki/Asiatic_cheetah" title="Asiatic cheetah">Asiatic cheetah</a>; the blackbuck, no longer extant in Punjab, is now severely endangered in India, and the cheetah is extinct.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-232" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-232"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>212<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The pervasive and ecologically devastating human encroachment of recent decades has critically endangered Indian wildlife. In response, the system of <a href="/wiki/National_parks_of_India" class="mw-redirect" title="National parks of India">national parks</a> and <a href="/wiki/Protected_areas_of_India" title="Protected areas of India">protected areas</a>, first established in 1935, was expanded substantially. In 1972, India enacted the <a href="/wiki/Wildlife_Protection_Act_of_1972" class="mw-redirect" title="Wildlife Protection Act of 1972">Wildlife Protection Act</a><sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEMinistry&#95;of&#95;Environment&#95;and&#95;Forests&#95;1972&#95;233-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMinistry_of_Environment_and_Forests_1972-233"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>213<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> and <a href="/wiki/Project_Tiger" title="Project Tiger">Project Tiger</a> to safeguard crucial wilderness; the Forest Conservation Act was enacted in 1980 and amendments added in 1988.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEDepartment&#95;of&#95;Environment&#95;and&#95;Forests1988&#95;234-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDepartment_of_Environment_and_Forests1988-234"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>214<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> India hosts <a href="/wiki/Wildlife_sanctuaries_of_India" class="mw-redirect" title="Wildlife sanctuaries of India">more than five hundred wildlife sanctuaries</a> and <a href="/wiki/Biosphere_reserves_of_India" title="Biosphere reserves of India">eighteen<span class="nowrap">&#160;</span>biosphere reserves</a>,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-235" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-235"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>215<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> four of which are part of the <a href="/wiki/World_Network_of_Biosphere_Reserves" title="World Network of Biosphere Reserves">World Network of Biosphere Reserves</a>; its <a href="/wiki/List_of_Ramsar_sites_in_India" title="List of Ramsar sites in India">eighty-nine wetlands</a> are registered under the <a href="/wiki/Ramsar_Convention" title="Ramsar Convention">Ramsar Convention</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-236" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-236"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>216<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<ul class="gallery mw-gallery-packed">
		<li class="gallerybox" style="width: 302px">
			<div class="thumb" style="width: 300px;"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:Attacus_taprobanis-Kadavoor-2018-07-07-001.jpg" class="mw-file-description" title="An Attacus taprobanis moth from Kadavoor, Kerala"><img alt="An Attacus taprobanis moth from Kadavoor, Kerala" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/Attacus_taprobanis-Kadavoor-2018-07-07-001.jpg/500px-Attacus_taprobanis-Kadavoor-2018-07-07-001.jpg" decoding="async" width="300" height="200" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/Attacus_taprobanis-Kadavoor-2018-07-07-001.jpg/960px-Attacus_taprobanis-Kadavoor-2018-07-07-001.jpg 1.5x" data-file-width="6000" data-file-height="4000" /></a></span></div>
			<div class="gallerytext">An <i><a href="/wiki/Attacus_taprobanis" title="Attacus taprobanis">Attacus taprobanis</a></i> moth from <a href="/wiki/Kadavoor" title="Kadavoor">Kadavoor</a>, <a href="/wiki/Kerala" title="Kerala">Kerala</a></div>
		</li>
		<li class="gallerybox" style="width: 302px">
			<div class="thumb" style="width: 300px;"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:Bengal_tiger_in_Sanjay_Dubri_Tiger_Reserve_December_2024_by_Tisha_Mukherjee_11.jpg" class="mw-file-description" title="India has the majority of the world&#39;s wild tigers, approximately 3,170 in 2022.[217]"><img alt="India has the majority of the world&#39;s wild tigers, approximately 3,170 in 2022.[217]" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/84/Bengal_tiger_in_Sanjay_Dubri_Tiger_Reserve_December_2024_by_Tisha_Mukherjee_11.jpg/500px-Bengal_tiger_in_Sanjay_Dubri_Tiger_Reserve_December_2024_by_Tisha_Mukherjee_11.jpg" decoding="async" width="300" height="200" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/84/Bengal_tiger_in_Sanjay_Dubri_Tiger_Reserve_December_2024_by_Tisha_Mukherjee_11.jpg/960px-Bengal_tiger_in_Sanjay_Dubri_Tiger_Reserve_December_2024_by_Tisha_Mukherjee_11.jpg 1.5x" data-file-width="4391" data-file-height="2927" /></a></span></div>
			<div class="gallerytext">India has the majority of the world's wild <a href="/wiki/Tiger" title="Tiger">tigers</a>, approximately 3,170 in 2022.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-237" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-237"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>217<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></div>
		</li>
		<li class="gallerybox" style="width: 302px">
			<div class="thumb" style="width: 300px;"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:003_Purple_sunbird_in_Jim_Corbett_National_Park_Photo_by_Giles_Laurent.jpg" class="mw-file-description" title="A purple sunbird perched on an Indian coral tree, Jim Corbett National Park, Uttarakhand"><img alt="A purple sunbird perched on an Indian coral tree, Jim Corbett National Park, Uttarakhand" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5f/003_Purple_sunbird_in_Jim_Corbett_National_Park_Photo_by_Giles_Laurent.jpg/500px-003_Purple_sunbird_in_Jim_Corbett_National_Park_Photo_by_Giles_Laurent.jpg" decoding="async" width="300" height="200" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5f/003_Purple_sunbird_in_Jim_Corbett_National_Park_Photo_by_Giles_Laurent.jpg/960px-003_Purple_sunbird_in_Jim_Corbett_National_Park_Photo_by_Giles_Laurent.jpg 1.5x" data-file-width="6619" data-file-height="4413" /></a></span></div>
			<div class="gallerytext">A <a href="/wiki/Purple_sunbird" title="Purple sunbird">purple sunbird</a> perched on an <a href="/wiki/Indian_coral_tree" class="mw-redirect" title="Indian coral tree">Indian coral tree</a>, <a href="/wiki/Jim_Corbett_National_Park" title="Jim Corbett National Park">Jim Corbett National Park</a>, <a href="/wiki/Uttarakhand" title="Uttarakhand">Uttarakhand</a></div>
		</li>
		<li class="gallerybox" style="width: 257.33333333333px">
			<div class="thumb" style="width: 255.33333333333px;"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:Crested_hawk-eagle_(Nisaetus_cirrhatus_cirrhatus)_with_Indian_garden_lizard.jpg" class="mw-file-description" title="A Crested hawk-eagle with an Indian garden lizard in Satpura National Park, Madhya Pradesh"><img alt="A Crested hawk-eagle with an Indian garden lizard in Satpura National Park, Madhya Pradesh" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/Crested_hawk-eagle_%28Nisaetus_cirrhatus_cirrhatus%29_with_Indian_garden_lizard.jpg/500px-Crested_hawk-eagle_%28Nisaetus_cirrhatus_cirrhatus%29_with_Indian_garden_lizard.jpg" decoding="async" width="256" height="200" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/Crested_hawk-eagle_%28Nisaetus_cirrhatus_cirrhatus%29_with_Indian_garden_lizard.jpg/960px-Crested_hawk-eagle_%28Nisaetus_cirrhatus_cirrhatus%29_with_Indian_garden_lizard.jpg 1.5x" data-file-width="4077" data-file-height="3198" /></a></span></div>
			<div class="gallerytext">A <a href="/wiki/Crested_hawk-eagle" class="mw-redirect" title="Crested hawk-eagle">Crested hawk-eagle</a> with an Indian <a href="/wiki/Garden_lizard" class="mw-redirect" title="Garden lizard">garden lizard</a> in <a href="/wiki/Satpura_National_Park" class="mw-redirect" title="Satpura National Park">Satpura National Park</a>, Madhya Pradesh</div>
		</li>
		<li class="gallerybox" style="width: 402px">
			<div class="thumb" style="width: 400px;"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:Saltwater_crocodile_in_Sundarbans_National_Park_November_2024_by_Tisha_Mukherjee_03.jpg" class="mw-file-description" title="Saltwater crocodile in Sundarbans National Park, West Bengal"><img alt="Saltwater crocodile in Sundarbans National Park, West Bengal" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/42/Saltwater_crocodile_in_Sundarbans_National_Park_November_2024_by_Tisha_Mukherjee_03.jpg/960px-Saltwater_crocodile_in_Sundarbans_National_Park_November_2024_by_Tisha_Mukherjee_03.jpg" decoding="async" width="400" height="200" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/42/Saltwater_crocodile_in_Sundarbans_National_Park_November_2024_by_Tisha_Mukherjee_03.jpg/1280px-Saltwater_crocodile_in_Sundarbans_National_Park_November_2024_by_Tisha_Mukherjee_03.jpg 2x" data-file-width="5461" data-file-height="2731" /></a></span></div>
			<div class="gallerytext"><a href="/wiki/Saltwater_crocodile" title="Saltwater crocodile">Saltwater crocodile</a> in <a href="/wiki/Sundarbans_National_Park" title="Sundarbans National Park">Sundarbans National Park</a>, <a href="/wiki/West_Bengal" title="West Bengal">West Bengal</a></div>
		</li>
</ul>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Government_and_politics">Government and politics</h2></div>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Politics">Politics</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Politics_of_India" title="Politics of India">Politics of India</a></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">See also: <a href="/wiki/Democracy_in_India" title="Democracy in India">Democracy in India</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size mw-halign-right" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Indira_Gandhi_official_portrait.png" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a1/Indira_Gandhi_official_portrait.png/250px-Indira_Gandhi_official_portrait.png" decoding="async" width="250" height="370" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a1/Indira_Gandhi_official_portrait.png/500px-Indira_Gandhi_official_portrait.png 1.5x" data-file-width="2592" data-file-height="3840" /></a><figcaption><a href="/wiki/Indira_Gandhi" title="Indira Gandhi">Indira Gandhi</a>, India's first and only female Prime Minister, is the second-longest-serving Indian prime minister after her father, <a href="/wiki/Jawaharlal_Nehru" title="Jawaharlal Nehru">Jawaharlal Nehru</a>.</figcaption></figure>
<figure class="mw-default-size mw-halign-right" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:The_Prime_Minister,_Shri_Narendra_Modi_addressing_the_inaugural_function_of_the_WCIT_India_2018_at_Hyderabad_via_video_conferencing_from_Misuru,_on_February_19,_2018_(1).jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ed/The_Prime_Minister%2C_Shri_Narendra_Modi_addressing_the_inaugural_function_of_the_WCIT_India_2018_at_Hyderabad_via_video_conferencing_from_Misuru%2C_on_February_19%2C_2018_%281%29.jpg/250px-thumbnail.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="212" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ed/The_Prime_Minister%2C_Shri_Narendra_Modi_addressing_the_inaugural_function_of_the_WCIT_India_2018_at_Hyderabad_via_video_conferencing_from_Misuru%2C_on_February_19%2C_2018_%281%29.jpg/500px-thumbnail.jpg 1.5x" data-file-width="1274" data-file-height="1080" /></a><figcaption><a href="/wiki/Narendra_Modi" title="Narendra Modi">Narendra Modi</a> has been India's Prime Minister since 2014.</figcaption></figure>
<p>India is a <a href="/wiki/Parliamentary_republic" title="Parliamentary republic">parliamentary republic</a> with a <a href="/wiki/Multi-party_system" title="Multi-party system">multi-party system</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEBurnellCalvert1999125&#95;238-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurnellCalvert1999125-238"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>218<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> There are six<span class="nowrap">&#160;</span>recognised <a href="/wiki/List_of_political_parties_in_India#National_parties" title="List of political parties in India">national parties</a> in the country, including the <a href="/wiki/Indian_National_Congress" title="Indian National Congress">Indian National Congress</a> (generally, "the Congress") and the <a href="/wiki/Bharatiya_Janata_Party" title="Bharatiya Janata Party">Bharatiya Janata Party</a> (BJP); there are over 50<span class="nowrap">&#160;</span><a href="/wiki/List_of_political_parties_in_India#State_parties" title="List of political parties in India">regional parties</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEElection&#95;Commission&#95;of&#95;India&#95;239-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEElection_Commission_of_India-239"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>219<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The Congress is considered the <a href="/wiki/Centrism" title="Centrism">ideological centre</a> in Indian <a href="/wiki/Political_culture" title="Political culture">political culture</a>;<sup id="cite&#95;ref-240" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-240"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>220<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> the BJP is <a href="/wiki/Right-wing_politics" title="Right-wing politics">right-wing</a>. 
</p><p>After India's independence on August 15, 1947, <a href="/wiki/Jawaharlal_Nehru" title="Jawaharlal Nehru">Jawaharlal Nehru</a> became prime minister of the <a href="/wiki/Dominion_of_India" title="Dominion of India">Dominion of India</a>, an office he held until January 26, 1950, when India became a republic; Nehru remained the caretaker prime minister until the following year.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-244" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-244"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>u<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In the general elections in <a href="/wiki/1951_Indian_general_election" class="mw-redirect" title="1951 Indian general election">1951</a>, <a href="/wiki/1957_Indian_general_election" title="1957 Indian general election">1957</a>, and <a href="/wiki/1962_Indian_general_election" title="1962 Indian general election">1962</a>, the Congress, led by Nehru, won by comfortable margins. After Nehru died in office in May 1964, <a href="/wiki/Lal_Bahadur_Shastri" title="Lal Bahadur Shastri">Lal Bahadur Shastri</a> was unanimously chosen by the Congress to be parliamentary leader, and thus prime minister. After the <a href="/wiki/India%E2%80%93Pakistan_war_of_1965" title="India–Pakistan war of 1965">India–Pakistan war of 1965</a>, Shastri died in January 1966, soon after signing the <a href="/wiki/Tashkent_Declaration" title="Tashkent Declaration">Tashkent Peace Declaration</a>. The Congress chose <a href="/wiki/Indira_Gandhi" title="Indira Gandhi">Indira Gandhi</a> to be prime minister. She led the party to election victories in <a href="/wiki/1967_Indian_general_election" title="1967 Indian general election">1967</a> and <a href="/wiki/1971_Indian_general_election" title="1971 Indian general election">1971</a>, the latter a <a href="/wiki/Landslide_victory" title="Landslide victory">landslide</a> after Pakistan's defeat in the <a href="/wiki/Bangladesh_Liberation_War" title="Bangladesh Liberation War">Bangladesh Liberation War</a>. In 1975, Indira Gandhi declared a <a href="/wiki/The_Emergency_(India)" title="The Emergency (India)">state of emergency</a>, suspending many civil liberties. Following public discontent with the Emergency, the Congress was voted out of power in <a href="/wiki/1977_Indian_general_election" title="1977 Indian general election">1977</a>; <a href="/wiki/Janata_Party" title="Janata Party">Janata Party</a>, which had opposed the Emergency, was voted in. Its government lasted two years; <a href="/wiki/Morarji_Desai" title="Morarji Desai">Morarji Desai</a> and <a href="/wiki/Charan_Singh" title="Charan Singh">Charan Singh</a> served as prime ministers. The Congress returned to power in 1980. After a <a href="/wiki/Operation_Blue_Star" title="Operation Blue Star">military operation</a> against <a href="/wiki/Sikhs" title="Sikhs">Sikh</a> militants occupying the <a href="/wiki/Golden_Temple" title="Golden Temple">Golden Temple</a> in <a href="/wiki/Amritsar" title="Amritsar">Amritsar</a>, Indira Gandhi was <a href="/wiki/Assassination_of_Indira_Gandhi" title="Assassination of Indira Gandhi">assassinated</a> by a Sikh bodyguard on October 31, 1984. She was succeeded as prime minister by <a href="/wiki/Rajiv_Gandhi" title="Rajiv Gandhi">Rajiv Gandhi</a>, who led Congress to a comfortable victory in the <a href="/wiki/1984_Indian_general_election" title="1984 Indian general election">elections</a> at the end of the year. In 1989, a <a href="/wiki/National_Front_(India)" class="mw-redirect" title="National Front (India)">National Front</a> coalition, led by the <a href="/wiki/Janata_Dal" title="Janata Dal">Janata Dal</a>, in alliance with the <a href="/wiki/Communist_Party_of_India_(Marxist)" title="Communist Party of India (Marxist)">Left Front</a>, won the <a href="/wiki/1989_Indian_general_election" title="1989 Indian general election">general elections</a>. The subsequent government lasted just under two years; <a href="/wiki/V._P._Singh" title="V. P. Singh">V. P. Singh</a> and <a href="/wiki/Chandra_Shekhar" title="Chandra Shekhar">Chandra Shekhar</a> served as prime ministers.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEBhambhri1992118,&#95;143&#95;245-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBhambhri1992118,_143-245"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>224<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In 1991, soon after the first round of polling in the <a href="/wiki/1991_Indian_general_election" title="1991 Indian general election">general election</a>, Congress leader Rajiv Gandhi <a href="/wiki/Assassination_of_Rajiv_Gandhi" title="Assassination of Rajiv Gandhi">was assassinated</a> by a member of a <a href="/wiki/Sri_Lankan_Tamil" class="mw-redirect" title="Sri Lankan Tamil">Sri Lankan Tamil</a> <a href="/wiki/Liberation_Tigers_of_Tamil_Eelam" title="Liberation Tigers of Tamil Eelam">separatist organisation</a> who was seeking to avenge <a href="/wiki/Indian_intervention_in_the_Sri_Lankan_civil_war" title="Indian intervention in the Sri Lankan civil war">Indian intervention in the Sri Lankan civil war</a>. After the elections, Congress emerged as the largest single party; a new Congress leader, <a href="/wiki/P._V._Narasimha_Rao" title="P. V. Narasimha Rao">P. V. Narasimha Rao</a>, formed a <a href="/wiki/Minority_government" title="Minority government">minority government</a> which served a full five-year term.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-246" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-246"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>225<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>In 1996, the BJP briefly formed a government after winning the <a href="/wiki/1996_Indian_general_election" title="1996 Indian general election">general election</a>. <a href="/wiki/United_Front_(India,_1996)" title="United Front (India, 1996)">United Front</a> coalition governments followed, which relied on external political support, <a href="/wiki/H._D._Deve_Gowda" title="H. D. Deve Gowda">H. D. Deve Gowda</a> and <a href="/wiki/I._K._Gujral" class="mw-redirect" title="I. K. Gujral">I. K. Gujral</a> serving as prime ministers. After the <a href="/wiki/1998_Indian_general_election" title="1998 Indian general election">1998 Indian general election</a>, <a href="/wiki/Atal_Bihari_Vajpayee" title="Atal Bihari Vajpayee">Atal Bihari Vajpayee</a> of the BJP became prime minister; his government was short-lived due to the lack of a continued mandate. Elections were held again in <a href="/wiki/1999_Indian_general_election" title="1999 Indian general election">1999</a>. The BJP, now a part of the <a href="/wiki/National_Democratic_Alliance" title="National Democratic Alliance">National Democratic Alliance</a> (NDA), formed a coalition government led by Vajpayee, who became the first non-Congress prime minister to complete a five-year term.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEDunleavyDiwakarDunleavy2007&#95;247-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDunleavyDiwakarDunleavy2007-247"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>226<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In the <a href="/wiki/2004_Indian_general_election" title="2004 Indian general election">2004 general election</a>, the NDA was defeated. Congress emerged as the largest single party and formed a coalition, the <a href="/wiki/United_Progressive_Alliance" title="United Progressive Alliance">United Progressive Alliance</a> (UPA). <a href="/wiki/Manmohan_Singh" title="Manmohan Singh">Manmohan Singh</a>, a Sikh, who was proposed by Congress President <a href="/wiki/Sonia_Gandhi" title="Sonia Gandhi">Sonia Gandhi</a> to be parliamentary leader, served as prime minister of the UPA government, but with some external support.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-248" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-248"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>v<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The UPA returned to power in the <a href="/wiki/2009_Indian_general_election" title="2009 Indian general election">2009 general election</a> with increased numbers, no longer dependent on external support.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEKulkeRothermund2004384&#95;249-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKulkeRothermund2004384-249"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>227<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Singh became the first prime minister to be re-elected after Jawaharlal Nehru in 1962.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEBusiness&#95;Standard2009&#95;250-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBusiness_Standard2009-250"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>228<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In the <a href="/wiki/2014_Indian_general_election" title="2014 Indian general election">2014 general election</a>, the BJP under <a href="/wiki/Narendra_Modi" title="Narendra Modi">Narendra Modi</a> became the first political party since 1984 to win an absolute majority.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-251" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-251"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>229<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The party won a larger majority in the <a href="/wiki/2019_Indian_general_election" title="2019 Indian general election">2019 general election</a>. After losing its majority in the <a href="/wiki/2024_Indian_general_election" title="2024 Indian general election">2024 general election</a>, the BJP formed a coalition government with its NDA partners. Modi is the longest-serving non-Congress prime minister.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-252" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-252"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>230<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Government">Government</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main articles: <a href="/wiki/Government_of_India" title="Government of India">Government of India</a>, <a href="/wiki/State_governments_of_India" title="State governments of India">State governments of India</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Local_government_in_India" title="Local government in India">Local government in India</a></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">See also: <a href="/wiki/Constitution_of_India" title="Constitution of India">Constitution of India</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size mw-halign-right" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Constitution_of_India.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4d/Constitution_of_India.jpg/250px-Constitution_of_India.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="335" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4d/Constitution_of_India.jpg/500px-Constitution_of_India.jpg 1.5x" data-file-width="2985" data-file-height="4000" /></a><figcaption>The original preamble of the <a href="/wiki/Constitution_of_India" title="Constitution of India">Constitution of India</a> in 1950. In 1976, during the tenure of Indian prime minister <a href="/wiki/Indira_Gandhi" title="Indira Gandhi">Indira Gandhi</a>, the first sentence was changed to "Sovereign Socialist Secular Democratic Republic".</figcaption></figure>
<figure class="mw-default-size mw-halign-right" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Rashtrapati_Bhavan-Delhi-India4445.JPG" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f4/Rashtrapati_Bhavan-Delhi-India4445.JPG/250px-Rashtrapati_Bhavan-Delhi-India4445.JPG" decoding="async" width="250" height="178" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f4/Rashtrapati_Bhavan-Delhi-India4445.JPG/500px-Rashtrapati_Bhavan-Delhi-India4445.JPG 1.5x" data-file-width="3394" data-file-height="2410" /></a><figcaption>The <a href="/wiki/Rashtrapati_Bhavan,_New_Delhi" class="mw-redirect" title="Rashtrapati Bhavan, New Delhi">Rashtrapati Bhavan</a>, the official residence of the <a href="/wiki/President_of_India" title="President of India">President of India</a>, was designed by British architects <a href="/wiki/Edwin_Lutyens" title="Edwin Lutyens">Edwin Lutyens</a> and <a href="/wiki/Herbert_Baker" title="Herbert Baker">Herbert Baker</a> for the <a href="/wiki/Viceroy_of_India" class="mw-redirect" title="Viceroy of India">Viceroy of India</a>, and constructed between 1911 and 1931 during the <a href="/wiki/British_Raj" title="British Raj">British Raj</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Bremner2016&#95;253-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Bremner2016-253"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>231<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></figcaption></figure>
<p>The Constitution of India was drafted by the <a href="/wiki/Constituent_Assembly_of_India" title="Constituent Assembly of India">Constituent Assembly of India</a> with uncommon speed and absence of irregularities between 1946 and 1949.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTESpear1990242&#95;254-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpear1990242-254"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>232<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The <a href="/wiki/Government_of_India_Act_1935" title="Government of India Act 1935">Government of India Act 1935</a> was used as a model and framework.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTESpear1990242&#95;254-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpear1990242-254"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>232<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Long passages from the Act were included. The constitution describes a federal state with a parliamentary system of democracy.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTESpear1990242&#95;254-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpear1990242-254"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>232<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The federal structure was conspicuous for the strength of the central government, which exclusively exercised control of defence, foreign affairs, railways, ports, and currency.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTESpear1990242&#95;254-3" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpear1990242-254"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>232<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The President, the constitutional head of state, has reserve powers for taking over the administration of a state.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTESpear1990242&#95;254-4" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpear1990242-254"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>232<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The central legislature has two houses: the <i><a href="/wiki/Lok_Sabha" title="Lok Sabha">Lok Sabha</a></i>, whose delegates are directly elected by the people in general elections every five years, and the <i><a href="/wiki/Rajya_Sabha" title="Rajya Sabha">Rajya Sabha</a></i>, whose members are nominated by the elected representatives in the states.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTESpear1990242&#95;254-5" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpear1990242-254"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>232<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> There are also features not to be found in the Act of 1935. The definition of fundamental rights is based on the <a href="/wiki/Constitution_of_the_United_States" title="Constitution of the United States">Constitution of the United States</a>, and the constitutional directives, or goals of endeavor, are based on the <a href="/wiki/Constitution_of_Ireland" title="Constitution of Ireland">Constitution of Ireland</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTESpear1990243&#95;255-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpear1990243-255"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>233<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> An Indian institution recommended by the constitution is the <i><a href="/wiki/Panchayati_raj_in_India" title="Panchayati raj in India">panchayat</a></i> or village committees.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTESpear1990243&#95;255-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpear1990243-255"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>233<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Untouchability" title="Untouchability">Untouchability</a> is illegal (Article 17) and caste distinctions are derecognized (Articles 15(2) and 16(2)).<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTESpear1990243&#95;255-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpear1990243-255"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>233<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The promulgation of the Indian constitution transformed India into a republic within the Commonwealth.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTESpear1990243&#95;255-3" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpear1990243-255"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>233<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The <a href="/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_India" title="Prime Minister of India">Prime Minister of India</a> is the <a href="/wiki/Head_of_government" title="Head of government">head of government</a> and exercises most <a href="/wiki/Executive_(government)" title="Executive (government)">executive power</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTESharma1950&#95;256-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTESharma1950-256"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>234<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Appointed by the president,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTESharma2007162&#95;257-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTESharma2007162-257"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>235<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> the prime minister is supported by the <a href="/wiki/Political_party" title="Political party">party</a> or <a href="/wiki/Political_alliance" class="mw-redirect" title="Political alliance">political alliance</a> with a majority of seats in the lower house of parliament.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTESharma1950&#95;256-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTESharma1950-256"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>234<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The executive of the Indian government consists of the president, the <a href="/wiki/Vice_President_of_India" title="Vice President of India">vice-president</a>, and the <a href="/wiki/Union_Council_of_Ministers" title="Union Council of Ministers">Union Council of Ministers</a>—with the <a href="/wiki/Cabinet_(government)" title="Cabinet (government)">cabinet</a> being its executive committee—headed by the prime minister. Any minister holding a portfolio must be a member of one of the houses of parliament.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTESharma200731&#95;258-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTESharma200731-258"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>236<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In the Indian parliamentary system, the executive is subordinate to the legislature; the prime minister and their council are directly responsible to the lower house of the parliament. <a href="/wiki/Civil_Services_of_India" title="Civil Services of India">Civil servants</a> act as permanent executives and all decisions of the <a href="/wiki/Executive_(government)" title="Executive (government)">executive</a> are implemented by them.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEMathew2003524&#95;259-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMathew2003524-259"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>237<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>India has a three-tier<span class="nowrap">&#160;</span>unitary <a href="/wiki/Judicial_independence" title="Judicial independence">independent judiciary</a><sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTENeuborne2003478&#95;260-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTENeuborne2003478-260"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>238<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> comprising the <a href="/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_India" title="Supreme Court of India">supreme court</a>, headed by the <a href="/wiki/Chief_Justice_of_India" title="Chief Justice of India">Chief Justice of India</a>, 25<span class="nowrap">&#160;</span><a href="/wiki/High_courts_of_India" title="High courts of India">high courts</a>, and a large number of trial courts.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTENeuborne2003478&#95;260-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTENeuborne2003478-260"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>238<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The supreme court has <a href="/wiki/Original_jurisdiction" title="Original jurisdiction">original jurisdiction</a> over cases involving <a href="/wiki/Fundamental_rights_in_India" title="Fundamental rights in India">fundamental rights</a> and over disputes between states and the centre and has <a href="/wiki/Appellate_jurisdiction" class="mw-redirect" title="Appellate jurisdiction">appellate jurisdiction</a> over the high courts.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTESharma2007238,&#95;255&#95;261-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTESharma2007238,_255-261"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>239<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> It has the power to both strike down union or state laws which contravene the constitution<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTESripati1998423–424&#95;262-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTESripati1998423–424-262"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>240<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> and invalidate any government action it deems unconstitutional.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEPylee2003b314&#95;263-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPylee2003b314-263"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>241<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>

coords="115,119,116,120,117,119,118,118,118,116,119,114,119,113,118,112,117,110,119,108,121,108,123,105,124,105,125,105,126,105,127,105,124,103,122,101,123,101,123,100,121,99,123,98,125,98,125,98,124,97,124,96,122,96,122,95,121,93,120,92,119,92,117,93,116,92,113,92,113,91,113,90,111,90,108,89,108,89,105,87,104,86,102,85,99,84,98,83,98,83,96,82,96,83,93,81,92,82,89,80,89,79,88,79,86,78,88,77,84,74,82,75,81,76,81,74,79,75,79,73,81,71,79,70,76,73,75,75,74,77,75,80,75,81,76,84,76,84,77,86,77,88,76,90,77,92,78,93,79,94,78,95,79,96,77,96,77,97,77,97,78,97,79,97,80,96,81,96,83,96,83,97,85,97,87,97,88,98,89,100,88,101,87,104,86,105,84,106,84,107,83,108,83,109,83,110,83,111,82,111,83,113,83,114,83,115,83,116,84,115,85,116,86,117,87,116,88,115,87,114,87,113,87,113,86,113,86,112,86,111,85,110,85,109,84,108,85,107,86,108,88,106,87,108,88,109,89,110,90,109,90,108,92,109,91,110,93,110,94,109,96,108,96,108,98,109,98,110,97,111,98,111,98,110,101,109,101,111,104,111,104,109,105,110,106,109,106,110,108,111,108,112,110,112,111,113,113,113,114,113,114,114,114,115,114,116,113,117,114,119" alt="Uttar Pradesh" title="Uttar Pradesh" /><area href="/wiki/Uttarakhand" shape="poly" coords="79,69,81,71,80,73,79,73,80,75,81,75,82,76,84,74,87,77,86,78,88,80,89,79,90,81,93,82,94,81,95,83,96,82,96,81,98,78,97,77,98,75,98,74,99,73,100,73,102,71,100,70,98,68,96,68,96,66,95,65,93,64,93,65,91,64,89,64,89,64,87,63,86,63,84,63,83,63,82,64,81,63,80,66,80,68,80,69" alt="Uttarakhand" title="Uttarakhand" /><area href="/wiki/Haryana" shape="poly" coords="73,65,74,65,74,66,74,69,73,68,73,69,72,70,72,71,72,71,71,70,70,71,70,71,70,72,69,73,68,73,67,73,64,74,64,74,63,75,63,76,63,74,62,73,61,72,59,72,59,72,58,72,58,73,58,73,59,74,58,75,58,76,59,77,60,77,61,78,63,78,64,80,64,81,65,83,66,84,68,86,68,88,68,89,69,89,69,88,70,87,70,86,71,87,72,88,73,86,74,87,74,89,74,91,75,90,77,89,77,89,77,88,78,87,77,85,76,84,75,84,74,83,73,83,74,82,74,81,76,81,75,80,75,78,75,77,75,76,76,74,76,73,77,72,78,71,79,70,78,69,76,68,76,68,76,66,74,65" alt="Haryana" title="Haryana" /><area href="/wiki/Punjab,_India" shape="poly" coords="73,64,73,66,73,66,74,67,74,68,74,69,73,68,73,69,72,70,72,71,72,71,71,70,70,71,70,71,70,73,69,73,68,74,67,73,65,74,64,73,63,75,62,74,63,73,62,72,61,72,59,72,59,73,57,72,55,71,54,71,54,70,54,69,54,68,55,67,57,65,60,63,59,62,59,61,60,60,59,58,60,57,61,56,64,56,64,55,65,54,67,52,68,53,66,55,66,56,67,56,68,57,68,58,69,59,69,61,69,61,71,60,72,61,72,62,72,64,73,64" alt="Punjab" title="Punjab" /><area href="/wiki/Himachal_Pradesh" shape="poly" coords="72,64,73,64,75,66,76,68,76,68,78,69,80,68,80,67,80,65,80,64,82,63,82,64,83,63,84,63,86,63,88,63,88,64,89,64,88,63,87,62,87,60,87,60,86,58,86,55,86,54,85,52,84,51,83,52,82,51,80,49,79,49,76,49,75,48,74,47,72,47,71,48,70,49,68,50,68,49,68,50,68,52,67,52,68,53,66,55,66,56,68,57,68,58,68,59,69,60,69,61,70,61,70,61,71,62,72,63" alt="Himachal Pradesh" title="Himachal Pradesh" /></map><figcaption>A clickable map of the 28 states and 8 union territories of India</figcaption></figure>
<p>India is a federal union comprising 28 <a href="/wiki/States_and_union_territories_of_India" title="States and union territories of India">states</a> and 8 <a href="/wiki/Union_territory" title="Union territory">union territories</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTELibrary&#95;of&#95;Congress2004&#95;19-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTELibrary_of_Congress2004-19"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>12<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> All states, as well as the union territories of <a href="/wiki/Jammu_and_Kashmir_(union_territory)" title="Jammu and Kashmir (union territory)">Jammu and Kashmir</a>, <a href="/wiki/Puducherry_(union_territory)" title="Puducherry (union territory)">Puducherry</a> and the <a href="/wiki/Delhi" title="Delhi">National Capital Territory of Delhi</a>, have elected legislatures and governments following the <a href="/wiki/Westminster_system" title="Westminster system">Westminster system</a>. The remaining five union territories are directly ruled by the central government through appointed administrators. In 1956, under the <a href="/wiki/States_Reorganisation_Act,_1956" title="States Reorganisation Act, 1956">States Reorganisation Act</a>, states were reorganised on a linguistic basis.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTESharma200749&#95;264-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTESharma200749-264"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>242<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> There are over a quarter of a million local government bodies at city, town, district, block and village levels.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-265" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-265"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>243<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>

<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Foreign_relations">Foreign relations</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Foreign_relations_of_India" title="Foreign relations of India">Foreign relations of India</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Jawaharlal_Nehru,_Nasser_and_Tito_at_the_Conference_of_Non-Aligned_Nations_held_in_Belgrade.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0a/Jawaharlal_Nehru%2C_Nasser_and_Tito_at_the_Conference_of_Non-Aligned_Nations_held_in_Belgrade.jpg/250px-Jawaharlal_Nehru%2C_Nasser_and_Tito_at_the_Conference_of_Non-Aligned_Nations_held_in_Belgrade.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="156" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0a/Jawaharlal_Nehru%2C_Nasser_and_Tito_at_the_Conference_of_Non-Aligned_Nations_held_in_Belgrade.jpg/500px-Jawaharlal_Nehru%2C_Nasser_and_Tito_at_the_Conference_of_Non-Aligned_Nations_held_in_Belgrade.jpg 1.5x" data-file-width="640" data-file-height="400" /></a><figcaption>In the 1950s and 60s, India played a pivotal role in the <a href="/wiki/Non-Aligned_Movement" title="Non-Aligned Movement">Non-Aligned Movement</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Dinkel2018&#95;266-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Dinkel2018-266"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>244<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> From left to right: <a href="/wiki/Gamal_Abdel_Nasser" title="Gamal Abdel Nasser">Gamal Abdel Nasser</a> of <a href="/wiki/United_Arab_Republic" title="United Arab Republic">United Arab Republic</a> (now Egypt), <a href="/wiki/Josip_Broz_Tito" title="Josip Broz Tito">Josip Broz Tito</a> of <a href="/wiki/Socialist_Federal_Republic_of_Yugoslavia" title="Socialist Federal Republic of Yugoslavia">Yugoslavia</a> and <a href="/wiki/Jawaharlal_Nehru" title="Jawaharlal Nehru">Jawaharlal Nehru</a> in Belgrade, September 1961.</figcaption></figure>
<p>India remained a member of the <a href="/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations" title="Commonwealth of Nations">Commonwealth of Nations</a> after becoming a republic in 1950.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-storm-india-commonwealth&#95;267-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-storm-india-commonwealth-267"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>245<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-268" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-268"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>246<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> It strongly supported decolonisation in Africa and Asia in the 1950s, and played a leading role in the Non-Aligned Movement.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTERothermund200048,&#95;227&#95;269-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTERothermund200048,_227-269"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>247<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> After cordial relations with China during the greater part of the 1950s, India and China went to <a href="/wiki/Sino-Indian_War" title="Sino-Indian War">war in 1962</a>; India was widely thought to have been decisively defeated.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-62-humiliation&#95;270-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-62-humiliation-270"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>248<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> By 1967, however, India was able to fend off <a href="/wiki/Sino-Indian_War_of_1967" class="mw-redirect" title="Sino-Indian War of 1967">Chinese excursions into Sikkim</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-271" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-271"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>249<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>India has had <a href="/wiki/Indo-Pakistani_relations" class="mw-redirect" title="Indo-Pakistani relations">uneasy relations</a> with its western neighbour, Pakistan. The two went to war in <a href="/wiki/Indo-Pakistani_War_of_1947" class="mw-redirect" title="Indo-Pakistani War of 1947">1947</a>, <a href="/wiki/Indo-Pakistani_War_of_1965" class="mw-redirect" title="Indo-Pakistani War of 1965">1965</a>, <a href="/wiki/Indo-Pakistani_War_of_1971" class="mw-redirect" title="Indo-Pakistani War of 1971">1971</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Kargil_War" title="Kargil War">1999</a>. Three wars were fought over the <a href="/wiki/Kashmir_conflict" title="Kashmir conflict">disputed territory of Kashmir</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEGilbert2002486–487&#95;272-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGilbert2002486–487-272"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>250<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> After the 1965 war, India began to pursue close military and economic <a href="/wiki/India-Soviet_Union_relations" class="mw-redirect" title="India-Soviet Union relations">ties with the Soviet Union</a>. By the late 1960s, the Soviet Union was India's largest arms supplier.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTESharma199956&#95;273-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTESharma199956-273"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>251<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:The_Prime_Minister,_Dr._Manmohan_Singh_with_the_President_of_France,_Mr._Nicolas_Sarkozy,_at_the_Bastille_Day_Parade_of_France,_in_Paris_on_July_14,_2009_(2).jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/15/The_Prime_Minister%2C_Dr._Manmohan_Singh_with_the_President_of_France%2C_Mr._Nicolas_Sarkozy%2C_at_the_Bastille_Day_Parade_of_France%2C_in_Paris_on_July_14%2C_2009_%282%29.jpg/250px-thumbnail.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="207" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/15/The_Prime_Minister%2C_Dr._Manmohan_Singh_with_the_President_of_France%2C_Mr._Nicolas_Sarkozy%2C_at_the_Bastille_Day_Parade_of_France%2C_in_Paris_on_July_14%2C_2009_%282%29.jpg/500px-thumbnail.jpg 1.5x" data-file-width="1900" data-file-height="1573" /></a><figcaption>Indian prime minister <a href="/wiki/Manmohan_Singh" title="Manmohan Singh">Manmohan Singh</a> and French president <a href="/wiki/Nicolas_Sarkozy" title="Nicolas Sarkozy">Nicolas Sarkozy</a> review the 221st <a href="/wiki/Bastille_Day_military_parade" title="Bastille Day military parade">Bastille Day military parade</a> in Paris, July 2009. India's oldest regiment, the <a href="/wiki/Maratha_Light_Infantry" title="Maratha Light Infantry">Maratha Light Infantry</a>, founded in 1768, led the parade.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-guardian-muir-diary-maratha&#95;274-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-guardian-muir-diary-maratha-274"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>252<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></figcaption></figure>
<p>China's <a href="/wiki/596_(nuclear_test)" class="mw-redirect" title="596 (nuclear test)">nuclear test of 1964</a> and threats to intervene in support of Pakistan in the 1965 war caused India to produce nuclear weapons.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEPerkovich200160–86,&#95;106–125&#95;275-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPerkovich200160–86,_106–125-275"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>253<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> India conducted its <a href="/wiki/Smiling_Buddha" title="Smiling Buddha">first nuclear weapons test</a> in 1974 and carried out <a href="/wiki/Pokhran-II" title="Pokhran-II">additional underground testing</a> in 1998. India has signed neither the <a href="/wiki/Comprehensive_Nuclear-Test-Ban_Treaty" title="Comprehensive Nuclear-Test-Ban Treaty">Comprehensive Nuclear-Test-Ban Treaty</a> nor the <a href="/wiki/Nuclear_Non-Proliferation_Treaty" class="mw-redirect" title="Nuclear Non-Proliferation Treaty">Nuclear Non-Proliferation Treaty</a>, considering both to be flawed and discriminatory.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEKumar2010&#95;276-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKumar2010-276"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>254<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> India maintains a "<a href="/wiki/No_first_use" title="No first use">no first use</a>" nuclear policy and is developing a <a href="/wiki/Nuclear_triad" title="Nuclear triad">nuclear triad</a> capability as a part of its "<a href="/wiki/Minimum_Credible_Deterrence" class="mw-redirect" title="Minimum Credible Deterrence">Minimum Credible Deterrence</a>" doctrine.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTENair2007&#95;277-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTENair2007-277"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>255<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEPandit2009&#95;278-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPandit2009-278"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>256<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Since the end of the <a href="/wiki/Cold_War" title="Cold War">Cold War</a>, India has increased its economic, strategic, and military cooperation with the <a href="/wiki/India%E2%80%93United_States_relations" title="India–United States relations">United States</a> and the <a href="/wiki/India%E2%80%93European_Union_relations" title="India–European Union relations">European Union</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEEuropean&#95;Union&#95;2008&#95;279-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEuropean_Union_2008-279"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>257<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In 2008, a <a href="/wiki/U.S.%E2%80%93India_Civil_Nuclear_Agreement" class="mw-redirect" title="U.S.–India Civil Nuclear Agreement">civilian nuclear agreement</a> was signed between India and the United States. Although India possessed nuclear weapons at the time and was not a party to the Nuclear Non-Proliferation Treaty, it received waivers from the <a href="/wiki/International_Atomic_Energy_Agency" title="International Atomic Energy Agency">International Atomic Energy Agency</a> and the <a href="/wiki/Nuclear_Suppliers_Group" title="Nuclear Suppliers Group">Nuclear Suppliers Group</a>, ending earlier restrictions on India's nuclear technology and commerce; India subsequently signed co-operation agreements involving <a href="/wiki/Nuclear_power_in_India" title="Nuclear power in India">civilian nuclear energy</a> with Russia,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEBritish&#95;Broadcasting&#95;Corporation&#95;2009&#95;280-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBritish_Broadcasting_Corporation_2009-280"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>258<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> France,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTERediff&#95;2008&#95;a&#95;281-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTERediff_2008_a-281"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>259<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> the <a href="/wiki/India%E2%80%93United_Kingdom_relations" title="India–United Kingdom relations">United Kingdom</a>,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEReuters2010&#95;282-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReuters2010-282"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>260<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> and <a href="/wiki/Canada%E2%80%93India_relations" title="Canada–India relations">Canada</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTECurry2010&#95;283-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTECurry2010-283"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>261<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Economy">Economy</h2></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Economy_of_India" title="Economy of India">Economy of India</a></div>
<p>According to the <a href="/wiki/International_Monetary_Fund" title="International Monetary Fund">International Monetary Fund</a>, the Indian economy in 2024 was nominally worth $3.94&#160;trillion; it is the <a href="/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(nominal)" title="List of countries by GDP (nominal)">fifth-largest economy</a> by market exchange rates and, at around $15.0&#160;trillion, the <a href="/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(PPP)" title="List of countries by GDP (PPP)">third-largest</a> by <a href="/wiki/Purchasing_power_parity" title="Purchasing power parity">purchasing power parity</a> (PPP).<sup id="cite&#95;ref-IMFWEO.IN&#95;24-4" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-IMFWEO.IN-24"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>16<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> With an average annual GDP growth rate of 5.8% over the past two decades, and reaching 6.1% during 2011–2012,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEInternational&#95;Monetary&#95;Fund&#95;20112&#95;284-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEInternational_Monetary_Fund_20112-284"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>262<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> India is one of the <a href="/wiki/List_of_countries_by_real_GDP_growth_rate" title="List of countries by real GDP growth rate">world's fastest-growing economies</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTENayakGoldarAgrawal2010xxv&#95;285-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTENayakGoldarAgrawal2010xxv-285"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>263<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> However, it has a low per capita GDP, ranking <a href="/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(nominal)_per_capita" title="List of countries by GDP (nominal) per capita">144th</a> in the world in nominal terms and <a href="/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(PPP)_per_capita" title="List of countries by GDP (PPP) per capita">123rd</a> adjusted for PPP.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-IMFWEO.IN&#95;24-5" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-IMFWEO.IN-24"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>16<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The vast majority of Indians fall into the global low-income group based on average daily income.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-286" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-286"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>264<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Until 1991, all Indian governments followed <a href="/wiki/Protectionism" title="Protectionism">protectionist</a> policies that were influenced by socialist economics. Widespread <a href="/wiki/Licence_Raj" title="Licence Raj">state intervention and regulation</a> largely walled the economy off from the outside world. An acute <a href="/wiki/1991_Indian_economic_crisis" title="1991 Indian economic crisis">balance of payments crisis in 1991</a> forced the nation to <a href="/wiki/Economic_liberalisation_in_India" title="Economic liberalisation in India">liberalise its economy</a>;<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEWolpert2003xiv&#95;287-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWolpert2003xiv-287"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>265<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> since then, it has moved increasingly towards a free-market system<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEOrganisation&#95;for&#95;Economic&#95;Co-operation&#95;and&#95;Development&#95;2007&#95;288-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOrganisation_for_Economic_Co-operation_and_Development_2007-288"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>266<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEGargan1992&#95;289-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGargan1992-289"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>267<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> by emphasising both foreign trade and direct investment inflows.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEAlamgir200823,&#95;97&#95;290-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAlamgir200823,_97-290"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>268<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> India has been a member of <a href="/wiki/World_Trade_Organization" title="World Trade Organization">World Trade Organization</a> since 1 January 1995.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEWorld&#95;Trade&#95;Organization&#95;1995&#95;291-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWorld_Trade_Organization_1995-291"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>269<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The 522-million-worker <a href="/wiki/Labour_in_India" title="Labour in India">Indian labour force</a> is the <a href="/wiki/List_of_countries_by_labour_force" title="List of countries by labour force">world's second largest</a>, as of 2017<sup class="plainlinks noexcerpt noprint asof-tag update" style="display:none;"><a class="external text" href="https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=India&amp;action=edit">&#91;update&#93;</a></sup>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTECentral&#95;Intelligence&#95;Agency&#95;292-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTECentral_Intelligence_Agency-292"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>270<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The service sector makes up 55.6% of GDP, the industrial sector 26.3% and the agricultural sector 18.1%. India's <a href="/wiki/Remittance" title="Remittance">foreign exchange remittances</a> of US$100 billion in 2022,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-293" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-293"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>271<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> the highest in the world, were contributed to its economy by 32 million Indians working in foreign countries.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-294" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-294"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>272<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In 2006, the share of external trade in India's GDP stood at 24%, up from 6% in 1985.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEOrganisation&#95;for&#95;Economic&#95;Co-operation&#95;and&#95;Development&#95;2007&#95;288-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOrganisation_for_Economic_Co-operation_and_Development_2007-288"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>266<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In 2008, India's share of world trade was 1.7%;<sup id="cite&#95;ref-295" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-295"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>273<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> in 2021, India was the <a href="/wiki/List_of_countries_by_imports" title="List of countries by imports">world's ninth-largest importer</a> and the <a href="/wiki/List_of_countries_by_exports" title="List of countries by exports">sixteenth-largest exporter</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-296" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-296"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>274<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Between 2001 and 2011, the contribution of petrochemical and engineering goods to total exports grew from 14% to 42%.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEEconomist&#95;2011&#95;297-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEconomist_2011-297"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>275<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> India was the <a href="/wiki/List_of_countries_by_textile_exports" title="List of countries by textile exports">world's second-largest textile exporter</a> after <a href="/wiki/China" title="China">China</a> in the 2013 calendar year.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEEconomic&#95;Times&#95;2014&#95;298-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEconomic_Times_2014-298"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>276<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Averaging an economic growth rate of 7.5% for several years before 2007,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEOrganisation&#95;for&#95;Economic&#95;Co-operation&#95;and&#95;Development&#95;2007&#95;288-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOrganisation_for_Economic_Co-operation_and_Development_2007-288"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>266<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> India has more than doubled its hourly wage rates during the first decade of the 21st century.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEBonner2010&#95;299-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBonner2010-299"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>277<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Some 431 million Indians have left poverty since 1985; India's middle classes are projected to number around 580&#160;million by 2030.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEFarrellBeinhocker2007&#95;300-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFarrellBeinhocker2007-300"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>278<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In 2024, India's consumer market was the <a href="/wiki/List_of_largest_consumer_markets" title="List of largest consumer markets">world's third largest</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-301" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-301"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>279<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> India's nominal <a href="/wiki/GDP_per_capita" class="mw-redirect" title="GDP per capita">GDP per capita</a> increased steadily from US$308 in 1991, when economic liberalisation began, to US$1,380 in 2010, to an estimated US$2,731 in 2024. It is expected to grow to US$3,264 by 2026.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-IMFWEO.IN&#95;24-6" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-IMFWEO.IN-24"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>16<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<ul class="gallery mw-gallery-packed">

			<div class="gallerytext">In 2019, 43% of India's total workforce was employed in agriculture.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-worldbank-ilo-total-agriculture&#95;302-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-worldbank-ilo-total-agriculture-302"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>280<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></div>

			<div class="gallerytext">India is the <a href="/wiki/List_of_countries_by_milk_production" title="List of countries by milk production">world's largest producer of milk</a>, with the largest population of cattle. In 2018, nearly 80% of India's milk was sourced from small farms with herd size between one and two, the milk harvested by hand milking.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-milk-sourcing&#95;304-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-milk-sourcing-304"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>282<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></div>
		</li>
