<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Industries">Industries</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main articles: <a href="/wiki/Industry_in_India" class="mw-redirect" title="Industry in India">Industry in India</a> and <a href="/wiki/Energy_in_India" title="Energy in India">Energy in India</a></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">See also: <a href="/wiki/Energy_policy_of_India" title="Energy policy of India">Energy policy of India</a></div>
<p>The <a href="/wiki/Automotive_industry_in_India" title="Automotive industry in India">Indian automotive industry</a>, the world's second-fastest growing, increased domestic sales by 26% during 2009–2010,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEBusiness&#95;Line&#95;2010&#95;305-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBusiness_Line_2010-305"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>283<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> and exports by 36% during 2008–2009.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEExpress&#95;India&#95;2009&#95;306-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEExpress_India_2009-306"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>284<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In 2022, India became the world's third-largest vehicle market after China and the United States, surpassing Japan.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-307" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-307"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>285<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> At the end of 2011, the <a href="/wiki/Information_technology_in_India" title="Information technology in India">Indian IT industry</a> employed 2.8&#160;million professionals, generated revenues close to US$100&#160;billion equalling 7.5% of Indian GDP, and contributed 26% of India's merchandise exports.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTENasscom&#95;2011–2012&#95;308-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTENasscom_2011–2012-308"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>286<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The <a href="/wiki/Pharmaceutical_industry_in_India" title="Pharmaceutical industry in India">pharmaceutical industry in India</a> includes 3,000 pharmaceutical companies and 10,500 manufacturing units; India is the world's third-largest pharmaceutical producer, largest producer of generic medicines, and supplies up to 50–60% of global vaccines demand, contributing up to <span style="white-space: nowrap">US$</span>24.44&#160;billion in exports. India's local pharmaceutical market is estimated up to <span style="white-space: nowrap">US$</span>42&#160;billion.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Phamra1&#95;309-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Phamra1-309"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>287<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-Pharma2&#95;310-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Pharma2-310"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>288<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> India is among the top 12 biotech destinations in the world.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-311" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-311"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>289<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEYep2011&#95;312-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYep2011-312"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>290<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The Indian biotech industry grew by 15.1% in 2012–2013, increasing its revenues from <span style="white-space: nowrap">₹</span>204.4&#160;billion (<a href="/wiki/Indian_rupee" title="Indian rupee">Indian rupees</a>) to <span style="white-space: nowrap">₹</span>235.24&#160;billion (US$3.94&#160;billion at June 2013 exchange rates).<sup id="cite&#95;ref-313" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-313"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>291<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>India's capacity to generate electrical power is 300 gigawatts, of which 42 gigawatts is renewable.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Par&#95;314-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Par-314"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>292<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Coal_in_India" title="Coal in India">The country's usage of coal</a> is a major cause of <a href="/wiki/Climate_change_in_India#Greenhouse_gas_emissions" title="Climate change in India">India's greenhouse gas emissions</a>, but <a href="/wiki/Renewable_energy_in_India" title="Renewable energy in India">its renewable energy</a> is growing.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-315" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-315"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>293<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> India emits about 7% of global greenhouse gas emissions. This equates to about 2.5 tons of <a href="/wiki/Carbon_dioxide" title="Carbon dioxide">carbon dioxide</a> per person per year, which is half the world average.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-USAID2018&#95;316-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-USAID2018-316"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>294<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-UNEP2019&#95;317-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-UNEP2019-317"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>295<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Increasing <a href="/wiki/Electrification" title="Electrification">access to electricity</a> and <a href="/wiki/Clean_cooking" class="mw-redirect" title="Clean cooking">clean cooking</a> with <a href="/wiki/Liquefied_petroleum_gas" title="Liquefied petroleum gas">liquefied petroleum gas</a> have been priorities for energy in India.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-318" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-318"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>296<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<ul class="gallery mw-gallery-packed">
		<li class="gallerybox" style="width: 358px">
			<div class="thumb" style="width: 356px;"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:Panoramic_view_of_Taj_Palace_Hotel_and_Taj_Tower_with_the_iconic_Gateway_of_India_in_the_background.jpg" class="mw-file-description" title="Mumbai, the centre of India&#39;s tertiary sector finance industry, also contributes to tourism. Shown here are the Taj Mahal Palace Hotel to the left and the Gateway of India."><img alt="Mumbai, the centre of India&#39;s tertiary sector finance industry, also contributes to tourism. Shown here are the Taj Mahal Palace Hotel to the left and the Gateway of India." src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/Panoramic_view_of_Taj_Palace_Hotel_and_Taj_Tower_with_the_iconic_Gateway_of_India_in_the_background.jpg/960px-Panoramic_view_of_Taj_Palace_Hotel_and_Taj_Tower_with_the_iconic_Gateway_of_India_in_the_background.jpg" decoding="async" width="356" height="200" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/Panoramic_view_of_Taj_Palace_Hotel_and_Taj_Tower_with_the_iconic_Gateway_of_India_in_the_background.jpg/1280px-Panoramic_view_of_Taj_Palace_Hotel_and_Taj_Tower_with_the_iconic_Gateway_of_India_in_the_background.jpg 2x" data-file-width="5515" data-file-height="3102" /></a></span></div>
			<div class="gallerytext"><a href="/wiki/Mumbai" title="Mumbai">Mumbai</a>, the centre of India's <a href="/wiki/Tertiary_sector" title="Tertiary sector">tertiary sector</a> <a href="/wiki/Finance_industry" class="mw-redirect" title="Finance industry">finance industry</a>, also contributes to <a href="/wiki/Tourism_in_India" title="Tourism in India">tourism</a>. Shown here are the <a href="/wiki/Taj_Mahal_Palace_Hotel" title="Taj Mahal Palace Hotel">Taj Mahal Palace Hotel</a> to the left and the <a href="/wiki/Gateway_of_India" title="Gateway of India">Gateway of India</a>.</div>
		</li>
		<li class="gallerybox" style="width: 324.66666666667px">
			<div class="thumb" style="width: 322.66666666667px;"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:Cherry_Resort_inside_Temi_Tea_Garden,_Namchi,_Sikkim.jpg" class="mw-file-description" title="A tea garden in Sikkim. India, the world&#39;s second-largest producer of tea, is a nation of one billion tea drinkers, who consume 70% of India&#39;s tea output."><img alt="A tea garden in Sikkim. 			<div class="gallerytext">A tea garden in Sikkim. India, the <a href="/wiki/List_of_countries_by_tea_production" class="mw-redirect" title="List of countries by tea production">world's second-largest producer of tea</a>, is a nation of one billion tea drinkers, who consume 70% of India's tea output.</div>
		</li>
		<li class="gallerybox" style="width: 882px">
			<div class="thumb" style="width: 880px;"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:Bangalore_Panorama_edit1.jpg" class="mw-file-description" title="A panorama of Bangalore, the centre of India&#39;s software development economy. In the 1980s, when the first multinational corporations began to set up centres in India, they chose Bangalore because of the large pool of skilled graduates in the area, in turn due to the many science and engineering colleges in the surrounding region.[297]"><img alt="A panorama of Bangalore, the centre of India&#39;s software development economy. In the 1980s, when the first multinational corporations began to set up centres in India, they chose Bangalore because of the large pool of skilled graduates in the area, in turn due to the many science and engineering colleges in the surrounding region.[297]" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/38/Bangalore_Panorama_edit1.jpg/1920px-Bangalore_Panorama_edit1.jpg" decoding="async" width="880" height="200" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/38/Bangalore_Panorama_edit1.jpg/3840px-Bangalore_Panorama_edit1.jpg 1.5x" data-file-width="7715" data-file-height="1754" /></a></span></div>
			<div class="gallerytext">A panorama of <a href="/wiki/Bangalore" class="mw-redirect" title="Bangalore">Bangalore</a>, the centre of India's software development economy. In the 1980s, when the first <a href="/wiki/Multinational_corporation" title="Multinational corporation">multinational corporations</a> began to set up centres in India, they chose Bangalore because of the large pool of skilled graduates in the area, in turn due to the many science and engineering colleges in the surrounding region.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-ScottGarofoli2007&#95;319-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-ScottGarofoli2007-319"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>297<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></div>
		</li>
		<li class="gallerybox" style="width: 302px">
			<div class="thumb" style="width: 300px;"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:Chinese_Fishing_Net_Raising_Birds_Sunrise_Ashtamudi_Kollam_Mar22_A7C_01784.jpg" class="mw-file-description" title="A Chinese fishing net being raised out of the water in Kochi. Fishing, which contributes 1.07% to India&#39;s total GDP,[298] supports the livelihood of over 28 million people, especially within marginalized and vulnerable communities.[299] India is the third-largest fish producing country in the world, accounting for 7.96% of global production.[298]"><img alt="A Chinese fishing net being raised out of the water in Kochi. Fishing, which contributes 1.07% to India&#39;s total GDP,[298] supports the livelihood of over 28 million people, especially within marginalized and vulnerable communities.[299] India is the third-largest fish producing country in the world, accounting for 7.96% of global production.[298]" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/19/Chinese_Fishing_Net_Raising_Birds_Sunrise_Ashtamudi_Kollam_Mar22_A7C_01784.jpg/500px-Chinese_Fishing_Net_Raising_Birds_Sunrise_Ashtamudi_Kollam_Mar22_A7C_01784.jpg" decoding="async" width="300" height="200" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/19/Chinese_Fishing_Net_Raising_Birds_Sunrise_Ashtamudi_Kollam_Mar22_A7C_01784.jpg/960px-Chinese_Fishing_Net_Raising_Birds_Sunrise_Ashtamudi_Kollam_Mar22_A7C_01784.jpg 1.5x" data-file-width="5908" data-file-height="3939" /></a></span></div>
			<div class="gallerytext">A <a href="/wiki/Chinese_fishing_nets" title="Chinese fishing nets">Chinese fishing net</a> being raised out of the water in <a href="/wiki/Kochi" title="Kochi">Kochi</a>. Fishing, which contributes 1.07% to India's total <a href="/wiki/Gross_domestic_product" title="Gross domestic product">GDP</a>,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-:2&#95;320-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:2-320"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>298<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> supports the livelihood of over 28 million people, especially within marginalized and vulnerable communities.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-321" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-321"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>299<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> India is the third-largest fish producing country in the world, accounting for 7.96% of global production.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-:2&#95;320-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:2-320"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>298<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></div>
		</li>
</ul>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Demographics">Demographics</h2></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Demographics_of_India" title="Demographics of India">Demographics of India</a></div>
<p>With an estimated 1,428,627,663 residents in 2023, India is the world's most populous country.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-WPP&#95;UN&#95;21-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-WPP_UN-21"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>13<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> 1,210,193,422 residents were reported in the <a href="/wiki/2011_Census_of_India" class="mw-redirect" title="2011 Census of India">2011 provisional census report</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEProvisional&#95;Population&#95;Totals&#95;Paper&#95;1&#95;of&#95;2011&#95;India160&#95;322-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEProvisional_Population_Totals_Paper_1_of_2011_India160-322"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>300<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The median age was 28.7 in 2020.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTECentral&#95;Intelligence&#95;Agency&#95;292-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTECentral_Intelligence_Agency-292"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>270<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Medical advances made in the last 50 years as well as increased agricultural productivity brought about by the "<a href="/wiki/Green_Revolution_in_India" title="Green Revolution in India">Green Revolution</a>" have caused India's population to grow rapidly,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTERorabacher201035–39&#95;323-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTERorabacher201035–39-323"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>301<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> though India's decennial rates of growth are decreasing: its population grew by 17.64% from 2001 to 2011,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEProvisional&#95;Population&#95;Totals&#95;Paper&#95;1&#95;of&#95;2011&#95;India165&#95;324-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEProvisional_Population_Totals_Paper_1_of_2011_India165-324"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>302<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> compared to 21.54% growth in the previous decade (1991–2001).<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEProvisional&#95;Population&#95;Totals&#95;Paper&#95;1&#95;of&#95;2011&#95;India165&#95;324-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEProvisional_Population_Totals_Paper_1_of_2011_India165-324"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>302<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The first post-colonial census, conducted in 1951, counted 361&#160;million people.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-325" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-325"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>303<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The <a href="/wiki/Life_expectancy" title="Life expectancy">life expectancy</a> at birth has increased from 49.7 years in 1970–1975 to 72.0 years in 2023.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-:5&#95;326-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:5-326"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>304<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-327" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-327"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>305<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The under-five <a href="/wiki/Infant_mortality" title="Infant mortality">mortality</a> rate for the country was 113 per 1,000 live births in 1994 whereas in 2018 it reduced to 41.1 per 1,000 live births.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-:5&#95;326-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:5-326"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>304<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The human sex ratio, according to the 2011 census, is 940 females per 1,000 males.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEProvisional&#95;Population&#95;Totals&#95;Paper&#95;1&#95;of&#95;2011&#95;India160&#95;322-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEProvisional_Population_Totals_Paper_1_of_2011_India160-322"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>300<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Female_infanticide_in_India" title="Female infanticide in India">Female infanticide in India</a>, and lately <a href="/wiki/Female_foeticide_in_India" title="Female foeticide in India">female foeticide</a>, have created lop-sided gender ratios; the number of <a href="/wiki/Missing_women" title="Missing women">missing women</a> in the country quadrupled from 15 million to 63&#160;million during the period 1964–2014, faster than the population growth during the same period.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-The&#95;Hindu&#95;November&#95;17&#95;2019c&#95;328-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-The_Hindu_November_17_2019c-328"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>306<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> According to an Indian government study, an additional 21 million girls are unwanted and do not receive adequate care.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-The&#95;Guardian&#95;November&#95;17&#95;2019c&#95;329-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-The_Guardian_November_17_2019c-329"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>307<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Despite a government ban on sex-selective foeticide, the practice has far from stopped.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Foreign&#95;Policy&#95;November&#95;17&#95;2019c&#95;330-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Foreign_Policy_November_17_2019c-330"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>308<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Migration from rural to urban areas has been an important dynamic in India's recent history. The number of people living in urban areas grew by 31.2% between 1991 and 2001.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEGarg2005&#95;331-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGarg2005-331"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>309<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In 2001, over 70% lived in rural areas.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEDysonVisaria2005115–129&#95;332-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDysonVisaria2005115–129-332"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>310<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTERatna2007271–272&#95;333-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTERatna2007271–272-333"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>311<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The level of urbanisation increased further from 27.81% in the 2001 census to 31.16% in the 2011 census. The slowing down of the overall population growth rate was due to the sharp decline in the growth rate in rural areas since 1991.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEChandramouli2011&#95;334-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChandramouli2011-334"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>312<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In the 2011 census, there were 53 <a href="/wiki/List_of_million-plus_urban_agglomerations_in_India" title="List of million-plus urban agglomerations in India">million-plus urban agglomerations in India</a>. Among them <a href="/wiki/Mumbai" title="Mumbai">Mumbai</a>, <a href="/wiki/Delhi" title="Delhi">Delhi</a>, <a href="/wiki/Kolkata" title="Kolkata">Kolkata</a>, <a href="/wiki/Chennai" title="Chennai">Chennai</a>, <a href="/wiki/Bangalore" class="mw-redirect" title="Bangalore">Bengaluru</a>, <a href="/wiki/Hyderabad" title="Hyderabad">Hyderabad</a> and <a href="/wiki/Ahmedabad" title="Ahmedabad">Ahmedabad</a>, in decreasing order by population.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-censusindia&#95;2011&#95;335-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-censusindia_2011-335"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>313<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> 
</p>
<ul class="gallery mw-gallery-packed">
		<li class="gallerybox" style="width: 248px">
			<div class="thumb" style="width: 246px;"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:Life-expectancy,_1881_to_2023,_IND.svg" class="mw-file-description" title="The historical development of life expectancy in India from 1881 to 2023"><img alt="The historical development of life expectancy in India from 1881 to 2023" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d7/Life-expectancy%2C_1881_to_2023%2C_IND.svg/500px-Life-expectancy%2C_1881_to_2023%2C_IND.svg.png" decoding="async" width="246" height="200" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d7/Life-expectancy%2C_1881_to_2023%2C_IND.svg/960px-Life-expectancy%2C_1881_to_2023%2C_IND.svg.png 1.5x" data-file-width="850" data-file-height="691" /></a></span></div>
			<div class="gallerytext"> The historical development of life expectancy in India from 1881 to 2023</div>
		</li>
		<li class="gallerybox" style="width: 285.33333333333px">
			<div class="thumb" style="width: 283.33333333333px;"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:Child-mortality_rate_in_india.png" class="mw-file-description" title="The child mortality rate in India from 1960 to 2023"><img alt="The child mortality rate in India from 1960 to 2023" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b3/Child-mortality_rate_in_india.png/500px-Child-mortality_rate_in_india.png" decoding="async" width="284" height="200" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b3/Child-mortality_rate_in_india.png/960px-Child-mortality_rate_in_india.png 1.5x" data-file-width="3400" data-file-height="2400" /></a></span></div>
			<div class="gallerytext">The child mortality rate in India from 1960 to 2023</div>
		</li>
</ul>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Languages">Languages</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Languages_of_India" title="Languages of India">Languages of India</a></div>
<p>Languages of India belong to several <a href="/wiki/Language_family" title="Language family">language families</a>. The <a href="/wiki/2011_Census_of_India" class="mw-redirect" title="2011 Census of India">2011 Census of India</a>, the last conducted by the Indian government, gives the following breakdown:<sup id="cite&#95;ref-jolad-agarwal&#95;336-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-jolad-agarwal-336"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>314<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<table class="wikitable" style="margin-right:auto; margin-left:auto;">
<tbody><tr>
<th style="background:#ddd;" colspan="6">Language families and speakers in India<sup id="cite&#95;ref-jolad-agarwal&#95;336-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-jolad-agarwal-336"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>314<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</th></tr>
<tr>

</td></tr>
<tr style="text-align:center;">
<td>5</td>
<td style="text-align:center;"><a href="/wiki/Afroasiatic_languages" title="Afroasiatic languages">Semito-Hamitic</a></td>
<td style="text-align:center;"></td>
<td style="text-align:center;">1</td>
<td style="text-align:center;">54,947</td>
<td style="text-align:center;">0%
</td></tr></tbody></table>
<p>There are also small numbers of speakers of <a href="/wiki/Kra%E2%80%93Dai_languages" title="Kra–Dai languages">Tai–Kadai</a>, <a href="/wiki/Andamanese_languages" title="Andamanese languages">Andamanese</a>, and minor language families and <a href="/wiki/Language_isolate" title="Language isolate">isolates</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Moseley2008&#95;338-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Moseley2008-338"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>315<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup class="reference nowrap"><span title="Page / location: 283">&#58;&#8202;283&#8202;</span></sup>
</p><p>The official language of India's federal government was chosen by the <a href="/wiki/Constituent_Assembly_of_India" title="Constituent Assembly of India">Constituent Assembly of India</a> in September 1949 after three years of debate between two opposing camps. <a href="/wiki/Hindi_language" class="mw-redirect" title="Hindi language">Hindi language</a> protagonists wanted Hindi in the <a href="/wiki/Devanagari_script" class="mw-redirect" title="Devanagari script">Devanagari script</a> to be the sole "national language" of India whereas delegates from South India preferred English to have a place in the Constitution.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-lerner-Hindi&#95;339-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-lerner-Hindi-339"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>316<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-isaka-Hindi&#95;340-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-isaka-Hindi-340"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>317<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The compromise reached declared (i) Hindi to be the "official language" of India's federal government; (ii) English to be an associate official language for 15 years during which Hindi's formal lexicon would be developed; and (iii) the international form of <a href="/wiki/Hindu%E2%80%93Arabic_numeral_system" title="Hindu–Arabic numeral system">Hindu–Arabic numerals</a> to be the official numerals.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-lerner-Hindi&#95;339-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-lerner-Hindi-339"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>316<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-isaka-Hindi&#95;340-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-isaka-Hindi-340"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>317<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The compromise resolution became articles 343–351 of India's constitution, which came into effect on 26 January 1950.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-lerner-Hindi&#95;339-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-lerner-Hindi-339"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>316<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-isaka-Hindi&#95;340-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-isaka-Hindi-340"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>317<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In 1965, when the 15 years were up, the Government of India announced that English would continue to be the "de facto formal language of India".<sup id="cite&#95;ref-lerner-Hindi&#95;339-3" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-lerner-Hindi-339"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>316<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-isaka-Hindi&#95;340-3" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-isaka-Hindi-340"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>317<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The <a href="/wiki/Eighth_Schedule_to_the_Constitution_of_India" title="Eighth Schedule to the Constitution of India">Eighth Schedule of India's Constitution</a> also recognises 22 languages, including Hindi but not English, which the government is obligated to develop. These are sometimes called "scheduled languages". This list includes major regional languages, but also others—such as <a href="/wiki/Sanskrit" title="Sanskrit">Sanskrit</a>, which no longer has <a href="/wiki/First_language" title="First language">first language</a> speakers in India, and <a href="/wiki/Urdu" title="Urdu">Urdu</a>, which is not region-specific—because of their value to India's cultural heritage.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-KachruKachru2008-1&#95;341-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-KachruKachru2008-1-341"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>318<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-GazzolaWickström2016&#95;342-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-GazzolaWickström2016-342"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>319<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-Groff2017-lead&#95;343-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Groff2017-lead-343"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>320<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In 1950, there were 14 scheduled languages: <a href="/wiki/Assamese_language" title="Assamese language">Assamese</a>, <a href="/wiki/Bengali_language" title="Bengali language">Bengali</a>, <a href="/wiki/Gujarati_language" title="Gujarati language">Gujarati</a>, <a href="/wiki/Hindi" title="Hindi">Hindi</a>, <a href="/wiki/Kannada" title="Kannada">Kannada</a>, <a href="/wiki/Kashmiri_language" title="Kashmiri language">Kashmiri</a>, <a href="/wiki/Malayalam" title="Malayalam">Malayalam</a>, <a href="/wiki/Marathi_language" title="Marathi language">Marathi</a>, <a href="/wiki/Odia_language" title="Odia language">Oriya</a>, <a href="/wiki/Punjabi_language" title="Punjabi language">Punjabi</a>, <a href="/wiki/Sanskrit" title="Sanskrit">Sanskrit</a>, <a href="/wiki/Tamil_language" title="Tamil language">Tamil</a>, <a href="/wiki/Telugu_language" title="Telugu language">Telugu</a> and <a href="/wiki/Urdu" title="Urdu">Urdu</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-jolad-agarwal&#95;336-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-jolad-agarwal-336"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>314<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In the following decades constitutional amendments added others: <a href="/wiki/Sindhi_language" title="Sindhi language">Sindhi</a> (1967), <a href="/wiki/Nepali_language" title="Nepali language">Nepali</a>, <a href="/wiki/Meitei_language" title="Meitei language">Manipuri</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Konkani_language" title="Konkani language">Konkani</a> (1992), <a href="/wiki/Maithili_language" title="Maithili language">Maithili</a>, <a href="/wiki/Dogri_language" title="Dogri language">Dogri</a>, <a href="/wiki/Santali_language" title="Santali language">Santali</a> and <a href="/wiki/Boro_language_(India)" title="Boro language (India)">Bodo</a> (2004).<sup id="cite&#95;ref-jolad-agarwal&#95;336-3" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-jolad-agarwal-336"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>314<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<ul class="gallery mw-gallery-packed">
		<li class="gallerybox" style="width: 134px">
			<div class="thumb" style="width: 132px;"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:Language_Map_of_India.jpg" class="mw-file-description" title="The regions of first-language speech of the main languages of India"><img alt="The regions of first-language speech of the main languages of India" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8e/Language_Map_of_India.jpg/250px-Language_Map_of_India.jpg" decoding="async" width="132" height="150" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8e/Language_Map_of_India.jpg/330px-Language_Map_of_India.jpg 1.5x, //upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8e/Language_Map_of_India.jpg/500px-Language_Map_of_India.jpg 2x" data-file-width="720" data-file-height="818" /></a></span></div>

		</li>
		<li class="gallerybox" style="width: 172.66666666667px">

		</li>
</ul>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Religion">Religion</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Religion_in_India" title="Religion in India">Religion in India</a></div>
<p><a href="/wiki/Religion" title="Religion">Religion</a> in India is characterised by a diversity of beliefs and practices. Throughout India's history, religion has been an important part of its culture. The <a href="/wiki/Indian_subcontinent" title="Indian subcontinent">Indian subcontinent</a> is the birthplace of four <a href="/wiki/Major_religious_groups" title="Major religious groups">major world religions</a>: <a href="/wiki/Buddhism" title="Buddhism">Buddhism</a>, <a href="/wiki/Hinduism" title="Hinduism">Hinduism</a>, <a href="/wiki/Jainism" title="Jainism">Jainism</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Sikhism" title="Sikhism">Sikhism</a>. India has the largest population of Hindus, Sikhs, and Jains, the third-largest population of Muslims (after <a href="/wiki/Indonesia" title="Indonesia">Indonesia</a> and <a href="/wiki/Pakistan" title="Pakistan">Pakistan</a>) and the ninth largest of Buddhists.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Firstpost&#95;Aug2016&#95;345-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Firstpost_Aug2016-345"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>322<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> India also has the largest population of people adhering to both <a href="/wiki/Zoroastrianism" title="Zoroastrianism">Zoroastrianism</a> (<a href="/wiki/Parsi" class="mw-redirect" title="Parsi">Parsis</a> and <a href="/wiki/Irani_(India)" class="mw-redirect" title="Irani (India)">Iranis</a>) and the <a href="/wiki/Bah%C3%A1%CA%BC%C3%AD_Faith_in_India" title="Baháʼí Faith in India">Bahá'í Faith</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTESmith200894&#95;346-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTESmith200894-346"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>323<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The <a href="/wiki/Preamble_to_the_Constitution_of_India" title="Preamble to the Constitution of India">Preamble to the Constitution of India</a> declares India to be a <a href="/wiki/Secular_state" title="Secular state">secular state</a>,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-347" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-347"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>324<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-348" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-348"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>325<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> and <a href="/wiki/Freedom_of_religion" title="Freedom of religion">freedom of religion</a> to be a <a href="/wiki/Fundamental_Rights_in_India" class="mw-redirect" title="Fundamental Rights in India">fundamental right</a> ("... liberty of thought, expression, belief, faith, and worship.")<sup id="cite&#95;ref-349" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-349"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>326<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> According to the 2011 census of India, 79.8% of the <a href="/wiki/Demographics_of_India" title="Demographics of India">population of India</a> follows <a href="/wiki/Hinduism_in_India" title="Hinduism in India">Hinduism</a>, 14.2% <a href="/wiki/Islam_in_India" title="Islam in India">Islam</a>, 2.3% <a href="/wiki/Christianity_in_India" title="Christianity in India">Christianity</a>, 1.7% <a href="/wiki/Sikhism_in_India" title="Sikhism in India">Sikhism</a>, 0.7% <a href="/wiki/Buddhism_in_India" class="mw-redirect" title="Buddhism in India">Buddhism</a> and 0.4% <a href="/wiki/Jainism_in_India" title="Jainism in India">Jainism</a>. Several tribal religions are also present in India, such as <a href="/wiki/Donyi-Polo" title="Donyi-Polo">Donyi-Polo</a>, <a href="/wiki/Sanamahism" title="Sanamahism">Sanamahism</a>, <a href="/wiki/Sarnaism" title="Sarnaism">Sarnaism</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Niamtre" title="Niamtre">Niamtre</a>.
</p>
<ul class="gallery mw-gallery-packed">

<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Education">Education</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Education_in_India" title="Education in India">Education in India</a></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">See also: <a href="/wiki/Literacy_in_India" title="Literacy in India">Literacy in India</a> and <a href="/wiki/History_of_education_in_the_Indian_subcontinent" title="History of education in the Indian subcontinent">History of education in the Indian subcontinent</a></div>
<p>The literacy rate in 2011 was 74.04%: 65.46% among females and 82.14% among males.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEProvisional&#95;Population&#95;Totals&#95;Paper&#95;1&#95;of&#95;2011&#95;India163&#95;350-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEProvisional_Population_Totals_Paper_1_of_2011_India163-350"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>327<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The rural-urban literacy gap, which was 21.2 percentage points in 2001, dropped to 16.1 percentage points in 2011. The improvement in the rural literacy rate is twice that of urban areas.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEChandramouli2011&#95;334-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChandramouli2011-334"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>312<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Kerala" title="Kerala">Kerala</a> is the most literate state with 93.91% literacy; while <a href="/wiki/Bihar" title="Bihar">Bihar</a> the least with 63.82%.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEProvisional&#95;Population&#95;Totals&#95;Paper&#95;1&#95;of&#95;2011&#95;India163&#95;350-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEProvisional_Population_Totals_Paper_1_of_2011_India163-350"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>327<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In 1981 the respective literacy rates for total population, men and women were 41%, 53% and 29%. In 1951, the rates were 18%, 27% and 9%. In 1921, the rates 7%, 12% and 2%. In 1891, they were 5%, 9% and 1%.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-351" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-351"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>328<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-352" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-352"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>329<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> According to Latika Chaudhary, in 1911 there were under three primary schools for every ten villages. Statistically, more caste and religious diversity reduced private spending. Primary schools taught literacy, so local diversity limited its growth.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-353" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-353"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>330<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The education system of India is the world's second-largest.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-354" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-354"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>331<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> India has over 900 universities, 40,000 colleges<sup id="cite&#95;ref-highered1&#95;355-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-highered1-355"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>332<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> and 1.5&#160;million schools.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-356" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-356"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>333<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In India's higher education system, a significant number of seats are reserved under <a href="/wiki/Reservation_in_India" title="Reservation in India">affirmative action</a> policies for the historically disadvantaged. In recent decades India's improved education system is often cited as one of the main contributors to <a href="/wiki/Economic_development_in_India" title="Economic development in India">its economic development</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Sify&#95;357-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Sify-357"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>334<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-358" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-358"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>335<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<ul class="gallery mw-gallery-packed">
		<li class="gallerybox" style="width: 268.66666666667px">
			<div class="thumb" style="width: 266.66666666667px;"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:47_Raika_School_-_eating_together_(3384824242).jpg" class="mw-file-description" title="Children await school lunch in Rayka (also Raika), a village in rural Gujarat. The salutation Jai Bhim written on the blackboard honours the jurist, social reformer, and Dalit leader B. R. Ambedkar."><img alt="Children await school lunch in Rayka (also Raika), a village in rural Gujarat. The salutation Jai Bhim written on the blackboard honours the jurist, social reformer, and Dalit leader B. R. Ambedkar." src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/47_Raika_School_-_eating_together_%283384824242%29.jpg/500px-47_Raika_School_-_eating_together_%283384824242%29.jpg" decoding="async" width="267" height="200" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/47_Raika_School_-_eating_together_%283384824242%29.jpg/960px-47_Raika_School_-_eating_together_%283384824242%29.jpg 1.5x" data-file-width="2048" data-file-height="1536" /></a></span></div>
			<div class="gallerytext">Children await school lunch in Rayka (also Raika), a village in rural Gujarat. The salutation <i><a href="/wiki/Jai_Bhim" title="Jai Bhim">Jai Bhim</a></i> written on the blackboard honours the jurist, social reformer, and <a href="/wiki/Dalit" title="Dalit">Dalit</a> leader <a href="/wiki/B._R._Ambedkar" title="B. R. Ambedkar">B. R. Ambedkar</a>.</div>
		</li>
		<li class="gallerybox" style="width: 268.66666666667px">
			<div class="thumb" style="width: 266.66666666667px;"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:Madrasah1.jpg" class="mw-file-description" title="The Madrasah of the Masjid-i-Ala mosque in Srirangapatna, Karnataka. The mosque was built in the period 1786–87, during the rule of Tipu Sultan."><img alt="The Madrasah of the Masjid-i-Ala mosque in Srirangapatna, Karnataka. The mosque was built in the period 1786–87, during the rule of Tipu Sultan." src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/Madrasah1.jpg/500px-Madrasah1.jpg" decoding="async" width="267" height="200" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/Madrasah1.jpg/960px-Madrasah1.jpg 1.5x" data-file-width="2048" data-file-height="1536" /></a></span></div>
			<div class="gallerytext">The <a href="/wiki/Madrasah" class="mw-redirect" title="Madrasah">Madrasah</a> of the <a href="/wiki/Masjid-i-Ala" title="Masjid-i-Ala">Masjid-i-Ala</a> mosque in <a href="/wiki/Srirangapatna" title="Srirangapatna">Srirangapatna</a>, Karnataka. The mosque was built in the period 1786–87, during the rule of <a href="/wiki/Tipu_Sultan" title="Tipu Sultan">Tipu Sultan</a>.</div>
		</li>
		<li class="gallerybox" style="width: 268.66666666667px">
			<div class="thumb" style="width: 266.66666666667px;"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:ThomasonCollegeOfEngineeringRoorkeeEst1847.jpg" class="mw-file-description" title="The Indian Institute of Technology, Roorkee, formerly the Thomason College of Civil Engineering, is the oldest engineering college in India.[336][337] It was founded as the College of Civil Engineering in 1847 during East India Company rule to train officers and surveyors employed in the construction of the Ganges Canal.[337][336]"><img alt="The Indian Institute of Technology, Roorkee, formerly the Thomason College of Civil Engineering, is the oldest engineering college in India.[336][337] It was founded as the College of Civil Engineering in 1847 during East India Company rule to train officers and surveyors employed in the construction of the Ganges Canal.[337][336]" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/08/ThomasonCollegeOfEngineeringRoorkeeEst1847.jpg/500px-ThomasonCollegeOfEngineeringRoorkeeEst1847.jpg" decoding="async" width="267" height="200" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/08/ThomasonCollegeOfEngineeringRoorkeeEst1847.jpg/960px-ThomasonCollegeOfEngineeringRoorkeeEst1847.jpg 1.5x" data-file-width="3648" data-file-height="2736" /></a></span></div>
			<div class="gallerytext"> The <a href="/wiki/IIT_Roorkee" title="IIT Roorkee">Indian Institute of Technology, Roorkee</a>, formerly the Thomason College of Civil Engineering, is the oldest engineering college in India.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-hedrick-thomason&#95;359-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-hedrick-thomason-359"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>336<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-subram-thomason&#95;360-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-subram-thomason-360"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>337<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> It was founded as the <i>College of Civil Engineering</i> in 1847 during <a href="/wiki/Company_rule_in_India" title="Company rule in India">East India Company rule</a> to train officers and surveyors employed in the construction of the <a href="/wiki/Ganges_Canal" title="Ganges Canal">Ganges Canal</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-subram-thomason&#95;360-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-subram-thomason-360"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>337<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-hedrick-thomason&#95;359-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-hedrick-thomason-359"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>336<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></div>
		</li>
</ul>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Health">Health</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Health_in_India" title="Health in India">Health in India</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Female_health_workers_in_India_(34332433890).jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8f/Female_health_workers_in_India_%2834332433890%29.jpg/250px-Female_health_workers_in_India_%2834332433890%29.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="188" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8f/Female_health_workers_in_India_%2834332433890%29.jpg/500px-Female_health_workers_in_India_%2834332433890%29.jpg 1.5x" data-file-width="1280" data-file-height="960" /></a><figcaption> Immunisation health workers in 2006. Eight years later, and three years after India's last case of polio, the WHO declared India to be polio-free.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-who-chan-14-feb-2014&#95;361-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-who-chan-14-feb-2014-361"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>338<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></figcaption></figure>
<p>India bears a disproportionately large burden of the world's <a href="/wiki/Tuberculosis_in_India" title="Tuberculosis in India">tuberculosis</a> rates, with <a href="/wiki/World_Health_Organization" title="World Health Organization">World Health Organization</a> (WHO) statistics for 2022 estimating 2.8 million new infections annually, accounting for 26% of the global total.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-362" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-362"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>339<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> It is estimated that approximately 40% of the population of India carry <a href="/wiki/Latent_tuberculosis" title="Latent tuberculosis">tuberculosis</a> infection.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-363" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-363"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>340<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>In 2018 <a href="/wiki/Chronic_obstructive_pulmonary_disease" title="Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease">chronic obstructive pulmonary disease</a> was the leading cause of death after <a href="/wiki/Heart_disease_in_India" title="Heart disease in India">heart disease</a>. The 10 <a href="/wiki/List_of_most-polluted_cities_by_particulate_matter_concentration" title="List of most-polluted cities by particulate matter concentration">most polluted cities in the world</a> are all in India with more than 140 million people breathing air 10 times or more over the WHO safe limit. In 2017, air pollution killed 1.24 million Indians.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-364" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-364"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>341<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Culture">Culture</h2></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Culture_of_India" title="Culture of India">Culture of India</a></div>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Society">Society</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main articles: <a href="/wiki/Caste_system_in_India" title="Caste system in India">Caste system in India</a> and <a href="/wiki/Gender_inequality_in_India" title="Gender inequality in India">Gender inequality in India</a></div>
<p>Although sometimes applied to other cultures and religions, caste is a uniquely Indian, and Hindu, social institution.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-366" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-366"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>x<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> All Hindus fall broadly into four castes, or <i><a href="/wiki/Varna_(Hinduism)" title="Varna (Hinduism)">varnas</a></i>: <a href="/wiki/Brahmin" title="Brahmin">Brahmin</a>, or priests, at the top; below them <a href="/wiki/Kshatriya" title="Kshatriya">Kshatriya</a>, or warriors; further below, <a href="/wiki/Vaishya" title="Vaishya">Vaishya</a>, or merchants and farmers; and at the bottom, <a href="/wiki/Shudra" title="Shudra">Shudra</a>, or the service class. Outside the caste system, and therefore of traditional Hinduism, lie people formerly called "outcastes" or "untouchables," and now <a href="/wiki/Scheduled_caste" class="mw-redirect" title="Scheduled caste">scheduled caste</a> (a term used in India's constitution) or <a href="/wiki/Dalit" title="Dalit">Dalit</a>, a later self-description of pride, meaning "broken" or "downtrodden". Each caste is further divided into sub-castes, or <a href="/wiki/J%C4%81ti" title="Jāti">jatis</a>, many of which are tied to occupations. The custom of <a href="/wiki/Endogamy" title="Endogamy">endogamy</a>, or marrying within one's subcaste, however, makes caste a hereditary label, not of one occupational choice, and has caused the caste system, therefore, to become entrenched.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-atrey1&#95;367-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-atrey1-367"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>343<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The <a href="/wiki/Constituent_Assembly_of_India" title="Constituent Assembly of India">Constituent Assembly of India</a> abolished untouchability in 1947,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-nyt-india-constit-assmb-caste&#95;368-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-nyt-india-constit-assmb-caste-368"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>344<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> the <a href="/wiki/Republic_of_India" class="mw-redirect" title="Republic of India">Republic of India</a> did more formally in 1950, and India has since enacted other anti-discriminatory laws and social welfare initiatives related to caste. Still, caste-based inequality, discrimination, segregation, and <a href="/wiki/Caste-related_violence_in_India" title="Caste-related violence in India">violence</a> persist.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-369" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-369"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>345<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-370" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-370"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>346<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Multi-generational <a href="/wiki/Patrilineal" class="mw-redirect" title="Patrilineal">patrilineal</a> <a href="/wiki/Joint_family" class="mw-redirect" title="Joint family">joint families</a> have been the norm in India, though <a href="/wiki/Nuclear_family" title="Nuclear family">nuclear families</a> are becoming common in urban areas.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEMakar2007&#95;371-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMakar2007-371"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>347<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> A very large majority of Indians have <a href="/wiki/Arranged_marriage_in_the_Indian_subcontinent" title="Arranged marriage in the Indian subcontinent">their marriages arranged</a> by their parents or family elders.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEMedora2003&#95;372-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMedora2003-372"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>348<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Marriage is thought to be for life;<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEMedora2003&#95;372-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMedora2003-372"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>348<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> and the divorce rate is extremely low;<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEJonesRamdas2005111&#95;373-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJonesRamdas2005111-373"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>349<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> less than one in a thousand marriages end in divorce.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-374" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-374"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>350<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Many women marry before reaching 18, which is their legal marriageable age; <a href="/wiki/Child_marriage" title="Child marriage">child marriages</a> are not uncommon, especially in rural areas.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTECullen-Dupont200996&#95;375-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTECullen-Dupont200996-375"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>351<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In large parts of Hindu northern India, moreover, a form of territorial <a href="/wiki/Exogamy" title="Exogamy">exogamy</a> is observed in which a bride marries out of her natal village and her parents do not visit her in her married home; the annual rite <a href="/wiki/Raksha_bandhan" class="mw-redirect" title="Raksha bandhan">raksha bandhan</a>, during which married women return to their <a href="/wiki/Birth" title="Birth">natal</a> homes, has served both to affirm bonds with their natal families and offer a recourse in times of marital stress.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Agarwal1994&#95;376-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Agarwal1994-376"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>352<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-Coleman2017&#95;377-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Coleman2017-377"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>353<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<ul class="gallery mw-gallery-packed">
		<li class="gallerybox" style="width: 302px">
			<div class="thumb" style="width: 300px;"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:Closeup_at_Gussadi_dance_performer.jpg" class="mw-file-description" title="A member of the Gond tribe during the Dandari festival in Jainoor, Telangana. Some 8.6% of India&#39;s population belong to tribal groups. The supercontinent Gondwana is named after the Gond region of India. Their religion predates the Hindu synthesis of the mid-first-millennium BCE."><img alt="A member of the Gond tribe during the Dandari festival in Jainoor, Telangana. Their religion predates the Hindu synthesis of the mid-first-millennium BCE." src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/Closeup_at_Gussadi_dance_performer.jpg/500px-Closeup_at_Gussadi_dance_performer.jpg" decoding="async" width="300" height="200" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/Closeup_at_Gussadi_dance_performer.jpg/960px-Closeup_at_Gussadi_dance_performer.jpg 1.5x" data-file-width="6000" data-file-height="4000" /></a></span></div>
			<div class="gallerytext">A member of the <a href="/wiki/Gondi_people" title="Gondi people">Gond</a> tribe during the <a href="/wiki/Gussadi_dance" title="Gussadi dance">Dandari</a> festival in <a href="/wiki/Jainoor" title="Jainoor">Jainoor</a>, Telangana. Some 8.6% of India's population belong to tribal groups. The supercontinent <a href="/wiki/Gondwana" title="Gondwana">Gondwana</a> is named after the Gond region of India. Their religion predates the <a href="/wiki/Hindu_synthesis" class="mw-redirect" title="Hindu synthesis">Hindu synthesis</a> of the mid-first-millennium BCE.</div>
		</li>
		<li class="gallerybox" style="width: 302.66666666667px">
			<div class="thumb" style="width: 300.66666666667px;"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:A_smiling_member_of_the_Ramnami_Samaj_(edited).jpg" class="mw-file-description" title="A member of the Ramnami Samaj, a movement among Dalits, whose members worship the Hindu deity Rama and tattoo their bodies with his name"><img alt="A member of the Ramnami Samaj, a movement among Dalits, whose members worship the Hindu deity Rama and tattoo their bodies with his name" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/69/A_smiling_member_of_the_Ramnami_Samaj_%28edited%29.jpg/500px-A_smiling_member_of_the_Ramnami_Samaj_%28edited%29.jpg" decoding="async" width="301" height="200" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/69/A_smiling_member_of_the_Ramnami_Samaj_%28edited%29.jpg/960px-A_smiling_member_of_the_Ramnami_Samaj_%28edited%29.jpg 1.5x" data-file-width="6048" data-file-height="4024" /></a></span></div>

		</li>
		<li class="gallerybox" style="width: 150px">
			<div class="thumb" style="width: 148px;"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:Hindu_Bride,_Ahmedabad,_Gujarat.jpg" class="mw-file-description" title="A Hindu bride in Ahmedabad, Gujarat"><img alt="A Hindu bride in Ahmedabad, Gujarat" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/80/Hindu_Bride%2C_Ahmedabad%2C_Gujarat.jpg/250px-Hindu_Bride%2C_Ahmedabad%2C_Gujarat.jpg" decoding="async" width="148" height="200" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/80/Hindu_Bride%2C_Ahmedabad%2C_Gujarat.jpg/500px-Hindu_Bride%2C_Ahmedabad%2C_Gujarat.jpg 1.5x" data-file-width="2032" data-file-height="2750" /></a></span></div>

		</li>
</ul>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Visual_art">Visual art</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Indian_art" title="Indian art">Indian art</a></div>
<p>India has a very ancient tradition of art, which has exchanged many influences with the rest of <a href="/wiki/Eurasia" title="Eurasia">Eurasia</a>, especially in the first millennium. During this period <a href="/wiki/Buddhist_art" title="Buddhist art">Buddhist art</a> spread with Indian religions to <a href="/wiki/Central_Asia" title="Central Asia">Central</a>, <a href="/wiki/East_Asia" title="East Asia">East</a> and <a href="/wiki/Southeast_Asia" title="Southeast Asia">Southeast Asia</a>, the last also greatly influenced by Hindu art.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTERowland1970185–198,&#95;252,&#95;385–466&#95;378-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTERowland1970185–198,_252,_385–466-378"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>354<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Thousands of <a href="/wiki/Indus_Valley_civilisation#Seals" class="mw-redirect" title="Indus Valley civilisation">seals from the Indus Valley civilisation</a> of the third millennium BCE have been found, usually carved with animals, but also some with human figures. The <a href="/wiki/Pashupati_seal" title="Pashupati seal">Pashupati seal</a>, excavated in <a href="/wiki/Mohenjo-daro" title="Mohenjo-daro">Mohenjo-daro</a>, Pakistan, in 1928–29, is the best known.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTECraven199714–16&#95;379-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTECraven199714–16-379"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>355<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEHarle199417–18&#95;380-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarle199417–18-380"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>356<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Virtually no art survives from a long period following the Indus Valley Civilisation.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEHarle199417–18&#95;380-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarle199417–18-380"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>356<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTERowland197046–47&#95;381-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTERowland197046–47-381"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>357<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Almost all surviving ancient Indian art thereafter is in various forms of religious <a href="/wiki/Indian_sculpture" class="mw-redirect" title="Indian sculpture">sculpture</a> in durable materials, or coins. There was probably originally far more in wood, which is lost. In north India <a href="/wiki/Mauryan_art" title="Mauryan art">Mauryan art</a> is the first imperial movement.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTECraven199735–46&#95;382-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTECraven199735–46-382"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>358<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTERowland197067–70&#95;383-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTERowland197067–70-383"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>359<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEHarle199422–24&#95;384-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarle199422–24-384"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>360<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Over the following centuries a distinctly Indian style of sculpting the human figure developed, with less interest in articulating precise anatomy than <a href="/wiki/Ancient_Greek_sculpture" title="Ancient Greek sculpture">ancient Greek sculpture</a> but showing smoothly flowing forms expressing <i>prana</i> ("breath" or life-force).<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTECraven199722,&#95;88&#95;385-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTECraven199722,_88-385"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>361<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTERowland197035,&#95;99–100&#95;386-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTERowland197035,_99–100-386"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>362<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> This is often complicated by the need to give figures multiple arms or heads, or represent different genders on the left and right of figures, as with the <a href="/wiki/Ardhanarishvara" title="Ardhanarishvara">Ardhanarishvara</a> form of Shiva and <a href="/wiki/Parvati" title="Parvati">Parvati</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTECraven199718–19&#95;387-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTECraven199718–19-387"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>363<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEBlurton1993151&#95;388-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBlurton1993151-388"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>364<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Most of the earliest large sculpture is Buddhist, either excavated from Buddhist <a href="/wiki/Stupa" title="Stupa">stupas</a> such as <a href="/wiki/Sanchi" class="mw-redirect" title="Sanchi">Sanchi</a>, <a href="/wiki/Sarnath" title="Sarnath">Sarnath</a> and <a href="/wiki/Amaravati_Stupa" title="Amaravati Stupa">Amaravati</a>,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEHarle199432–38&#95;389-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarle199432–38-389"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>365<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> or is rock cut <a href="/wiki/Relief" title="Relief">reliefs</a> at sites such as <a href="/wiki/Ajanta_Caves" title="Ajanta Caves">Ajanta</a>, <a href="/wiki/Karla_Caves" title="Karla Caves">Karla</a> and <a href="/wiki/Ellora" class="mw-redirect" title="Ellora">Ellora</a>. Hindu and Jain sites appear rather later.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEHarle199443–55&#95;390-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarle199443–55-390"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>366<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTERowland1970113–119&#95;391-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTERowland1970113–119-391"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>367<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In spite of this complex mixture of religious traditions, generally, the prevailing artistic style at any time and place has been shared by the major religious groups, and sculptors probably usually served all communities.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEBlurton199310–11&#95;392-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBlurton199310–11-392"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>368<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Gupta_art" title="Gupta art">Gupta art</a>, at its peak <abbr title="circa">c.</abbr><span style="white-space:nowrap;">&#8201;300&#160;<span title="Common Era">CE</span></span>&#160;– c.<span style="white-space:nowrap;">&#8201;500&#160;<span title="Common Era">CE</span></span>, is often regarded as a classical period whose influence lingered for many centuries after; it saw a new dominance of Hindu sculpture, as at the <a href="/wiki/Elephanta_Caves" title="Elephanta Caves">Elephanta Caves</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTECraven1997111–121&#95;393-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTECraven1997111–121-393"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>369<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEMichell200044–70&#95;394-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMichell200044–70-394"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>370<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Across the north, this became rather stiff and formulaic after <abbr title="circa">c.</abbr><span style="white-space:nowrap;">&#8201;800&#160;<span title="Common Era">CE</span></span>, though rich with finely carved detail in the surrounds of statues.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEHarle1994212–216&#95;395-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarle1994212–216-395"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>371<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> But in the South, under the <a href="/wiki/Pallava_dynasty" title="Pallava dynasty">Pallava</a> and <a href="/wiki/Chola_dynasty" title="Chola dynasty">Chola dynasties</a>, sculpture in both stone and bronze had a <a href="/wiki/Chola_art_and_architecture#Sculpture_and_bronzes" title="Chola art and architecture">sustained period of great achievement</a>; the large bronzes with Shiva as <a href="/wiki/Nataraja" title="Nataraja">Nataraja</a> have become an iconic symbol of India.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTECraven1997152–160&#95;396-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTECraven1997152–160-396"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>372<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEBlurton1993225–227&#95;397-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBlurton1993225–227-397"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>373<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Ancient paintings have only survived at a few sites, of which the crowded scenes of court life in the <a href="/wiki/Ajanta_Caves" title="Ajanta Caves">Ajanta Caves</a> are some of the most important.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEHarle1994356–361&#95;398-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarle1994356–361-398"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>374<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTERowland1970242–251&#95;399-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTERowland1970242–251-399"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>375<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Painted manuscripts of religious texts survive from Eastern India from 10th century onwards, most of the earliest being Buddhist and later Jain. These significantly influenced later artistic styles.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEHarle1994361–370&#95;400-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarle1994361–370-400"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>376<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The Persian-derived <a href="/wiki/Deccan_painting" title="Deccan painting">Deccan painting</a>, starting just before the <a href="/wiki/Mughal_miniature" class="mw-redirect" title="Mughal miniature">Mughal miniature</a>, between them give the first large body of secular painting, with an emphasis on portraits, and the recording of princely pleasures and wars.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTECraven1997202–208&#95;401-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTECraven1997202–208-401"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>377<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEHarle1994372–382,&#95;400–406&#95;402-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarle1994372–382,_400–406-402"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>378<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The style spread to Hindu courts, especially <a href="/wiki/Rajput_painting" title="Rajput painting">among the Rajputs</a>, and developed a variety of styles, with the smaller courts often the most innovative, with figures such as <a href="/wiki/Nih%C3%A2l_Chand" title="Nihâl Chand">Nihâl Chand</a> and <a href="/wiki/Nainsukh" title="Nainsukh">Nainsukh</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTECraven1997222–243&#95;403-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTECraven1997222–243-403"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>379<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEHarle1994384–397,&#95;407–420&#95;404-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarle1994384–397,_407–420-404"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>380<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> As a market developed among European residents, it was supplied by <a href="/wiki/Company_painting" class="mw-redirect" title="Company painting">Company painting</a> by Indian artists with considerable Western influence.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTECraven1997243&#95;405-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTECraven1997243-405"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>381<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEMichell2000210&#95;406-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMichell2000210-406"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>382<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In the 19th century, cheap <a href="/wiki/Kalighat_painting" title="Kalighat painting">Kalighat paintings</a> of gods and everyday life, done on paper, were urban <a href="/wiki/Folk_art" title="Folk art">folk art</a> from <a href="/wiki/Calcutta" class="mw-redirect" title="Calcutta">Calcutta</a>, which later saw the <a href="/wiki/Bengal_School_of_Art" title="Bengal School of Art">Bengal School of Art</a>, reflecting the art colleges founded by the British, the first movement in <a href="/wiki/Modern_Indian_painting" title="Modern Indian painting">modern Indian painting</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEMichell2000210–211&#95;407-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMichell2000210–211-407"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>383<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEBlurton1993211&#95;408-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBlurton1993211-408"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>384<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<ul class="gallery mw-gallery-packed">
		<li class="gallerybox" style="width: 218px">
