<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Mathematics">Mathematics</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main articles: <a href="/wiki/Indian_mathematics" title="Indian mathematics">Indian mathematics</a> and <a href="/wiki/Kerala_school_of_astronomy_and_mathematics" title="Kerala school of astronomy and mathematics">Kerala school of astronomy and mathematics</a></div>
<p>Significant mathematics began in India in the first millennium BCE. The <i><a href="/wiki/Shulba_Sutras" title="Shulba Sutras">Śulba Sūtras</a></i> (literally, "Aphorisms of the Chords" in <a href="/wiki/Vedic_Sanskrit" title="Vedic Sanskrit">Vedic Sanskrit</a>) (c. 700–400 BCE) contain the earliest extant verbal expression of the <a href="/wiki/Pythagorean_theorem" title="Pythagorean theorem">Pythagorean theorem</a> (although very likely it had been known to the <a href="/wiki/First_Babylonian_dynasty" class="mw-redirect" title="First Babylonian dynasty">Old Babylonians</a>.)<sup id="cite&#95;ref-hayashi2005-p363&#95;409-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-hayashi2005-p363-409"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>385<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-412" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-412"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>y<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> All mathematical works were orally transmitted until approximately 500&#160;BCE; thereafter, they were transmitted both orally and in manuscript form. The oldest extant mathematical document produced on the Indian subcontinent is the birch bark <a href="/wiki/Bakhshali_manuscript" title="Bakhshali manuscript">Bakhshali manuscript</a> from the 7th century CE.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-hayashi95&#95;413-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-hayashi95-413"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>388<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-plofker-brit6&#95;414-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-plofker-brit6-414"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>389<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>In the classical period of Indian mathematics (400 CE to 1200 CE), important contributions were made by <a href="/wiki/Aryabhata" title="Aryabhata">Aryabhata</a>, <a href="/wiki/Brahmagupta" title="Brahmagupta">Brahmagupta</a>, <a href="/wiki/Bhaskara_II" class="mw-redirect" title="Bhaskara II">Bhaskara II</a>, <a href="/wiki/Var%C4%81hamihira" title="Varāhamihira">Varāhamihira</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Madhava_of_Sangamagrama" title="Madhava of Sangamagrama">Madhava</a>. The <a href="/wiki/Decimal" title="Decimal">decimal number system</a> in use today<sup id="cite&#95;ref-irfah346&#95;415-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-irfah346-415"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>390<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> was first recorded in Indian mathematics.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-416" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-416"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>391<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Indian mathematicians made early contributions to the study of the concept of <a href="/wiki/0_(number)" class="mw-redirect" title="0 (number)">zero</a> as a number,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-bourbaki46&#95;417-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-bourbaki46-417"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>392<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Negative_numbers" class="mw-redirect" title="Negative numbers">negative numbers</a>,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-bourbaki49&#95;418-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-bourbaki49-418"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>393<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Arithmetic" title="Arithmetic">arithmetic</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Algebra" title="Algebra">algebra</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-concise-britannica&#95;419-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-concise-britannica-419"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>394<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Trigonometry" title="Trigonometry">Trigonometry</a><sup id="cite&#95;ref-420" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-420"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>395<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> was further advanced in India, and the modern definitions of <a href="/wiki/Sine" class="mw-redirect" title="Sine">sine</a> and <a href="/wiki/Cosine" class="mw-redirect" title="Cosine">cosine</a> were developed there.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-421" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-421"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>396<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> These mathematical concepts were transmitted to the Middle East, China, and Europe.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-concise-britannica&#95;419-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-concise-britannica-419"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>394<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> A later landmark in Indian mathematics was the development of the <a href="/wiki/Series_(mathematics)" title="Series (mathematics)">series</a> expansions for <a href="/wiki/Trigonometric_function" class="mw-redirect" title="Trigonometric function">trigonometric functions</a> (sine, cosine, and <a href="/wiki/Arc_tangent" class="mw-redirect" title="Arc tangent">arc tangent</a>) by mathematicians of the <a href="/wiki/Kerala_school_of_astronomy_and_mathematics" title="Kerala school of astronomy and mathematics">Kerala school</a> in the 15th century CE. Their work, completed two centuries before the invention of <a href="/wiki/Calculus" title="Calculus">calculus</a> in Europe, provided the first example of a <a href="/wiki/Power_series" title="Power series">power series</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-422" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-422"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>z<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-423" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-423"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>397<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In the modern era <a href="/wiki/Srinivasa_Ramanujan" title="Srinivasa Ramanujan">Srinivasa Ramanujan</a> made fundamental contributions to <a href="/wiki/Number_theory" title="Number theory">number theory</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-hardy-ramanumajan-notice&#95;424-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-hardy-ramanumajan-notice-424"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>398<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Music">Music</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Music_of_India" title="Music of India">Music of India</a></div>
<p>India contains a wide array of musical practices, including many different <a href="/wiki/Indian_folk_music" title="Indian folk music">folk musics</a> from different regions. <a href="/wiki/Indian_classical_music" title="Indian classical music">Indian classical music</a> has Vedic origins, and split in the 13th century into the two main traditions of <a href="/wiki/Hindustani_classical_music" title="Hindustani classical music">Hindustani</a> and <a href="/wiki/Carnatic_music" title="Carnatic music">Carnatic</a> music. Hindustani is associated with North India and is more <a href="/wiki/Musical_improvisation" title="Musical improvisation">improvisational</a>, featuring instruments such as the <a href="/wiki/Sitar" title="Sitar">sitar</a> and <a href="/wiki/Tabla" title="Tabla">tabla</a>, and Carnatic is South Indian and more focused on written <a href="/wiki/Musical_composition" title="Musical composition">compositions</a> such as the <i><a href="/wiki/Kriti_(music)" title="Kriti (music)">kriti</a></i>, while both styles contain common elements such as the <a href="/wiki/Raga" title="Raga">raga</a> melodic framework and <a href="/wiki/Tala_(music)" title="Tala (music)">tala</a> rhythmic meter.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-425" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-425"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>399<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Indian music has influenced western genres, notably <a href="/wiki/Rock_music" title="Rock music">rock</a> and <a href="/wiki/Jazz" title="Jazz">jazz</a> musicians during the <a href="/wiki/Counterculture_of_the_1960s" title="Counterculture of the 1960s">1960s counterculture</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-426" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-426"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>400<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p><a href="/wiki/Filmi" title="Filmi">Filmi</a> is music written for <a href="/wiki/Cinema_of_India" title="Cinema of India">Indian cinema</a>, generally composed by <a href="/wiki/List_of_Indian_film_music_directors" title="List of Indian film music directors">music directors</a> and sung by <a href="/wiki/Playback_singers" class="mw-redirect" title="Playback singers">playback singers</a>. Modern <a href="/wiki/Indian_pop" title="Indian pop">Indian pop</a> takes influences from classical, folk, and western pop music.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-427" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-427"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>401<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<ul class="gallery mw-gallery-packed">
		<li class="gallerybox" style="width: 133.33333333333px">
			<div class="thumb" style="width: 131.33333333333px;"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:Sakunthala_1940_filmposter_(2).jpg" class="mw-file-description" title="M. S. Subbulakshmi, Carnatic music vocalist and the first Indian musician to perform at the United Nations in 1966, began her career singing in Tamil films."><img alt="M. S. 			<div class="gallerytext"><a href="/wiki/M._S._Subbulakshmi" title="M. S. Subbulakshmi">M. S. Subbulakshmi</a>, Carnatic music vocalist and the first Indian musician to perform at the <a href="/wiki/United_Nations" title="United Nations">United Nations</a> in 1966, began her career singing in <a href="/wiki/Tamil_films" class="mw-redirect" title="Tamil films">Tamil films</a>. </div>
		</li>
		<li class="gallerybox" style="width: 293.33333333333px">
			<div class="thumb" style="width: 291.33333333333px;"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:L_Vaidyanathan,_L_Subramaniam_and_L_Shankar_trio_with_Palghat_Mani_Iyer_on_Mridangam.jpg" class="mw-file-description" title="Carnatic music mridangam player Palghat Mani Iyer (left) at a concert with three violinists, from left to right: L. 			<div class="gallerytext">Carnatic music <a href="/wiki/Mridangam" title="Mridangam">mridangam</a> player <a href="/wiki/Palghat_Mani_Iyer" title="Palghat Mani Iyer">Palghat Mani Iyer</a> (left) at a concert with three violinists, from left to right: <a href="/wiki/L._Vaidyanathan" title="L. Vaidyanathan">L. Vaidyanathan</a>, <a href="/wiki/L._Subramaniam" title="L. Subramaniam">L. Subramaniam</a>, and <a href="/wiki/L._Shankar" title="L. Shankar">L. Shankar</a>.</div>
		</li>
		<li class="gallerybox" style="width: 162px">

			<div class="gallerytext"><a href="/wiki/Allauddin_Khan" title="Allauddin Khan">Allauddin Khan</a>, a <a href="/wiki/Hindustani_classical_music" title="Hindustani classical music">Hindustani classical music</a> <a href="/wiki/Sarod" title="Sarod">sarod</a> player was also an influential teacher. 		</li>
</ul>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Dance">Dance</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Dance_in_India" title="Dance in India">Dance in India</a></div>
<p>Dance in India has drawn heavily from <a href="/wiki/Indian_classical_dance" title="Indian classical dance">Indian classical dance</a> traditions. Many of these in turn arose in temples or other religious contexts. Their sponsorship and promotion, however, has continued in secular, modern India.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-vatsyayan-lord&#95;428-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-vatsyayan-lord-428"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>402<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-429" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-429"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>aa<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> India also has local and modern dance traditions.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-vatsyayan-lord&#95;428-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-vatsyayan-lord-428"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>402<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Whether or not a dance is classical is determined by the <a href="/wiki/Sangeet_Natak_Academi" class="mw-redirect" title="Sangeet Natak Academi">Sangeet Natak Academi</a>, the Indian government's organisation for performing arts.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-430" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-430"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>ab<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Although more dances could perhaps meet the criteria for classical, the Akademi has chosen eight.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-431" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-431"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>ac<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> 
</p>
<table class="wikitable" style="margin-right:auto; margin-left:auto;">
<caption>Classical Dances of India<sup id="cite&#95;ref-vatsyayan-lord&#95;428-5" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-vatsyayan-lord-428"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>402<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-432" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-432"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>403<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>

</th></tr>

<td style="text-align:center;"><a href="/wiki/Court_dance" class="mw-redirect" title="Court dance">Court dance</a></td>

<td style="text-align:center;">South India</td>
<td style="text-align:center;">Dance-drama</td>
<td style="text-align:center;">Carnatic music ensemble
</td></tr>
<tr style="text-align:center;">

<td style="text-align:center;"><a href="/wiki/Northeast_India" title="Northeast India">Northeast India</a></td>
<td style="text-align:center;">Temple/ritual dance</td>
<td style="text-align:center;">Ensemble comprising <a href="/wiki/Pung_Cholom" title="Pung Cholom">Pung Cholom</a>, flutes, trumpets, <a href="/wiki/Tanbur" title="Tanbur">Tambura</a>, <a href="/wiki/Pena_(musical_instrument)" title="Pena (musical instrument)">Pena</a>, and cymbals
</td></tr>
<tr style="text-align:center;">
<td>6</td>
<td style="text-align:center;"><a href="/wiki/Mohiniattam" class="mw-redirect" title="Mohiniattam">Mohiniattam</a></td>
<td style="text-align:center;">Kerala</td>
<td style="text-align:center;">South India</td>
<td style="text-align:center;">Dance-drama</td>
<td style="text-align:center;">Carnatic ensemble
</td></tr>
<tr style="text-align:center;">
<td>7</td>
<td style="text-align:center;"><a href="/wiki/Odissi" title="Odissi">Odissi</a></td>
<td style="text-align:center;"><a href="/wiki/Odissa" class="mw-redirect" title="Odissa">Odissa</a></td>
<td style="text-align:center;"><a href="/wiki/East_India" title="East India">East India</a></td>
<td style="text-align:center;">Temple dance</td>

</td></tr>
<tr style="text-align:center;">
<td>8</td>
<td style="text-align:center;"><a href="/wiki/Sattriya" title="Sattriya">Sattriya</a></td>
<td style="text-align:center;"><a href="/wiki/Assam" title="Assam">Assam</a></td>
<td style="text-align:center;">Northeast India</td>
<td style="text-align:center;">Dance-drama</td>
<td style="text-align:center;"><a href="/wiki/Borgeet" title="Borgeet">Borgeet</a> accompanied by <a href="/wiki/Khol" title="Khol">khol</a> drums and cymbals.
</td></tr></tbody></table>
<p>The best-known classical dance is Bharatnatyam, which began in the temple dances of <a href="/wiki/Tamil_people" class="mw-redirect" title="Tamil people">Tamil</a> <a href="/wiki/Devadasi" title="Devadasi">devadasis</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-vatsyayan-lord&#95;428-6" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-vatsyayan-lord-428"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>402<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Identified with "prostitutes and courtesans", their dancing was formally banned in 1947.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-433" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-433"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>ad<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Concurrently, the dance was rehabilitated as a "pure" art form, with <a href="/wiki/Rukmini_Devi_Arundale" title="Rukmini Devi Arundale">Rukmini Devi Arundale</a> as a prominent figure. A devdasi who went on to attain national and international prominence was <a href="/wiki/Balasaraswati" title="Balasaraswati">Thanjavur Balasaraswati</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-vatsyayan-lord&#95;428-8" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-vatsyayan-lord-428"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>402<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Some sources consider the dance-dramas <a href="/wiki/Chhau_dance" class="mw-redirect" title="Chhau dance">Chhau</a> of <a href="/wiki/Jharkhand" title="Jharkhand">Jharkhand</a>, <a href="/wiki/West_Bengal" title="West Bengal">West Bengal</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Odissa" class="mw-redirect" title="Odissa">Odissa</a> and <a href="/wiki/Yakshagana" title="Yakshagana">Yakshagana</a> of <a href="/wiki/Karnataka" title="Karnataka">Karnataka</a> to also belong to the classical tradition.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-burch-2013&#95;434-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-burch-2013-434"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>404<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> 
</p><p>Local dance traditions vary widely across India. In addition to the dance-dramas Chhau and Yakshagana, they include dance-dramas <a href="/wiki/Raslila" title="Raslila">Raslila</a> of western <a href="/wiki/Uttar_Pradesh" title="Uttar Pradesh">Uttar Pradesh</a> and <a href="/wiki/Terukkuttu" title="Terukkuttu">Terukkuttu</a> of Tamil Nadu; calendrical and festival dances such as the <a href="/wiki/Bhangra_(dance)" title="Bhangra (dance)">Bhangra</a> of Punjab, especially at <a href="/wiki/Vaisakhi" title="Vaisakhi">Vaisakhi</a>, the onset of spring, and <a href="/wiki/Garba_(dance)" title="Garba (dance)">Garba</a> of <a href="/wiki/Gujarat" title="Gujarat">Gujarat</a> during <a href="/wiki/Navratri" class="mw-redirect" title="Navratri">Navratri</a>; and tribal or <a href="/wiki/Adivasi" title="Adivasi">Adivasi</a> dances, such as those of the <a href="/wiki/Santal" class="mw-redirect" title="Santal">Santal</a> and <a href="/wiki/Toda_people" title="Toda people">Toda people</a>, the latter, for example, in honour of the god <a href="/wiki/Toda_people#Religion" title="Toda people">Ön</a> who brought buffalo to earth.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-vatsyayan-lord&#95;428-9" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-vatsyayan-lord-428"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>402<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Among 20th-century directions is the <a href="/wiki/Modern_dance" title="Modern dance">modern dance</a> of <a href="/wiki/Uday_Shankar" title="Uday Shankar">Uday Shankar</a> in which classical styles were employed but not adhered to rigidly. Examples are dance-dramas based on the ancient Indian <a href="/wiki/Animal_fable" class="mw-redirect" title="Animal fable">animal fables</a>, <i><a href="/wiki/Panchatantra" title="Panchatantra">Panchatantra</a></i>, and Nehru's mid-century meditation on Indian history, <i><a href="/wiki/The_Discovery_of_India" title="The Discovery of India">The Discovery of India</a></i>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-vatsyayan-lord&#95;428-10" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-vatsyayan-lord-428"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>402<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Dance has been an essential aspect of Indian films from the first <a href="/wiki/Talkies" class="mw-redirect" title="Talkies">talkies</a> of the 1930s. The individual and group dances of <a href="/wiki/Bollywood" class="mw-redirect" title="Bollywood">Bollywood</a>, for example, show a broad range of influences, including classical, local, and Western popular dance.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-vatsyayan-lord&#95;428-11" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-vatsyayan-lord-428"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>402<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Towards the end of the 20th century, innovations in <a href="/wiki/British_South_Asian" class="mw-redirect" title="British South Asian">British South Asian</a> music and dance, such as <a href="/wiki/Music_of_the_United_Kingdom_(1990s)#Post-Bhangra" title="Music of the United Kingdom (1990s)">Post-Bhangra</a>, fed back into dance in India.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-vatsyayan-lord&#95;428-12" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-vatsyayan-lord-428"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>402<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<ul class="gallery mw-gallery-packed">
		<li class="gallerybox" style="width: 302px">
			<div class="thumb" style="width: 300px;"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:Kathakali_of_Kerala_at_Nishagandhi_dance_festival_2024_(266).jpg" class="mw-file-description" title="The Kathakali dance of Kerala"><img alt="The Kathakali dance of Kerala" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/39/Kathakali_of_Kerala_at_Nishagandhi_dance_festival_2024_%28266%29.jpg/500px-Kathakali_of_Kerala_at_Nishagandhi_dance_festival_2024_%28266%29.jpg" decoding="async" width="300" height="200" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/39/Kathakali_of_Kerala_at_Nishagandhi_dance_festival_2024_%28266%29.jpg/960px-Kathakali_of_Kerala_at_Nishagandhi_dance_festival_2024_%28266%29.jpg 1.5x" data-file-width="5568" data-file-height="3712" /></a></span></div>
			<div class="gallerytext">The <i>Kathakali</i> dance of <a href="/wiki/Kerala" title="Kerala">Kerala</a></div>
		</li>
		<li class="gallerybox" style="width: 129.33333333333px">
			<div class="thumb" style="width: 127.33333333333px;"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:Bharata_Natyam_Performance_DS.jpg" class="mw-file-description" title="The Bharatanatyam dance of Tamil Nadu"><img alt="The Bharatanatyam dance of Tamil Nadu" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/Bharata_Natyam_Performance_DS.jpg/250px-Bharata_Natyam_Performance_DS.jpg" decoding="async" width="128" height="200" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/Bharata_Natyam_Performance_DS.jpg/330px-Bharata_Natyam_Performance_DS.jpg 1.5x, //upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/Bharata_Natyam_Performance_DS.jpg/500px-Bharata_Natyam_Performance_DS.jpg 2x" data-file-width="1500" data-file-height="2357" /></a></span></div>
			<div class="gallerytext">The <i>Bharatanatyam</i> dance of <a href="/wiki/Tamil_Nadu" title="Tamil Nadu">Tamil Nadu</a></div>
		</li>
		<li class="gallerybox" style="width: 302px">
			<div class="thumb" style="width: 300px;"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:Kathak_Solo_Performance_(8).jpg" class="mw-file-description" title="The Kathak dance of northern India absorbed Persian and Central Asian influences during Mughal rule."><img alt="The Kathak dance of northern India absorbed Persian and Central Asian influences during Mughal rule." src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/Kathak_Solo_Performance_%288%29.jpg/500px-Kathak_Solo_Performance_%288%29.jpg" decoding="async" width="300" height="200" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/Kathak_Solo_Performance_%288%29.jpg/960px-Kathak_Solo_Performance_%288%29.jpg 1.5x" data-file-width="2000" data-file-height="1333" /></a></span></div>
			<div class="gallerytext"> The <i>Kathak</i> dance of northern India absorbed Persian and Central Asian influences during <a href="/wiki/Mughal_Empire" title="Mughal Empire">Mughal rule</a>.</div>
		</li>
</ul>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Clothing">Clothing</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Clothing_in_India" title="Clothing in India">Clothing in India</a></div>
<p>From ancient times until the advent of the modern, the most widely worn traditional dress in India was <a href="/wiki/Draped_garment" title="Draped garment">draped</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Tarlo1996-26&#95;435-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Tarlo1996-26-435"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>405<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> For women it took the form of a <a href="/wiki/Sari" title="Sari">sari</a>, a single piece of cloth many yards long.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Tarlo1996-26&#95;435-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Tarlo1996-26-435"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>405<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The sari was traditionally wrapped around the lower body and the shoulder.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Tarlo1996-26&#95;435-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Tarlo1996-26-435"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>405<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In its modern form, it is combined with an underskirt, or Indian <a href="/wiki/Petticoat#Asian_petticoats" title="Petticoat">petticoat</a>, and tucked in along the waist band for more secure fastening. It is also commonly worn with an Indian <a href="/wiki/Blouse" title="Blouse">blouse</a>, or <a href="/wiki/Choli" title="Choli">choli</a>, which serves as the primary upper-body garment, the sari's end—passing over the shoulder—covering the midriff and obscuring the upper body's contours.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Tarlo1996-26&#95;435-3" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Tarlo1996-26-435"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>405<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> For men, a similar but shorter length of cloth, the <a href="/wiki/Dhoti" title="Dhoti">dhoti</a>, has served as a lower-body garment.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Tarlo1996-26-28&#95;436-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Tarlo1996-26-28-436"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>406<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The use of stitched clothes became widespread after Muslim rule was established by the <a href="/wiki/Delhi_sultanate" class="mw-redirect" title="Delhi sultanate">Delhi sultanate</a> (<abbr title="circa">c.</abbr><span style="white-space:nowrap;">&#8201;1300 CE</span>) and continued by the <a href="/wiki/Mughal_Empire" title="Mughal Empire">Mughal Empire</a> (<abbr title="circa">c.</abbr><span style="white-space:nowrap;">&#8201;1525 CE</span>).<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Rahman-Alkazi2002&#95;437-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Rahman-Alkazi2002-437"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>407<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Among the garments introduced during this time and still commonly worn are: the <a href="/wiki/Shalwar_kameez" title="Shalwar kameez">shalwars</a> and <a href="/wiki/Pyjama" class="mw-redirect" title="Pyjama">pyjamas</a>, both styles of trousers, and the tunics <a href="/wiki/Kurta" title="Kurta">kurta</a> and <a href="/wiki/Kameez" class="mw-redirect" title="Kameez">kameez</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Rahman-Alkazi2002&#95;437-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Rahman-Alkazi2002-437"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>407<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Shalwars are atypically wide at the waist but narrow to a cuffed bottom. They are held up by a drawstring, which causes them to become pleated around the waist.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-StevensonWaite2011&#95;438-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-StevensonWaite2011-438"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>408<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> When the pants are cut quite narrow, on the <a href="/wiki/Grain_(textile)#Bias" title="Grain (textile)">bias</a>, they are called <a href="/wiki/Churidar" title="Churidar">churidars</a>. The kameez is a long shirt or tunic.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-StevensonWaite2011-b&#95;439-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-StevensonWaite2011-b-439"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>409<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Its side seams left open below the waistline.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-440" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-440"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>410<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The <a href="/wiki/Kurta" title="Kurta">kurta</a> is traditionally collarless and made of cotton or silk; it is worn plain or with embroidered decoration, such as <a href="/wiki/Chikankari" title="Chikankari">chikankari</a>; and typically falls to either just above or just below the wearer's knees.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Shukla2015&#95;441-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Shukla2015-441"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>411<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>In the last 50 years, fashions have changed a great deal in India. Increasingly, in urban northern India, the sari is no longer the apparel of everyday wear, though they remain popular on formal occasions. The traditional shalwar kameez is rarely worn by younger urban women, who favour churidars or jeans. In office settings, ubiquitous air conditioning allows men to wear sports jackets year-round. For weddings and formal occasions, men in the middle and upper classes often wear bandhgala, or short <a href="/wiki/Nehru_jackets" class="mw-redirect" title="Nehru jackets">Nehru jackets</a>, with pants, with the groom and his <a href="/wiki/Groomsmen" class="mw-redirect" title="Groomsmen">groomsmen</a> sporting <a href="/wiki/Sherwani" title="Sherwani">sherwanis</a> and churidars.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Dwyer2014&#95;442-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Dwyer2014-442"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>412<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<ul class="gallery mw-gallery-packed">

<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Cuisine">Cuisine</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Indian_cuisine" title="Indian cuisine">Indian cuisine</a></div>
<p>The foundation of a typical Indian meal is a cereal cooked plainly and complemented with savoury dishes.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Davidson2014-p409&#95;444-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Davidson2014-p409-444"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>414<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The cooked cereal could be steamed rice; <a href="/wiki/Chapati" title="Chapati">chapati</a>, a thin unleavened bread;<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Davidson2014-p161&#95;445-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Davidson2014-p161-445"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>415<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Idli" title="Idli">idli</a>, a steamed breakfast cake; or <a href="/wiki/Dosa_(food)" title="Dosa (food)">dosa</a>, a griddled pancake.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-tamang-yeast-idlidosa&#95;446-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-tamang-yeast-idlidosa-446"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>416<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The savoury dishes might include <a href="/wiki/Lentil" title="Lentil">lentils</a>, <a href="/wiki/Pulses" class="mw-redirect" title="Pulses">pulses</a>, vegetables, meat, poultry and fish commonly spiced with <a href="/wiki/Ginger_root" class="mw-redirect" title="Ginger root">ginger</a> and <a href="/wiki/Garlic" title="Garlic">garlic</a>, but also <a href="/wiki/Coriander" title="Coriander">coriander</a>, <a href="/wiki/Cumin" title="Cumin">cumin</a>, <a href="/wiki/Turmeric" title="Turmeric">turmeric</a>, <a href="/wiki/Cinnamon" title="Cinnamon">cinnamon</a>, <a href="/wiki/Cardamon" class="mw-redirect" title="Cardamon">cardamon</a> and others.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Davidson2014-p409&#95;444-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Davidson2014-p409-444"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>414<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In some instances, the ingredients may be mixed during the cooking process.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-jhala-princely-biryani&#95;447-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-jhala-princely-biryani-447"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>417<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> India has distinctive vegetarian cuisines, each a feature of the geographical and cultural histories of its adherents.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Davidson2014-p410&#95;448-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Davidson2014-p410-448"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>418<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> About 20% to 39% of India's population consists of vegetarians.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-449" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-449"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>419<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-450" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-450"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>420<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Although meat is eaten widely, the proportional consumption of meat is low.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-SahakianSaloma2016-50&#95;451-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-SahakianSaloma2016-50-451"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>421<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The most significant import of cooking techniques into India during the last millennium occurred during the <a href="/wiki/Mughal_Empire" title="Mughal Empire">Mughal Empire</a>, spreading into northern India from regions to its northwest,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-CollinghamCollingham2007&#95;452-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-CollinghamCollingham2007-452"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>422<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> along with dishes such as <a href="/wiki/Pilaf" title="Pilaf">pilaf</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTERoger2000&#95;453-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoger2000-453"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>423<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-sengupta-74&#95;454-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-sengupta-74-454"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>424<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Onions, garlic, almonds, and spices were added to the simple yogurt marinade of Persia.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-CollinghamCollingham2007&#95;452-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-CollinghamCollingham2007-452"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>422<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Rice was partially cooked and layered alternately with sauteed meat, the pot sealed tightly, and slow cooked according to another Persian cooking technique, to produce <a href="/wiki/Biryani" title="Biryani">biryani</a>,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-CollinghamCollingham2007&#95;452-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-CollinghamCollingham2007-452"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>422<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> a feature of festive dining in many parts of India.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-nandy2004&#95;455-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-nandy2004-455"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>425<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The diversity of Indian food served worldwide has been partially concealed by the dominance of <a href="/wiki/Punjabi_cuisine" title="Punjabi cuisine">Punjabi cuisine</a>. The popularity of <a href="/wiki/Tandoori_chicken" title="Tandoori chicken">tandoori chicken</a>—cooked in a <a href="/wiki/Tandoor" title="Tandoor">tandoor</a> oven, which had traditionally been used for baking bread in the rural Punjab and the Delhi region, especially among Muslims, but which is originally from <a href="/wiki/Central_Asia" title="Central Asia">Central Asia</a>—dates to the 1950s, and was caused in large part by an entrepreneurial response among people from the Punjab who had been displaced by the 1947 partition.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Davidson2014-p410&#95;448-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Davidson2014-p410-448"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>418<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<ul class="gallery mw-gallery-packed">
		<li class="gallerybox" style="width: 294px">
			<div class="thumb" style="width: 292px;"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:South_Indian_Thali_Cropped.jpg" class="mw-file-description" title="South Indian vegetarian thali, or platter"><img alt="South Indian vegetarian thali, or platter" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/98/South_Indian_Thali_Cropped.jpg/500px-South_Indian_Thali_Cropped.jpg" decoding="async" width="292" height="180" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/98/South_Indian_Thali_Cropped.jpg/960px-South_Indian_Thali_Cropped.jpg 1.5x" data-file-width="2567" data-file-height="1583" /></a></span></div>

			<div class="thumb" style="width: 266.66666666667px;"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:Pabda_Jhaal_-_Home-_Kolkata_-_West_Bengal.jpg" class="mw-file-description" title="Machher jhol, a spicy fish curry eaten in eastern India, Nepal, and Bangladesh"><img alt="Machher jhol, a spicy fish curry eaten in eastern India, Nepal, and Bangladesh" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/06/Pabda_Jhaal_-_Home-_Kolkata_-_West_Bengal.jpg/500px-Pabda_Jhaal_-_Home-_Kolkata_-_West_Bengal.jpg" decoding="async" width="267" height="180" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/06/Pabda_Jhaal_-_Home-_Kolkata_-_West_Bengal.jpg/960px-Pabda_Jhaal_-_Home-_Kolkata_-_West_Bengal.jpg 1.5x" data-file-width="5497" data-file-height="3710" /></a></span></div>

			<div class="thumb" style="width: 270px;"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:Khairdeen,_alias_Pritam,_relishing_a_mango_at_the_dusk_of_his_life_05.jpg" class="mw-file-description" title="Mango, the national fruit of India, is eaten widely in the summer months.[426][ae]"><img alt="Mango, the national fruit of India, is eaten widely in the summer months.[426][ae]" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8f/Khairdeen%2C_alias_Pritam%2C_relishing_a_mango_at_the_dusk_of_his_life_05.jpg/500px-Khairdeen%2C_alias_Pritam%2C_relishing_a_mango_at_the_dusk_of_his_life_05.jpg" decoding="async" width="270" height="180" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8f/Khairdeen%2C_alias_Pritam%2C_relishing_a_mango_at_the_dusk_of_his_life_05.jpg/960px-Khairdeen%2C_alias_Pritam%2C_relishing_a_mango_at_the_dusk_of_his_life_05.jpg 1.5x" data-file-width="6550" data-file-height="4373" /></a></span></div>
			<div class="gallerytext"><a href="/wiki/Mango" title="Mango">Mango</a>, the national fruit of India, is eaten widely in the summer months.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-mango-national-fruit&#95;456-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-mango-national-fruit-456"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>426<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-459" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-459"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>ae<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>  </div>
		</li>
</ul>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Sports">Sports</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Sport_in_India" title="Sport in India">Sport in India</a></div>
<p>Several traditional indigenous sports such as <i><a href="/wiki/Kabaddi" title="Kabaddi">kabaddi</a></i>, <i><a href="/wiki/Kho_kho" title="Kho kho">kho kho</a></i>, <i><a href="/wiki/Pehlwani" title="Pehlwani">pehlwani</a></i> and <i><a href="/wiki/Gilli-danda" class="mw-redirect" title="Gilli-danda">gilli-danda</a></i>, and also <a href="/wiki/Indian_martial_arts" title="Indian martial arts">martial arts</a>, such as <i><a href="/wiki/Kalarippayattu" class="mw-redirect" title="Kalarippayattu">Kalarippayattu</a></i> and <i><a href="/wiki/Marma_adi" class="mw-redirect" title="Marma adi">marma adi</a></i> remain popular. <a href="/wiki/Chess" title="Chess">Chess</a> is commonly held to have <a href="/wiki/History_of_chess#India" title="History of chess">originated in India</a> as <i><a href="/wiki/Chaturanga" title="Chaturanga">chaturaṅga</a></i>;<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEWolpert20032&#95;460-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWolpert20032-460"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>429<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> There has been a rise in the number of Indian <a href="/wiki/Grandmaster_(chess)" title="Grandmaster (chess)">grandmasters</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTERediff&#95;2008&#95;b&#95;461-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTERediff_2008_b-461"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>430<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Viswanathan_Anand" title="Viswanathan Anand">Viswanathan Anand</a> became the <a href="/wiki/World_Chess_Championship_2007" title="World Chess Championship 2007">undisputed Chess World Champion</a> in 2007 and held the status until 2013.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-462" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-462"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>431<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Parcheesi" title="Parcheesi">Parcheesi</a> is derived from <i><a href="/wiki/Pachisi" title="Pachisi">Pachisi</a></i> another traditional Indian pastime, which in early modern times was played on a giant marble court by <a href="/wiki/Mughal_Empire" title="Mughal Empire">Mughal emperor</a> <a href="/wiki/Akbar" title="Akbar">Akbar</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEBinmore200798&#95;463-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBinmore200798-463"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>432<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p><a href="/wiki/Cricket_in_India" title="Cricket in India">Cricket</a> is the most popular sport in India.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-464" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-464"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>433<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> <a href="/wiki/India_national_cricket_team" title="India national cricket team">India</a> is one of the more successful cricket teams, having won two <a href="/wiki/Cricket_World_Cup" title="Cricket World Cup">Cricket World Cups</a>, two <a href="/wiki/Men%27s_T20_World_Cup" title="Men&#39;s T20 World Cup">T20 World Cups</a>, and three <a href="/wiki/ICC_Champions_Trophy" title="ICC Champions Trophy">Champions Trophies</a>. <a href="/wiki/India_men%27s_national_field_hockey_team" title="India men&#39;s national field hockey team">India</a> has won a record eight <a href="/wiki/Field_hockey" title="Field hockey">field hockey</a> gold medals in the <a href="/wiki/Field_hockey_at_the_Summer_Olympics" title="Field hockey at the Summer Olympics">summer Olympics</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-465" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-465"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>434<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<ul class="gallery mw-gallery-packed">
		<li class="gallerybox" style="width: 292px">
			<div class="thumb" style="width: 290px;"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:Indian-Hockey-Team-Berlin-1936.jpg" class="mw-file-description" title="The Indian hockey team, captained by Dhyan Chand (standing second from left), after winning the finals at the 1936 Summer Olympics – their third of six consecutive Olympic golds"><img alt="The Indian hockey team, captained by Dhyan Chand (standing second from left), after winning the finals at the 1936 Summer Olympics – their third of six consecutive Olympic golds" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8e/Indian-Hockey-Team-Berlin-1936.jpg/500px-Indian-Hockey-Team-Berlin-1936.jpg" decoding="async" width="290" height="200" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/8/8e/Indian-Hockey-Team-Berlin-1936.jpg 1.5x" data-file-width="637" data-file-height="440" /></a></span></div>

		</li>

<li id="cite&#95;note-2"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-2">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text">Originally written in <a href="/wiki/Sadhu_bhasha" title="Sadhu bhasha">Sanskritised Bengali</a> and adopted as the national anthem in its Hindi translation</span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-7"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-7">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text">"[...] <i>Jana Gana Mana</i> is the National Anthem of India, subject to such alterations in the words as the Government may authorise as occasion arises; and the song <i>Vande Mataram</i>, which has played a historic part in the struggle for Indian freedom, shall be honoured equally with <i>Jana Gana Mana</i> and shall have equal status with it."<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEConstituent&#95;Assembly&#95;of&#95;India1950&#95;6-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConstituent_Assembly_of_India1950-6"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>5<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-8"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-8">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text">Written in a mixture of Sanskrit and <a href="/wiki/Sadhu_bhasha" title="Sadhu bhasha">Sanskritised Bengali</a></span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-11"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-11">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text">According to <a href="/wiki/Part_XVII_of_the_Constitution_of_India" class="mw-redirect" title="Part XVII of the Constitution of India">Part XVII of the Constitution of India</a>, <a href="/wiki/Standard_Hindi" class="mw-redirect" title="Standard Hindi">Hindi</a> in the <a href="/wiki/Devanagari" title="Devanagari">Devanagari</a> script is the <a href="/wiki/Official_language" title="Official language">official language</a> of the Union, along with <a href="/wiki/Indian_English" title="Indian English">English</a> as an additional official language.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTENational&#95;Informatics&#95;Centre2005&#95;1-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTENational_Informatics_Centre2005-1"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>1<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEMinistry&#95;of&#95;Home&#95;Affairs&#95;1960&#95;9-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMinistry_of_Home_Affairs_1960-9"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>6<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-india.gov.in2&#95;10-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-india.gov.in2-10"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>7<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> <a href="/wiki/States_and_union_territories_of_India" title="States and union territories of India">States and union territories</a> can have a different official language of their own other than Hindi or English.</span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-14"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-14">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text">Not all the state-level official languages are in the eighth schedule and not all the scheduled languages are state-level official languages. For example, the <a href="/wiki/Sindhi_language" title="Sindhi language">Sindhi language</a> is an 8th scheduled but not a state-level official language.</span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-15"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-15">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><a href="/wiki/Kashmiri_language" title="Kashmiri language">Kashmiri</a> and <a href="/wiki/Dogri_language" title="Dogri language">Dogri language</a> are the official languages of <a href="/wiki/Jammu_and_Kashmir_(union_territory)" title="Jammu and Kashmir (union territory)">Jammu and Kashmir</a> which is currently a <a href="/wiki/Union_territory" title="Union territory">union territory</a> and no longer the <a href="/wiki/Jammu_and_Kashmir_(state)" title="Jammu and Kashmir (state)">former state</a>.</span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-17"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-17">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text">
<ul><li>According to <a href="/wiki/Ethnologue" title="Ethnologue">Ethnologue</a>, there are 424 living indigenous languages in India, in contrast to 11 extinct indigenous languages. In addition, there are 30 living non-indigenous languages.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-16" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-16"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>10<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></li>
<li>Different sources give widely differing figures, primarily based on how the terms "language" and "dialect" are defined and grouped.</li></ul>
</span></li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-20"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-20">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text">"The country's exact size is subject to debate because some borders are disputed. The Indian government lists the total area as 3,287,260&#160;km<sup>2</sup> (1,269,220&#160;sq&#160;mi) and the total land area as 3,060,500&#160;km<sup>2</sup> (1,181,700&#160;sq&#160;mi); the United Nations lists the total area as 3,287,263&#160;km<sup>2</sup> (1,269,219&#160;sq&#160;mi) and total land area as 2,973,190&#160;km<sup>2</sup> (1,147,960&#160;sq&#160;mi)."<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTELibrary&#95;of&#95;Congress2004&#95;19-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTELibrary_of_Congress2004-19"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>12<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> </span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-27"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-27">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text">See <a href="/wiki/Date_and_time_notation_in_India" title="Date and time notation in India">Date and time notation in India</a>.</span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-29"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-29">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><a href="/wiki/ISO_15919" title="ISO 15919">ISO 15919</a>: <span title="ISO 15919 Indic (Hindi language) transliteration"><i lang="hi-Latn">Bhārat Gaṇarājya</i></span></span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-36"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-36">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text">The <a href="/wiki/Government_of_India" title="Government of India">Government of India</a> also regards <a href="/wiki/Afghanistan" title="Afghanistan">Afghanistan</a> as a bordering country, as it considers all of <a href="/wiki/Kashmir" title="Kashmir">Kashmir</a> to be part of India.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-35" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-35"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>25<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> However, this is <a href="/wiki/Kashmir_conflict" title="Kashmir conflict">disputed</a>, and the region bordering Afghanistan is administered by Pakistan.</span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-52"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-52">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text">"A Chinese pilgrim also recorded evidence of the caste system as he could observe it. According to this evidence the treatment meted out to untouchables such as the Chandalas was very similar to that which they experienced in later periods. This would contradict assertions that this rigid form of the caste system emerged in India only as a reaction to the Islamic conquest."<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEKulkeRothermund2016122–123&#95;51-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKulkeRothermund2016122–123-51"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>40<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-94"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-94">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text">"All these achievements are dwarfed, though, by the Sanskrit linguistic tradition culminating in the famous grammar by Pāṇini, known as the Aṣṭhādhyāyī. The elegance and comprehensiveness of its architecture have yet to be surpassed by any grammar of any language, and its ingenious methods of stratifying out use and mention, language and metalanguage, and theorem and metatheorem predate key discoveries in western philosophy by millennia."<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Evans-2009&#95;93-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Evans-2009-93"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>81<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-96"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-96">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text">the historic religion of the <a href="/wiki/Vedas" title="Vedas">Vedas</a>, and the precursor to <a href="/wiki/Hinduism" title="Hinduism">Hinduism</a></span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-100"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-100">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text">The Mauryan economy was helped by the rise of Buddhism and Jainism, creeds that promoted nonviolence, proscribed ostentation, or superfluous sacrifices and rituals, and reduced the costs of economic transactions; by coinage that increased economic accommodation in the region; and by the use of writing, which might have boosted more intricate business dealings.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTERoy201228&#95;99-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoy201228-99"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>85<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-102"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-102">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text">To promote movement and trade, the Mauryans built roads, most prominently a chiefly winter-time road—the Uttarapath—which connected eastern Afghanistan to their capital <a href="/wiki/Patliputra" class="mw-redirect" title="Patliputra">Pataliputra</a>, during the time of year when the water levels in the intersecting rivers were low, and they could be easily <a href="/wiki/Ford_(crossing)" title="Ford (crossing)">forded</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEIori2023184,&#95;219&#95;101-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIori2023184,_219-101"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>86<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-118"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-118">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text">When <a href="/wiki/Harsha" title="Harsha">Harsha</a> of <a href="/wiki/Kannauj" title="Kannauj">Kannauj</a>, who ruled much of the Indo-Gangetic Plain from 606 to 647&#160;<span title="Common Era">CE</span>, attempted to expand southwards, he was defeated by the <a href="/wiki/Chalukya_dynasty" title="Chalukya dynasty">Chalukya</a> ruler of the Deccan. When his successor attempted to expand eastwards, he was defeated by the <a href="/wiki/Pala_Empire" title="Pala Empire">Pala</a> king of <a href="/wiki/Bengal" title="Bengal">Bengal</a>. When the Chalukyas attempted to expand southwards, they were defeated by the <a href="/wiki/Pallava_dynasty" title="Pallava dynasty">Pallavas</a> from farther south, who in turn were opposed by the <a href="/wiki/Pandya_dynasty" title="Pandya dynasty">Pandyas</a> and the <a href="/wiki/Chola_dynasty" title="Chola dynasty">Cholas</a> from still farther south.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEStein2010115–116&#95;117-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStein2010115–116-117"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>101<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-124"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-124">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text">Central Asian warriors became supreme during South Asia’s medieval transition by deploying swift-horse cavalry skilled in firing arrows at full gallop, volley after volley.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTELudden201487&#95;123-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTELudden201487-123"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>106<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-190"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-190">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text">The northernmost point under Indian control is the disputed <a href="/wiki/Siachen_Glacier" title="Siachen Glacier">Siachen Glacier</a> in <a href="/wiki/Jammu_and_Kashmir_(union_territory)" title="Jammu and Kashmir (union territory)">Jammu and Kashmir</a>; however, the <a href="/wiki/Government_of_India" title="Government of India">Government of India</a> regards the entire region of the former princely state of Jammu and Kashmir, including <a href="/wiki/Gilgit-Baltistan" title="Gilgit-Baltistan">Gilgit-Baltistan</a> administered by Pakistan, to be its territory. It therefore assigns the latitude 37° 6′ to its northernmost point.</span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-214"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-214">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text">A biodiversity hotspot is a <a href="/wiki/Biogeography" title="Biogeography">biogeographical</a> region which has more than 1,500 <a href="/wiki/Vascular_plant" title="Vascular plant">vascular plant</a> species, but less than 30% of its primary habitat.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-SivaperumanVenkataraman2018&#95;213-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-SivaperumanVenkataraman2018-213"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>194<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-244"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-244">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text">During the last years of <a href="/wiki/British_Raj" title="British Raj">British rule</a>, elections were held in 1946 in all <a href="/wiki/Presidencies_and_provinces_of_British_India" title="Presidencies and provinces of British India">provinces of British India</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-241" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-241"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>221<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The Congress won electoral victories in eight of 11 provinces.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-242" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-242"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>222<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> A  Congress-led government was formed in September; Jawaharlal Nehru served as interim prime minister.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Gopal1976&#95;243-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Gopal1976-243"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>223<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-248"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-248">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text">Congress had the support of <a href="/wiki/Left-wing_politics" title="Left-wing politics">left-leaning</a> parties and MPs who opposed the BJP.</span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-337"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-337">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text">The 0% results from rounding to two decimal places.</span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-366"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-366">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text">Caste is a form of <a href="/wiki/Social_stratification" title="Social stratification">social stratification</a> characterised by <a href="/wiki/Endogamy" title="Endogamy">endogamy</a>, hereditary transmission of an occupation, ritual status in a hierarchy, and customary social interaction or exclusion defined by cultural notions of purity and pollution.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-caste-lead&#95;365-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-caste-lead-365"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>342<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-412"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-412">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text">Ancient and medieval Indian mathematical works, all composed in <a href="/wiki/Sanskrit" title="Sanskrit">Sanskrit</a> usually consisted of two sections: <i><a href="/wiki/Sutra" title="Sutra">sutras</a></i> in which a set of rules or problems were stated with economy in verse, and a prose commentary that explained the problem in more detail and provided justification for the solution.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-plofker&#95;410-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-plofker-410"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>386<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-filliozat-p140to143&#95;411-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-filliozat-p140to143-411"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>387<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-422"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-422">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text">Apart from geometric series</span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-429"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-429">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text">Among young urban middle-class women, for example, a proficiency in classical dance is sometimes a sought-after social achievement.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-vatsyayan-lord&#95;428-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-vatsyayan-lord-428"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>402<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-430"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-430">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text">The classical status increases a dance's visibility and attracts more funding from agencies and ticket purchases from audiences.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-vatsyayan-lord&#95;428-3" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-vatsyayan-lord-428"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>402<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-431"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-431">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text">Given the geographical distribution of the chosen dances and their stylistic range, the choices could be seen as a facet of India's ethos of national integration.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-vatsyayan-lord&#95;428-4" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-vatsyayan-lord-428"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>402<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-433"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-433">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text">As per the <a href="/wiki/Madras_Devadasis_(Prevention_of_Dedication)_Act" title="Madras Devadasis (Prevention of Dedication) Act">Madras Devadasis (Prevention of Dedication) Act</a> after agitation from the Indian middle and upper classes.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-vatsyayan-lord&#95;428-7" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-vatsyayan-lord-428"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>402<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-459"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-459">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text">Genetic studies have shown that mangos were first domesticated in the region between northeastern India, northwestern Myanmar, and Bangladesh.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Kuhn&#95;457-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Kuhn-457"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>427<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-Warschefsky&#95;458-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Warschefsky-458"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>428<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></span>
</li>
</ol></div></div>

<li id="cite&#95;note-31"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-31">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite id="CITEREFJamesSekher2024" class="citation book cs1">James, K. S.; Sekher, T. V. (2024). <a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://doi.org/10.1017/9781009318846.003">"India's Population Change: Critical Issues and Prospects."</a>. In James, K. S.; Sekher, T. V. (eds.). <i>India Population Report</i> (1st&#160;ed.). Cambridge: Cambridge University Press. pp.&#160;<span class="nowrap">1–</span>18. <a href="/wiki/Doi_(identifier)" class="mw-redirect" title="Doi (identifier)">doi</a>:<a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://doi.org/10.1017%2F9781009318846.003">10.1017/9781009318846.003</a>. <a href="/wiki/ISBN_(identifier)" class="mw-redirect" title="ISBN (identifier)">ISBN</a>&#160;<a href="/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-1-00-931886-0" title="Special:BookSources/978-1-00-931886-0"><bdi>978-1-00-931886-0</bdi></a>.</cite><span title="ctx&#95;ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft&#95;val&#95;fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Abook&amp;rft.genre=bookitem&amp;rft.atitle=India%27s+Population+Change%3A+Critical+Issues+and+Prospects.&amp;rft.btitle=India+Population+Report&amp;rft.place=Cambridge&amp;rft.pages=1-18&amp;rft.edition=1st&amp;rft.pub=Cambridge+University+Press&amp;rft.date=2024&amp;rft&#95;id=info%3Adoi%2F10.1017%2F9781009318846.003&amp;rft.isbn=978-1-00-931886-0&amp;rft.aulast=James&amp;rft.aufirst=K.+S.&amp;rft.au=Sekher%2C+T.+V.&amp;rft&#95;id=https%3A%2F%2Fdoi.org%2F10.1017%2F9781009318846.003&amp;rfr&#95;id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3AIndia" class="Z3988"></span></span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-32"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-32">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><a href="#CITEREFMetcalfMetcalf2012">Metcalf &amp; Metcalf 2012</a>, p.&#160;327: "Even though much remains to be done, especially in regard to eradicating poverty and securing effective structures of governance, India's achievements since independence in sustaining freedom and democracy have been singular among the world's new nations."</span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-stein-arnold-33"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-stein-arnold_33-0">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite id="CITEREFStein2012" class="citation cs1"><a href="/wiki/Burton_Stein" title="Burton Stein">Stein, Burton</a> (2012). <a href="/wiki/David_Arnold_(historian)" title="David Arnold (historian)">Arnold, David</a> (ed.). <i>A History of India</i>. The Blackwell History of the World Series (2nd&#160;ed.). Wiley-Blackwell. <q>One of these is the idea of India as 'the world's largest democracy', but a democracy forged less by the creation of representative institutions and expanding electorate under British rule than by the endeavours of India's founding fathers – Gandhi, Nehru, Patel and Ambedkar – and the labours of the Constituent Assembly between 1946 and 1949, embodied in the Indian constitution of 1950. This democratic order, reinforced by the regular holding of nationwide elections and polling for the state assemblies, has, it can be argued, consistently underpinned a fundamentally democratic state structure – despite the anomaly of the Emergency and the apparent durability of the Gandhi-Nehru dynasty.</q></cite><span title="ctx&#95;ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft&#95;val&#95;fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Abook&amp;rft.genre=book&amp;rft.btitle=A+History+of+India&amp;rft.series=The+Blackwell+History+of+the+World+Series&amp;rft.edition=2nd&amp;rft.pub=Wiley-Blackwell&amp;rft.date=2012&amp;rft.aulast=Stein&amp;rft.aufirst=Burton&amp;rfr&#95;id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3AIndia" class="Z3988"></span></span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-FOOTNOTEFisher2018184–185-34"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-FOOTNOTEFisher2018184–185_34-0">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><a href="#CITEREFFisher2018">Fisher 2018</a>, pp.&#160;184–185: "Since 1947, India's internal disputes over its national identity, while periodically bitter and occasionally punctuated by violence, have been largely managed with remarkable and sustained commitment to national unity and democracy."</span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-35"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-35">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite class="citation web cs1"><a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://web.archive.org/web/20150317182910/https://mha.nic.in/sites/upload_files/mha/files/BMIntro-1011.pdf">"Ministry of Home Affairs (Department of Border Management)"</a> <span class="cs1-format">(PDF)</span>. Archived from <a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://mha.nic.in/sites/upload_files/mha/files/BMIntro-1011.pdf">the original</a> <span class="cs1-format">(PDF)</span> on 17 March 2015<span class="reference-accessdate">. Retrieved <span class="nowrap">1 September</span> 2008</span>.</cite><span title="ctx&#95;ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft&#95;val&#95;fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Abook&amp;rft.genre=unknown&amp;rft.btitle=Ministry+of+Home+Affairs+%28Department+of+Border+Management%29&amp;rft&#95;id=https%3A%2F%2Fmha.nic.in%2Fsites%2Fupload&#95;files%2Fmha%2Ffiles%2FBMIntro-1011.pdf&amp;rfr&#95;id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3AIndia" class="Z3988"></span></span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-PetragliaAllchin-37"><span class="mw-cite-backlink">^ <a href="#cite_ref-PetragliaAllchin_37-0"><sup><i><b>a</b></i></sup></a> <a href="#cite_ref-PetragliaAllchin_37-1"><sup><i><b>b</b></i></sup></a></span> <span class="reference-text"><a href="#CITEREFPetragliaAllchin2007">Petraglia &amp; Allchin 2007</a>, p.&#160;<a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://books.google.com/books?id=Qm9GfjNlnRwC&amp;pg=PA10">10</a>: "Y-Chromosome and Mt-DNA data support the colonization of South Asia by modern humans originating in Africa. [...] Coalescence dates for most non-European populations average to between 73 and 55 ka."</span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-Dyson2018p1-38"><span class="mw-cite-backlink">^ <a href="#cite_ref-Dyson2018p1_38-0"><sup><i><b>a</b></i></sup></a> <a href="#cite_ref-Dyson2018p1_38-1"><sup><i><b>b</b></i></sup></a> <a href="#cite_ref-Dyson2018p1_38-2"><sup><i><b>c</b></i></sup></a></span> <span class="reference-text"><a href="#CITEREFDyson2018">Dyson 2018</a>, p.&#160;<a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://books.google.com/books?id=3TRtDwAAQBAJ&amp;pg=PA1">1</a>: "Modern human beings—<i>Homo sapiens</i>—originated in Africa. Then, intermittently, sometime between 60,000 and 80,000 years ago, tiny groups of them began to enter the north-west of the Indian subcontinent. It seems likely that initially they came by way of the coast. [...] it is virtually certain that there were <i>Homo sapiens</i> in the subcontinent 55,000 years ago, even though the earliest fossils that have been found of them date to only about 30,000 years before the present."</span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-Fisher2018p23-39"><span class="mw-cite-backlink">^ <a href="#cite_ref-Fisher2018p23_39-0"><sup><i><b>a</b></i></sup></a> <a href="#cite_ref-Fisher2018p23_39-1"><sup><i><b>b</b></i></sup></a></span> <span class="reference-text"><a href="#CITEREFFisher2018">Fisher 2018</a>, p.&#160;<a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://books.google.com/books?id=kZVuDwAAQBAJ&amp;pg=PA23">23</a>: "Scholars estimate that the first successful expansion of the <i>Homo sapiens</i> range beyond Africa and across the Arabian Peninsula occurred from as early as 80,000 years ago to as late as 40,000 years ago, although there may have been prior unsuccessful emigrations. Some of their descendants extended the human range ever further in each generation, spreading into each habitable land they encountered. One human channel was along the warm and productive coastal lands of the Persian Gulf and northern Indian Ocean. Eventually, various bands entered India between 75,000 years ago and 35,000 years ago."</span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-Dyson2018-28a-40"><span class="mw-cite-backlink">^ <a href="#cite_ref-Dyson2018-28a_40-0"><sup><i><b>a</b></i></sup></a> <a href="#cite_ref-Dyson2018-28a_40-1"><sup><i><b>b</b></i></sup></a></span> <span class="reference-text"><a href="#CITEREFDyson2018">Dyson 2018</a>, p.&#160;<a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://books.google.com/books?id=3TRtDwAAQBAJ&amp;pg=PA28">28</a></span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-Combined-2-41"><span class="mw-cite-backlink">^ <a href="#cite_ref-Combined-2_41-0"><sup><i><b>a</b></i></sup></a> <a href="#cite_ref-Combined-2_41-1"><sup><i><b>b</b></i></sup></a></span> <span class="reference-text">
<ul><li><a href="#CITEREFDyson2018">Dyson 2018</a>, pp.&#160;<a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://books.google.com/books?id=3TRtDwAAQBAJ&amp;pg=PA4">4–5</a>.</li>
<li><a href="#CITEREFFisher2018">Fisher 2018</a>, p.&#160;<a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://books.google.com/books?id=kZVuDwAAQBAJ&amp;pg=PA23">33</a>.</li></ul>
</span></li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-lowe1-42"><span class="mw-cite-backlink">^ <a href="#cite_ref-lowe1_42-0"><sup><i><b>a</b></i></sup></a> <a href="#cite_ref-lowe1_42-1"><sup><i><b>b</b></i></sup></a></span> <span class="reference-text"><a href="#CITEREFLowe2015">Lowe 2015</a>, pp.&#160;1–2: "<a href="/wiki/Rigveda" title="Rigveda">It</a> consists of 1,028 hymns (<i>sūktas</i>), highly crafted poetic compositions originally intended for recital during rituals and for the invocation of and communication with the Indo-Aryan gods. Modern scholarly opinion largely agrees that these hymns were composed between around 1500 BCE and 1200 BCE, during the eastward migration of the Indo-Aryan tribes from the mountains of what is today northern Afghanistan across the Punjab into north India."</span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-Combined-4-Rigveda-43"><span class="mw-cite-backlink">^ <a href="#cite_ref-Combined-4-Rigveda_43-0"><sup><i><b>a</b></i></sup></a> <a href="#cite_ref-Combined-4-Rigveda_43-1"><sup><i><b>b</b></i></sup></a></span> <span class="reference-text">
<ul><li><a href="#CITEREFWitzel2003">Witzel 2003</a>, pp.&#160;68–70: "It is known from internal evidence that the Vedic texts were orally composed in northern India, at first in the Greater Punjab and later on also in more eastern areas, including northern Bihar, between ca. 1500 BCE and ca. 500–400 BCE. The oldest text, the Rgveda, must have been more or less contemporary with the Mitanni texts of northern Syria/Iraq (1450–1350 BCE); [...] The Vedic texts were orally composed and transmitted, without the use of script, in an unbroken line of transmission from teacher to student that was formalised early on. This ensured an impeccable textual transmission superior to the classical texts of other cultures; it is in fact something of a <i>tape-recording</i> of ca. 1500–500 BCE. Not just the actual words, but even the long-lost musical (tonal) accent (as in old Greek or in Japanese) has been preserved up to the present. [...] The RV text was composed before the introduction and massive use of iron, that is before ca. 1200–1000 BCE."</li>
<li><a href="#CITEREFDoniger2014">Doniger 2014</a>, pp.&#160;xviii, 10: "A Chronology of Hinduism: ca. 1500–1000 BCE Rig Veda; ca. 1200–900 BCE Yajur Veda, Sama Veda and Atharva Veda [...] Hindu texts began with the <i>Rig Veda</i> ('Knowledge of Verses'), composed in northwest India around 1500 BCE; the first of the three Vedas, it is the earliest extant text composed in Sanskrit, the language of ancient India."</li>
<li><a href="#CITEREFLudden2014">Ludden 2014</a>, p.&#160;<a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://books.google.com/books?id=pBq9DwAAQBAJ&amp;pg=PA19">19</a></li>
<li><a href="#CITEREFDyson2018">Dyson 2018</a>, pp.&#160;<a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://books.google.com/books?id=3TRtDwAAQBAJ&amp;pg=PA14">14–15</a></li>
<li><a href="#CITEREFRobb2011">Robb 2011</a>, pp.&#160;<a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://books.google.com/books?id=GQ-2VH1LO_EC&amp;pg=PA46">46–</a></li></ul>
</span></li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-Combined-3-44"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-Combined-3_44-0">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text">* <a href="#CITEREFJamisonBrereton2020">Jamison &amp; Brereton 2020</a>, pp.&#160;2, 4–5: "The Ṛgveda is one of the four Vedas, which together constitute the oldest texts in Sanskrit and the earliest evidence for what will become Hinduism. [...] Although Vedic religion is very different in many regards from what is known as Classical Hinduism, the seeds are there. Gods like Viṣṇu and Śiva (under the name Rudra), who will become so dominant later, are already present in the Ṛgveda, though in roles both lesser than and different from those they will later play, and the principal Ṛgvedic gods like Indra remain in later Hinduism, though in diminished capacity."
<ul><li><a href="#CITEREFFlood2020">Flood 2020</a>, p.&#160;4, see note 4: "I take the term 'Hinduism' to meaningfully denote a range and history of practice characterised by a number of features, particularly reference to Vedic textual and sacrificial origins, belonging to endogamous social units (<i>jāti</i>/<i>varṇa</i>), participating in practices that involve making an offering to a deity and receiving a blessing (<i>pūjā</i>), and a first-level cultural polytheism (although many Hindus adhere to a second-level monotheism in which many gods are regarded as emanations or manifestations of the one, supreme being)."</li>
<li><a href="#CITEREFMichaels2017">Michaels 2017</a>, p.&#160;86: "Almost all traditional Hindu families observe until today at least three <i>samskaras</i> (initiation, marriage, and death ritual). Most other rituals have lost their popularity, are combined with other rites of passage, or are drastically shortened.&#160;Although <i>samskaras</i> vary from region to region, from class (<i>varna</i>) to class, and from caste to caste, their core elements remain the same owing to the common source, the Veda, and a common priestly tradition preserved by the <i>Brahmin</i> priests."</li>
<li><a href="#CITEREFFlood1996">Flood 1996</a>, p.&#160;35: "It is this Sanskrit, vedic, tradition which has maintained a continuity into modern times and which has provided the most important resource and inspiration for Hindu traditions and individuals.&#160;The Veda is the foundation for most later developments in what is known as Hinduism."</li></ul>
</span></li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-Combined-4-45"><span class="mw-cite-backlink">^ <a href="#cite_ref-Combined-4_45-0"><sup><i><b>a</b></i></sup></a> <a href="#cite_ref-Combined-4_45-1"><sup><i><b>b</b></i></sup></a></span> <span class="reference-text"><a href="#CITEREFDyson2018">Dyson 2018</a>, pp.&#160;<a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://books.google.com/books?id=3TRtDwAAQBAJ&amp;pg=PA16">16</a>, <a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://books.google.com/books?id=3TRtDwAAQBAJ&amp;pg=PA25">25</a>.</span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-Dyson2018-16a-46"><span class="mw-cite-backlink">^ <a href="#cite_ref-Dyson2018-16a_46-0"><sup><i><b>a</b></i></sup></a> <a href="#cite_ref-Dyson2018-16a_46-1"><sup><i><b>b</b></i></sup></a></span> <span class="reference-text"><a href="#CITEREFDyson2018">Dyson 2018</a>, p.&#160;<a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://books.google.com/books?id=3TRtDwAAQBAJ&amp;pg=PA16">16</a></span>
</li>

<li id="cite&#95;note-AsherAsher2006-152-59"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-AsherAsher2006-152_59-0">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><a href="#CITEREFAsherTalbot2006">Asher &amp; Talbot 2006</a>, p.&#160;<a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://books.google.com/books?id=ZvaGuaJIJgoC&amp;pg=PA152">152</a>.</span>
