</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-metcalf-metcalf-gandhi-1920-68"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-metcalf-metcalf-gandhi-1920_68-0">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><a href="#CITEREFMetcalfMetcalf2012">Metcalf &amp; Metcalf 2012</a>, p.&#160;202: "The year 1919 was a watershed in the modern history of India. [...] By its end the Montagu–Chelmsford reforms... were enacted. [...] The year, however, also brought the repressive Rowlatt bills and the catastrophe of the Amritsar massacre. For many, if not most, Indians the reforms had become a poisoned chalice. They chose instead a novel course of political action, that of 'non-violent non-cooperation', and a new leader, Mohandas K. Gandhi, only recently returned from twenty years in South Africa. Gandhi would endure as a lasting symbol of moral leadership for the entire world community."</span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-stein-arnold-successful-nationalism-69"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-stein-arnold-successful-nationalism_69-0">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><a href="#CITEREFStein2010">Stein 2010</a>, p.&#160;289: "Gandhi was the leading genius of the later and ultimately successful campaign for India's independence"</span>

<li id="cite&#95;note-86"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-86">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite id="CITEREFPaturiPatterson2016" class="citation book cs1">Paturi, Joseph; Patterson, Roger (2016). "Hinduism (with Hare Krishna)". In Hodge, Bodie; Patterson, Roger (eds.). <a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://books.google.com/books?id=oCo5DAAAQBAJ"><i>World Religions &amp; Cults Volume 2: Moralistic, Mythical and Mysticism Religions</i></a>. United States: New Leaf Publishing Group. pp.&#160;<span class="nowrap">59–</span>60. <a href="/wiki/ISBN_(identifier)" class="mw-redirect" title="ISBN (identifier)">ISBN</a>&#160;<a href="/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-0-89051-922-6" title="Special:BookSources/978-0-89051-922-6"><bdi>978-0-89051-922-6</bdi></a>. <q>The actual term Hindu first occurs as a Persian geographical term for the people who lived beyond the Indus River. The term Hindu originated as a geographical term and did not refer to a religion. Later, Hindu was taken by European languages from the Arabic term al-Hind, which referred to the people who lived across the Indus River. This Arabic term was itself taken from the Persian term Hindū, which refers to all Indians. By the 13th century, Hindustan emerged as a popular alternative name for India, meaning the "land of Hindus."<span class="cs1-kern-right"></span></q></cite><span title="ctx&#95;ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft&#95;val&#95;fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Abook&amp;rft.genre=bookitem&amp;rft.atitle=Hinduism+%28with+Hare+Krishna%29&amp;rft.btitle=World+Religions+%26+Cults+Volume+2%3A+Moralistic%2C+Mythical+and+Mysticism+Religions&amp;rft.place=United+States&amp;rft.pages=59-60&amp;rft.pub=New+Leaf+Publishing+Group&amp;rft.date=2016&amp;rft.isbn=978-0-89051-922-6&amp;rft.aulast=Paturi&amp;rft.aufirst=Joseph&amp;rft.au=Patterson%2C+Roger&amp;rft&#95;id=https%3A%2F%2Fbooks.google.com%2Fbooks%3Fid%3DoCo5DAAAQBAJ&amp;rfr&#95;id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3AIndia" class="Z3988"></span></span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-87"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-87">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite class="citation cs1"><a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://www.britannica.com/EBchecked/topic/266465/Hindustan">"Hindustan"</a>. <i><a href="/wiki/Encyclop%C3%A6dia_Britannica" title="Encyclopædia Britannica">Encyclopædia Britannica</a></i><span class="reference-accessdate">. Retrieved <span class="nowrap">17 July</span> 2011</span>.</cite><span title="ctx&#95;ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft&#95;val&#95;fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Abook&amp;rft.genre=bookitem&amp;rft.atitle=Hindustan&amp;rft.btitle=Encyclop%C3%A6dia+Britannica&amp;rft&#95;id=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.britannica.com%2FEBchecked%2Ftopic%2F266465%2FHindustan&amp;rfr&#95;id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3AIndia" class="Z3988"></span></span>

<li id="cite&#95;note-mattern-slow-moon-non-african-migration-88"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-mattern-slow-moon-non-african-migration_88-0">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><a href="#CITEREFMattern2019">Mattern 2019</a>, pp.&#160;146–147 All non-African human populations share a common ancestry in the recent past, about 80,000 to 50,000 years ago. Some time within this period, a group of modern humans migrated from Africa, traversed southern Asia, and crossed the sea to the ancient continent of Sahul (now Australia, Tasmania, and Papua New Guinea), picking up Neanderthal and Denisovan genes along the way; branches reached Europe somewhat later, and Americas last. This was not the first migration of anatomically modern humans out of Africa. ... But for the most part, earlier populations of humans around Eurasia sputtered out, returned to Africa, or were replaced by humans from the last migration out of Africa.}}</span>
</li>

<li id="cite&#95;note-Lowe2017-epic-95"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-Lowe2017-epic_95-0">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite id="CITEREFLowe2017" class="citation book cs1">Lowe, John J. (2017). <a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://books.google.com/books?id=nSgmDwAAQBAJ&amp;pg=PA58"><i>Transitive Nouns and Adjectives: Evidence from Early Indo-Aryan</i></a>. Oxford University Press. p.&#160;58. <a href="/wiki/ISBN_(identifier)" class="mw-redirect" title="ISBN (identifier)">ISBN</a>&#160;<a href="/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-0-19-879357-1" title="Special:BookSources/978-0-19-879357-1"><bdi>978-0-19-879357-1</bdi></a>. <q>The term 'Epic Sanskrit' refers to the language of the two great Sanskrit epics, the Mahābhārata and the Rāmāyaṇa. ...  It is likely, therefore, that the epic-like elements found in Vedic sources and the two epics that we have are not directly related, but that both drew on the same source, an oral tradition of storytelling that existed before, throughout, and after the Vedic period.</q></cite><span title="ctx&#95;ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft&#95;val&#95;fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Abook&amp;rft.genre=book&amp;rft.btitle=Transitive+Nouns+and+Adjectives%3A+Evidence+from+Early+Indo-Aryan&amp;rft.pages=58&amp;rft.pub=Oxford+University+Press&amp;rft.date=2017&amp;rft.isbn=978-0-19-879357-1&amp;rft.aulast=Lowe&amp;rft.aufirst=John+J.&amp;rft&#95;id=https%3A%2F%2Fbooks.google.com%2Fbooks%3Fid%3DnSgmDwAAQBAJ%26pg%3DPA58&amp;rfr&#95;id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3AIndia" class="Z3988"></span></span>

<li id="cite&#95;note-elverskog-2020-lead-1-106"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-elverskog-2020-lead-1_106-0">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite id="CITEREFElverskog2020" class="citation book cs1">Elverskog, Johan (2020). <i>The Buddha's Footprint: An Environmental History of Asia</i>. Philadelphia: University of Pennsylvania Press. p.&#160;56. <a href="/wiki/ISBN_(identifier)" class="mw-redirect" title="ISBN (identifier)">ISBN</a>&#160;<a href="/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-0-8122-5183-8" title="Special:BookSources/978-0-8122-5183-8"><bdi>978-0-8122-5183-8</bdi></a>. <q>The imperial edicts of Asoka echo this commodity view of trees.  In Pillar Edict V, Asoka decreed that "forests must not be burned without reason." The Buddhist community took this mandate further by declaring that in order to protect forests from such conflagrations monks were allowed to set counterfires</q></cite><span title="ctx&#95;ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft&#95;val&#95;fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Abook&amp;rft.genre=book&amp;rft.btitle=The+Buddha%27s+Footprint%3A+An+Environmental+History+of+Asia&amp;rft.place=Philadelphia&amp;rft.pages=56&amp;rft.pub=University+of+Pennsylvania+Press&amp;rft.date=2020&amp;rft.isbn=978-0-8122-5183-8&amp;rft.aulast=Elverskog&amp;rft.aufirst=Johan&amp;rfr&#95;id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3AIndia" class="Z3988"></span></span>

<li id="cite&#95;note-115"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-115">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite id="CITEREFLowe2017" class="citation book cs1">Lowe, John J. (2017). <a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://books.google.com/books?id=nSgmDwAAQBAJ&amp;pg=PA58"><i>Transitive Nouns and Adjectives: Evidence from Early Indo-Aryan</i></a>. <a href="/wiki/Oxford_University_Press" title="Oxford University Press">Oxford University Press</a>. p.&#160;58. <a href="/wiki/ISBN_(identifier)" class="mw-redirect" title="ISBN (identifier)">ISBN</a>&#160;<a href="/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-0-19-879357-1" title="Special:BookSources/978-0-19-879357-1"><bdi>978-0-19-879357-1</bdi></a>. <q>The term 'Epic Sanskrit' refers to the language of the two great Sanskrit epics, the Mahābhārata and the Rāmāyaṇa. ...  It is likely, therefore, that the epic-like elements found in Vedic sources and the two epics that we have are not directly related, but that both drew on the same source, an oral tradition of storytelling that existed before, throughout, and after the Vedic period.</q></cite><span title="ctx&#95;ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft&#95;val&#95;fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Abook&amp;rft.genre=book&amp;rft.btitle=Transitive+Nouns+and+Adjectives%3A+Evidence+from+Early+Indo-Aryan&amp;rft.pages=58&amp;rft.pub=Oxford+University+Press&amp;rft.date=2017&amp;rft.isbn=978-0-19-879357-1&amp;rft.aulast=Lowe&amp;rft.aufirst=John+J.&amp;rft&#95;id=https%3A%2F%2Fbooks.google.com%2Fbooks%3Fid%3DnSgmDwAAQBAJ%26pg%3DPA58&amp;rfr&#95;id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3AIndia" class="Z3988"></span></span>
</li>

<li id="cite&#95;note-exp-middle-class-173"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-exp-middle-class_173-0">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite id="CITEREFJavalgiGrossman2016" class="citation book cs1">Javalgi, Rajshekhar (Raj) G.; Grossman, David A. (2016). "7 Understanding the Characteristics and Entrepreneurial Activities of Middle-Class Consumers in Emerging Markets: The Case of India". In Marsh, Leslie L.; Li, Hongmei (eds.). <i>The Middle Class in Emerging Societies: Consumers, Lifestyles and Markets</i>. Routledge Research in Cultural and Media Studies Series. Routledge. <a href="/wiki/Doi_(identifier)" class="mw-redirect" title="Doi (identifier)">doi</a>:<a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://doi.org/10.4324%2F978135717692-10">10.4324/978135717692-10</a> (inactive 8 December 2025). <a href="/wiki/ISBN_(identifier)" class="mw-redirect" title="ISBN (identifier)">ISBN</a>&#160;<a href="/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-1-138-85882-4" title="Special:BookSources/978-1-138-85882-4"><bdi>978-1-138-85882-4</bdi></a>. <q>Between now and 2039, India is projected to add over 1 billion people to the global middle class creating the world's fifth-largest consumer market (Dobbs).  ... India's middle class saw its largest growth during the early 1990s when economic reforms led to integration into global markets.  As Western countries were experiencing economic contraction, India's economy continued to grow above 5 percent.</q></cite><span title="ctx&#95;ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft&#95;val&#95;fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Abook&amp;rft.genre=bookitem&amp;rft.atitle=7+Understanding+the+Characteristics+and+Entrepreneurial+Activities+of+Middle-Class+Consumers+in+Emerging+Markets%3A+The+Case+of+India&amp;rft.btitle=The+Middle+Class+in+Emerging+Societies%3A+Consumers%2C+Lifestyles+and+Markets&amp;rft.series=Routledge+Research+in+Cultural+and+Media+Studies+Series&amp;rft.pub=Routledge&amp;rft.date=2016&amp;rft&#95;id=info%3Adoi%2F10.4324%2F978135717692-10&amp;rft.isbn=978-1-138-85882-4&amp;rft.aulast=Javalgi&amp;rft.aufirst=Rajshekhar+%28Raj%29+G.&amp;rft.au=Grossman%2C+David+A.&amp;rfr&#95;id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3AIndia" class="Z3988"></span><span class="cs1-maint citation-comment"><code class="cs1-code">{{<a href="/wiki/Template:Cite_book" title="Template:Cite book">cite book</a>}}</code>:  CS1 maint: DOI inactive as of December 2025 (<a href="/wiki/Category:CS1_maint:_DOI_inactive_as_of_December_2025" title="Category:CS1 maint: DOI inactive as of December 2025">link</a>)</span></span>

<li id="cite&#95;note-FOOTNOTEDikshitSchwartzberg20237-183"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-FOOTNOTEDikshitSchwartzberg20237_183-0">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><a href="#CITEREFDikshitSchwartzberg2023">Dikshit &amp; Schwartzberg 2023</a>, p.&#160;7.</span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-FOOTNOTEPrakash&#95;et&#95;al.2000-184"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-FOOTNOTEPrakash_et_al.2000_184-0">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><a href="#CITEREFPrakash_et_al.2000">Prakash et al. 2000</a>.</span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-aravalli-185"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-aravalli_185-0">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><a href="#CITEREFKaul1970">Kaul 1970</a>, p.&#160;160, "&#160;The Aravalli range boldy defines the eastern limit of the arid and semi-arid zone. Probably the more humid conditions that prevail near the Aravallis prevented the extension of aridity towards the east and the Ganges Valley. It is noteworthy that, wherever there are gaps in this range, sand has advanced to the east of it."</span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-prasad-aravalli-186"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-prasad-aravalli_186-0">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><a href="#CITEREFPrasad1974">Prasad 1974</a>, p.&#160;372, "&#160;The topography of the Indian Desert is dominated by the Aravalli Ranges on its eastern border, which consist largely of tightly folded and highly metamorphosed Archaean rocks."</span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-fisher-aravalli-187"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-fisher-aravalli_187-0">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><a href="#CITEREFFisher2018">Fisher 2018</a>, p.&#160;83, "&#160;East of the lower Indus lay the inhospitable Rann of Kutch and Thar Desert. East of the upper Indus lay the more promising but narrow corridor between the Himalayan foothills on the north and the Thar Desert and Aravalli Mountains on the south. At the strategic choke point, just before reaching the fertile, well-watered Gangetic plain, sat Delhi. On this site, where life giving streams running off the most northern spur of the rocky Aravalli ridge flowed into the Jumna river, and where the war-horse and war-elephant trade intersected, a series of dynasties built fortified capitals."</span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-FOOTNOTEDikshitSchwartzberg20238-188"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-FOOTNOTEDikshitSchwartzberg20238_188-0">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><a href="#CITEREFDikshitSchwartzberg2023">Dikshit &amp; Schwartzberg 2023</a>, p.&#160;8.</span>

<li id="cite&#95;note-FOOTNOTETritsch200111–12-217"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-FOOTNOTETritsch200111–12_217-0">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><a href="#CITEREFTritsch2001">Tritsch 2001</a>, pp.&#160;11–12.</span>

<li id="cite&#95;note-218"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-218">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><a href="#CITEREFTritsch2001">Tritsch 2001</a>, p.&#160;12: "India has two natural zones of thorn forest, one in the rain shadow area of the Deccan Plateau east of the Western Ghats, and the other in the western part of the Indo-Gangetic plain.  Growth is limited only by moisture availability in these areas, so with irrigation the fertile alluvial soil of Punjab and Haryana has been turned into India's prime agricultural area.  Much of the thorn forest covering the plains probably had savannah-like features now no longer visible."</span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-Goyal2006-219"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-Goyal2006_219-0">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite id="CITEREFGoyal2006" class="citation cs1">Goyal, Anupam (2006). <a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://books.google.com/books?id=UTGQAAAAMAAJ&amp;pg=PA295"><i>The WTO and International Environmental Law: Towards Conciliation</i></a>. <a href="/wiki/Oxford_University_Press" title="Oxford University Press">Oxford University Press</a>. p.&#160;295. <a href="/wiki/ISBN_(identifier)" class="mw-redirect" title="ISBN (identifier)">ISBN</a>&#160;<a href="/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-0-19-567710-2" title="Special:BookSources/978-0-19-567710-2"><bdi>978-0-19-567710-2</bdi></a>.</cite><span title="ctx&#95;ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft&#95;val&#95;fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Abook&amp;rft.genre=book&amp;rft.btitle=The+WTO+and+International+Environmental+Law%3A+Towards+Conciliation&amp;rft.pages=295&amp;rft.pub=Oxford+University+Press&amp;rft.date=2006&amp;rft.isbn=978-0-19-567710-2&amp;rft.aulast=Goyal&amp;rft.aufirst=Anupam&amp;rft&#95;id=https%3A%2F%2Fbooks.google.com%2Fbooks%3Fid%3DUTGQAAAAMAAJ%26pg%3DPA295&amp;rfr&#95;id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3AIndia" class="Z3988"></span> Quote: "The Indian government successfully argued that the medicinal <i>neem</i> tree is part of traditional Indian knowledge. (page 295)"</span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-Hughes2013-220"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-Hughes2013_220-0">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite id="CITEREFHughes2013" class="citation cs1">Hughes, Julie E. (2013). <a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://books.google.com/books?id=RL8qWNmpkc0C&amp;pg=PT106"><i>Animal Kingdoms</i></a>. <a href="/wiki/Harvard_University_Press" title="Harvard University Press">Harvard University Press</a>. p.&#160;106. <a href="/wiki/ISBN_(identifier)" class="mw-redirect" title="ISBN (identifier)">ISBN</a>&#160;<a href="/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-0-674-07480-4" title="Special:BookSources/978-0-674-07480-4"><bdi>978-0-674-07480-4</bdi></a>. <q>At same time, the leafy pipal trees and comparative abundance that marked the Mewari landscape fostered refinements unattainable in other lands.</q></cite><span title="ctx&#95;ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft&#95;val&#95;fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Abook&amp;rft.genre=book&amp;rft.btitle=Animal+Kingdoms&amp;rft.pages=106&amp;rft.pub=Harvard+University+Press&amp;rft.date=2013&amp;rft.isbn=978-0-674-07480-4&amp;rft.aulast=Hughes&amp;rft.aufirst=Julie+E.&amp;rft&#95;id=https%3A%2F%2Fbooks.google.com%2Fbooks%3Fid%3DRL8qWNmpkc0C%26pg%3DPT106&amp;rfr&#95;id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3AIndia" class="Z3988"></span></span>

<li id="cite&#95;note-AmeriCostello2018-221"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-AmeriCostello2018_221-0">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite id="CITEREFAmeri2018" class="citation cs1">Ameri, Marta (2018). "Letting the Pictures Speak: An Image-Based Approach to the Mythological and Narrative Imagery of the Harappan World". In Ameri, Marta; Costello, Sarah Kielt; Jamison, Gregg; Scott, Sarah Jarmer (eds.). <a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://books.google.com/books?id=SklVDwAAQBAJ&amp;pg=PA156"><i>Seals and Sealing in the Ancient World: Case Studies from the Near East, Egypt, the Aegean, and South Asia</i></a>. <a href="/wiki/Cambridge_University_Press" title="Cambridge University Press">Cambridge University Press</a>. pp.&#160;<span class="nowrap">156–</span>157. <a href="/wiki/ISBN_(identifier)" class="mw-redirect" title="ISBN (identifier)">ISBN</a>&#160;<a href="/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-1-108-17351-3" title="Special:BookSources/978-1-108-17351-3"><bdi>978-1-108-17351-3</bdi></a>.</cite><span title="ctx&#95;ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft&#95;val&#95;fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Abook&amp;rft.genre=bookitem&amp;rft.atitle=Letting+the+Pictures+Speak%3A+An+Image-Based+Approach+to+the+Mythological+and+Narrative+Imagery+of+the+Harappan+World&amp;rft.btitle=Seals+and+Sealing+in+the+Ancient+World%3A+Case+Studies+from+the+Near+East%2C+Egypt%2C+the+Aegean%2C+and+South+Asia&amp;rft.pages=156-157&amp;rft.pub=Cambridge+University+Press&amp;rft.date=2018&amp;rft.isbn=978-1-108-17351-3&amp;rft.aulast=Ameri&amp;rft.aufirst=Marta&amp;rft&#95;id=https%3A%2F%2Fbooks.google.com%2Fbooks%3Fid%3DSklVDwAAQBAJ%26pg%3DPA156&amp;rfr&#95;id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3AIndia" class="Z3988"></span> Quote: "The last of the centaurs has the long, wavy, horizontal horns of a markhor, a human face, a heavy-set body that appears bovine, and a goat tail ... This figure is often depicted by itself, but it is also consistently represented in scenes that seem to reflect the adoration of a figure in a pipal tree or arbour and which may be termed ritual. These include fully detailed scenes like that visible in the large 'divine adoration' seal from Mohenjo-daro."</span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-Gwynne2011-222"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-Gwynne2011_222-0">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite id="CITEREFPaul&#95;Gwynne2011" class="citation cs1">Paul Gwynne (2011). <a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://books.google.com/books?id=tdsRKc_knZoC&amp;pg=RA5-PT195"><i>World Religions in Practice: A Comparative Introduction</i></a>. <a href="/wiki/John_Wiley_%26_Sons" class="mw-redirect" title="John Wiley &amp; Sons">John Wiley &amp; Sons</a>. p.&#160;358. <a href="/wiki/ISBN_(identifier)" class="mw-redirect" title="ISBN (identifier)">ISBN</a>&#160;<a href="/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-1-4443-6005-9" title="Special:BookSources/978-1-4443-6005-9"><bdi>978-1-4443-6005-9</bdi></a>. <q>The tree under which Sakyamuni became the Buddha is a peepal tree (<i><a href="/wiki/Ficus_religiosa" title="Ficus religiosa">Ficus religiosa</a></i>).</q></cite><span title="ctx&#95;ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft&#95;val&#95;fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Abook&amp;rft.genre=book&amp;rft.btitle=World+Religions+in+Practice%3A+A+Comparative+Introduction&amp;rft.pages=358&amp;rft.pub=John+Wiley+%26+Sons&amp;rft.date=2011&amp;rft.isbn=978-1-4443-6005-9&amp;rft.au=Paul+Gwynne&amp;rft&#95;id=https%3A%2F%2Fbooks.google.com%2Fbooks%3Fid%3DtdsRKc&#95;knZoC%26pg%3DRA5-PT195&amp;rfr&#95;id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3AIndia" class="Z3988"></span></span>

<li id="cite&#95;note-229"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-229">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite id="CITEREFFrost,&#95;Darrel&#95;R.2015" class="citation web cs1">Frost, Darrel R. (2015). <a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://research.amnh.org/vz/herpetology/amphibia/Amphibia/Anura/Bufonidae/Duttaphrynus/Duttaphrynus-beddomii">"<i>Duttaphrynus beddomii</i> (Günther, 1876)"</a>. <i>Amphibian Species of the World: an Online Reference. <li id="cite&#95;note-LovetteFitzpatrick2016-231"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-LovetteFitzpatrick2016_231-0">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite id="CITEREFLovetteFitzpatrick2016" class="citation cs1">Lovette, Irby J.; Fitzpatrick, John W. (2016). <a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://books.google.com/books?id=OGyQDAAAQBAJ&amp;pg=PA599"><i>Handbook of Bird Biology</i></a>. <a href="/wiki/John_Wiley_%26_Sons" class="mw-redirect" title="John Wiley &amp; Sons">John Wiley &amp; Sons</a>. p.&#160;599. <a href="/wiki/ISBN_(identifier)" class="mw-redirect" title="ISBN (identifier)">ISBN</a>&#160;<a href="/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-1-118-29105-4" title="Special:BookSources/978-1-118-29105-4"><bdi>978-1-118-29105-4</bdi></a>.</cite><span title="ctx&#95;ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft&#95;val&#95;fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Abook&amp;rft.genre=book&amp;rft.btitle=Handbook+of+Bird+Biology&amp;rft.pages=599&amp;rft.pub=John+Wiley+%26+Sons&amp;rft.date=2016&amp;rft.isbn=978-1-118-29105-4&amp;rft.aulast=Lovette&amp;rft.aufirst=Irby+J.&amp;rft.au=Fitzpatrick%2C+John+W.&amp;rft&#95;id=https%3A%2F%2Fbooks.google.com%2Fbooks%3Fid%3DOGyQDAAAQBAJ%26pg%3DPA599&amp;rfr&#95;id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3AIndia" class="Z3988"></span></span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-232"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-232">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><a href="#CITEREFTritsch2001">Tritsch 2001</a>, p.&#160;15: "Before it was so heavily settled and intensively exploited, the Punjab was dominated by thorn forest interspersed by rolling grasslands which were grazed on by millions of Blackbuck, accompanied by their dominant predator, the Cheetah. Always keen hunters, the Moghul princes kept tame cheetahs which were used to chase and bring down the Blackbuck. Today the Cheetah is extinct in India and the severely endangered Blackbuck no longer exists in the Punjab."</span>
</li>

<li id="cite&#95;note-storm-india-commonwealth-267"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-storm-india-commonwealth_267-0">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite id="CITEREFStorm2024" class="citation book cs1">Storm, Eric (2024). <a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://books.google.com/books?id=YfEIEQAAQBAJ&amp;pg=PA234"><i>Nationalism: A World History</i></a>. Princeton University Press. <a href="/wiki/ISBN_(identifier)" class="mw-redirect" title="ISBN (identifier)">ISBN</a>&#160;<a href="/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-0-691-23309-3" title="Special:BookSources/978-0-691-23309-3"><bdi>978-0-691-23309-3</bdi></a>. <a href="/wiki/LCCN_(identifier)" class="mw-redirect" title="LCCN (identifier)">LCCN</a>&#160;<a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://lccn.loc.gov/2024015293">2024015293</a>. <q>The whole constellation changed when India and Pakistan gained independence as dominions, which was a solution that made possible a speedier withdrawal of the British.  In the end, India decided to become a republic, although the leadership preferred to remain within the Commonwealth.</q></cite><span title="ctx&#95;ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft&#95;val&#95;fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Abook&amp;rft.genre=book&amp;rft.btitle=Nationalism%3A+A+World+History&amp;rft.pub=Princeton+University+Press&amp;rft.date=2024&amp;rft&#95;id=info%3Alccn%2F2024015293&amp;rft.isbn=978-0-691-23309-3&amp;rft.aulast=Storm&amp;rft.aufirst=Eric&amp;rft&#95;id=https%3A%2F%2Fbooks.google.com%2Fbooks%3Fid%3DYfEIEQAAQBAJ%26pg%3DPA234&amp;rfr&#95;id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3AIndia" class="Z3988"></span></span>

<li id="cite&#95;note-268"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-268">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite class="citation web cs1"><a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://web.archive.org/web/20100706045924/http://www.thecommonwealth.org/document/181889/34293/35468/214257/londondeclaration.htm">"The London Declaration"</a>. <li id="cite&#95;note-62-humiliation-270"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-62-humiliation_270-0">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text">(a) <link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite id="CITEREFGuyot-Rechard2017" class="citation cs1">Guyot-Rechard, Berenice (2017). <a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://books.google.com/books?id=FbktDQAAQBAJ&amp;pg=PA235"><i>Shadow States: India, China and the Himalayas, 1910–1962</i></a>. Cambridge University Press. p.&#160;235. <a href="/wiki/ISBN_(identifier)" class="mw-redirect" title="ISBN (identifier)">ISBN</a>&#160;<a href="/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-1-107-17679-9" title="Special:BookSources/978-1-107-17679-9"><bdi>978-1-107-17679-9</bdi></a>. <q>By invading NEFA, the PRC did not just aim to force a humiliated India to recognise its possession of the Aksai Chin.  It also hoped to get, once and for all, the upper hand in their shadowing competition.</q></cite><span title="ctx&#95;ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft&#95;val&#95;fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Abook&amp;rft.genre=book&amp;rft.btitle=Shadow+States%3A+India%2C+China+and+the+Himalayas%2C+1910%E2%80%931962&amp;rft.pages=235&amp;rft.pub=Cambridge+University+Press&amp;rft.date=2017&amp;rft.isbn=978-1-107-17679-9&amp;rft.aulast=Guyot-Rechard&amp;rft.aufirst=Berenice&amp;rft&#95;id=https%3A%2F%2Fbooks.google.com%2Fbooks%3Fid%3DFbktDQAAQBAJ%26pg%3DPA235&amp;rfr&#95;id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3AIndia" class="Z3988"></span>
<br />(b) <link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite id="CITEREFChubb2021" class="citation cs1">Chubb, Andrew (2021). <a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://books.google.com/books?id=D1crEAAAQBAJ&amp;pg=PA230">"The Sino-Indian Border Crisis: Chinese Perceptions of Indian Nationalism"</a>. In Golley, Jane; Jaivan, Linda; Strange, Sharon (eds.). <i>Crisis</i>. Australian National University Press. pp.&#160;<span class="nowrap">231–</span>232. <a href="/wiki/ISBN_(identifier)" class="mw-redirect" title="ISBN (identifier)">ISBN</a>&#160;<a href="/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-1-76046-439-4" title="Special:BookSources/978-1-76046-439-4"><bdi>978-1-76046-439-4</bdi></a>. <q>The ensuing cycle of escalation culminated in the 1962 Sino-Indian border war in which Mao Zedong's troops overran almost the entire state of Arunachal Pradesh in the eastern sector before unilaterally withdrawing, as if to underline the insult; most of the war's several thousand casualties were Indian. The PLA's decisive victories in the 1962 war not only humiliated the Indian Army, they also entrenched a status quo in Ladakh that was highly unfavourable for India, in which China controls almost all of the disputed territory. A nationalistic press and commentariat have kept 1962 vivid in India's popular consciousness.</q></cite><span title="ctx&#95;ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft&#95;val&#95;fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Abook&amp;rft.genre=bookitem&amp;rft.atitle=The+Sino-Indian+Border+Crisis%3A+Chinese+Perceptions+of+Indian+Nationalism&amp;rft.btitle=Crisis&amp;rft.pages=231-232&amp;rft.pub=Australian+National+University+Press&amp;rft.date=2021&amp;rft.isbn=978-1-76046-439-4&amp;rft.aulast=Chubb&amp;rft.aufirst=Andrew&amp;rft&#95;id=https%3A%2F%2Fbooks.google.com%2Fbooks%3Fid%3DD1crEAAAQBAJ%26pg%3DPA230&amp;rfr&#95;id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3AIndia" class="Z3988"></span>
<br />(c) <link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite id="CITEREFLintner2018" class="citation cs1">Lintner, Bertil (2018). <a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://books.google.com/books?id=-L9DDwAAQBAJ&amp;pg=PT106"><i>China's India War: Collision Course on the Roof of the World</i></a>. Oxford University Press. <a href="/wiki/ISBN_(identifier)" class="mw-redirect" title="ISBN (identifier)">ISBN</a>&#160;<a href="/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-0-19-909163-8" title="Special:BookSources/978-0-19-909163-8"><bdi>978-0-19-909163-8</bdi></a>. <q>Lin Biao was put in charge of the operation and that alliance between Mao and his loyal de facto chief of the PLA made the attack on India possible. With China's ultimate victory in the war, Mao's ultra-leftist line had won in China; whatever critical voices that were left in the Party after all the purges fell silent.</q></cite><span title="ctx&#95;ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft&#95;val&#95;fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Abook&amp;rft.genre=book&amp;rft.btitle=China%27s+India+War%3A+Collision+Course+on+the+Roof+of+the+World&amp;rft.pub=Oxford+University+Press&amp;rft.date=2018&amp;rft.isbn=978-0-19-909163-8&amp;rft.aulast=Lintner&amp;rft.aufirst=Bertil&amp;rft&#95;id=https%3A%2F%2Fbooks.google.com%2Fbooks%3Fid%3D-L9DDwAAQBAJ%26pg%3DPT106&amp;rfr&#95;id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3AIndia" class="Z3988"></span>
<br />(d) <link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite id="CITEREFMedcalf2020" class="citation cs1">Medcalf, Rory (2020). <a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://books.google.com/books?id=RCjXDwAAQBAJ&amp;pg=PT81"><i>Indo-Pacific Empire: China, America and the contest for the world's pivotal</i></a>. Manchester University Press. <a href="/wiki/ISBN_(identifier)" class="mw-redirect" title="ISBN (identifier)">ISBN</a>&#160;<a href="/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-1-5261-5077-6" title="Special:BookSources/978-1-5261-5077-6"><bdi>978-1-5261-5077-6</bdi></a>. <q>From an Indian perspective, the China-India war of 1962 was a shocking betrayal of the principles of co-operation and coexistence: a surprise attack that humiliated India and personally broke Nehru.</q></cite><span title="ctx&#95;ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft&#95;val&#95;fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Abook&amp;rft.genre=book&amp;rft.btitle=Indo-Pacific+Empire%3A+China%2C+America+and+the+contest+for+the+world%27s+pivotal&amp;rft.pub=Manchester+University+Press&amp;rft.date=2020&amp;rft.isbn=978-1-5261-5077-6&amp;rft.aulast=Medcalf&amp;rft.aufirst=Rory&amp;rft&#95;id=https%3A%2F%2Fbooks.google.com%2Fbooks%3Fid%3DRCjXDwAAQBAJ%26pg%3DPT81&amp;rfr&#95;id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3AIndia" class="Z3988"></span>
<br />(e) <link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite id="CITEREFGanguly1997" class="citation cs1">Ganguly, Sumit (1997). <a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://books.google.com/books?id=Fi66mjIqR1IC&amp;pg=PA44"><i>The Crisis in Kashmir: Portents of War, Hope of Peace</i></a>. Woodrow Wilson Center Press and Cambridge University Press. p.&#160;44. <a href="/wiki/ISBN_(identifier)" class="mw-redirect" title="ISBN (identifier)">ISBN</a>&#160;<a href="/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-0-521-65566-8" title="Special:BookSources/978-0-521-65566-8"><bdi>978-0-521-65566-8</bdi></a>. <q>In October 1962 India suffered the most humiliating military debacle in its post-independence history, at the hands of the Chinese People's Liberation Army (PLA). The outcome of this conflict had far-reaching consequences for Indian foreign and defence policies. The harsh defeat that the Chinese PLA had inflicted on the Indian Army called into question some of the most deeply held precepts of Nehru's foreign and defence policies.</q></cite><span title="ctx&#95;ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft&#95;val&#95;fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Abook&amp;rft.genre=book&amp;rft.btitle=The+Crisis+in+Kashmir%3A+Portents+of+War%2C+Hope+of+Peace&amp;rft.pages=44&amp;rft.pub=Woodrow+Wilson+Center+Press+and+Cambridge+University+Press&amp;rft.date=1997&amp;rft.isbn=978-0-521-65566-8&amp;rft.aulast=Ganguly&amp;rft.aufirst=Sumit&amp;rft&#95;id=https%3A%2F%2Fbooks.google.com%2Fbooks%3Fid%3DFi66mjIqR1IC%26pg%3DPA44&amp;rfr&#95;id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3AIndia" class="Z3988"></span>
<br />(f) <link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite id="CITEREFRaghavan2019" class="citation cs1">Raghavan, Srinath (2019). <a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://books.google.com/books?id=h-yoDwAAQBAJ&amp;pg=PA121">"A Missed Opportunity? The Nehru-Zhou Enlai Summit of 1960"</a>. In Bhagavan, Manu (ed.). <i>India and the Cold War</i>. University of North Carolina Press. p.&#160;121. <a href="/wiki/ISBN_(identifier)" class="mw-redirect" title="ISBN (identifier)">ISBN</a>&#160;<a href="/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-1-4696-5117-0" title="Special:BookSources/978-1-4696-5117-0"><bdi>978-1-4696-5117-0</bdi></a>. <q>The 'forward policy' adopted by India to prevent the Chinese from occupying territory claimed by them was undertaken in the mistaken belief that Beijing would be cautious in dealing with India owing to Moscow's stance on the dispute and its growing proximity to India. These misjudgments would eventually culminate in India's humiliating defeat in the war of October–November 1962.</q></cite><span title="ctx&#95;ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft&#95;val&#95;fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Abook&amp;rft.genre=bookitem&amp;rft.atitle=A+Missed+Opportunity%3F+The+Nehru-Zhou+Enlai+Summit+of+1960&amp;rft.btitle=India+and+the+Cold+War&amp;rft.pages=121&amp;rft.pub=University+of+North+Carolina+Press&amp;rft.date=2019&amp;rft.isbn=978-1-4696-5117-0&amp;rft.aulast=Raghavan&amp;rft.aufirst=Srinath&amp;rft&#95;id=https%3A%2F%2Fbooks.google.com%2Fbooks%3Fid%3Dh-yoDwAAQBAJ%26pg%3DPA121&amp;rfr&#95;id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3AIndia" class="Z3988"></span></span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-271"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-271">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite id="CITEREFBrahma&#95;Chellaney2006" class="citation book cs1"><a href="/wiki/Brahma_Chellaney" title="Brahma Chellaney">Brahma Chellaney</a> (2006). <a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://books.google.com/books?id=ZCmFAAAAMAAJ"><i>Asian Juggernaut: The Rise of China, India, and Japan</i></a>. <a href="/wiki/HarperCollins" title="HarperCollins">HarperCollins</a>. p.&#160;195. <a href="/wiki/ISBN_(identifier)" class="mw-redirect" title="ISBN (identifier)">ISBN</a>&#160;<a href="/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-81-7223-650-2" title="Special:BookSources/978-81-7223-650-2"><bdi>978-81-7223-650-2</bdi></a>. <q>Indeed, Beijing's acknowledgement of Indian control over Sikkim seems limited to the purpose of facilitating trade through the vertiginous Nathu-la Pass, the scene of bloody artillery duels in September 1967 when Indian troops beat back attacking Chinese forces.</q></cite><span title="ctx&#95;ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft&#95;val&#95;fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Abook&amp;rft.genre=book&amp;rft.btitle=Asian+Juggernaut%3A+The+Rise+of+China%2C+India%2C+and+Japan&amp;rft.pages=195&amp;rft.pub=HarperCollins&amp;rft.date=2006&amp;rft.isbn=978-81-7223-650-2&amp;rft.au=Brahma+Chellaney&amp;rft&#95;id=https%3A%2F%2Fbooks.google.com%2Fbooks%3Fid%3DZCmFAAAAMAAJ&amp;rfr&#95;id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3AIndia" class="Z3988"></span></span>

<li id="cite&#95;note-guardian-muir-diary-maratha-274"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-guardian-muir-diary-maratha_274-0">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite id="CITEREFMuir2009" class="citation cs1">Muir, Hugh (13 July 2009). <a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://web.archive.org/web/20141019165743/https://www.theguardian.com/politics/2009/jul/14/bbc-peter-salmon-trevor-mcdonald">"Diary"</a>. <i><a href="/wiki/The_Guardian" title="The Guardian">The Guardian</a></i>. Archived from <a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://www.theguardian.com/politics/2009/jul/14/bbc-peter-salmon-trevor-mcdonald">the original</a> on 19 October 2014<span class="reference-accessdate">. France's armed services.</q></cite><span title="ctx&#95;ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft&#95;val&#95;fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Ajournal&amp;rft.genre=article&amp;rft.jtitle=The+Guardian&amp;rft.atitle=Diary&amp;rft.date=2009-07-13&amp;rft.aulast=Muir&amp;rft.aufirst=Hugh&amp;rft&#95;id=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.theguardian.com%2Fpolitics%2F2009%2Fjul%2F14%2Fbbc-peter-salmon-trevor-mcdonald&amp;rfr&#95;id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3AIndia" class="Z3988"></span></span>

<li id="cite&#95;note-The&#95;Hindu&#95;November&#95;17&#95;2019c-328"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-The_Hindu_November_17_2019c_328-0">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite id="CITEREFKapoorShamika2014" class="citation news cs1">Kapoor, Mudit; Shamika, Ravi (10 February 2014). <a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://www.thehindu.com/opinion/lead/indias-missing-women/article5670801.ece">"India's missing women"</a>. <i><a href="/wiki/The_Hindu" title="The Hindu">The Hindu</a></i><span class="reference-accessdate">. Retrieved <span class="nowrap">17 November</span> 2019</span>. <q>In the last 50 years of Indian democracy, the absolute number of missing women has increased fourfold from 15 million to 68 million.  This is not merely a reflection of the overall population growth, but rather a worsening of the dangerous trend over time.  As a percentage of the female electorate, missing women have gone up significantly — from 13 per cent to approximately 20 per cent</q></cite><span title="ctx&#95;ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft&#95;val&#95;fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Ajournal&amp;rft.genre=article&amp;rft.jtitle=The+Hindu&amp;rft.atitle=India%27s+missing+women&amp;rft.date=2014-02-10&amp;rft.aulast=Kapoor&amp;rft.aufirst=Mudit&amp;rft.au=Shamika%2C+Ravi&amp;rft&#95;id=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.thehindu.com%2Fopinion%2Flead%2Findias-missing-women%2Farticle5670801.ece&amp;rfr&#95;id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3AIndia" class="Z3988"></span></span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-The&#95;Guardian&#95;November&#95;17&#95;2019c-329"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-The_Guardian_November_17_2019c_329-0">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite class="citation web cs1"><a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://www.theguardian.com/world/2018/jan/30/more-than-63-million-women-missing-in-india-statistics-show">"More than 63 million women 'missing' in India, statistics show"</a>. <i><a href="/wiki/Associated_Press" title="Associated Press">Associated Press</a> via <a href="/wiki/The_Guardian" title="The Guardian">The Guardian</a></i>. 30 January 2018<span class="reference-accessdate">. Retrieved <span class="nowrap">17 November</span> 2019</span>.</cite><span title="ctx&#95;ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft&#95;val&#95;fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Ajournal&amp;rft.genre=unknown&amp;rft.jtitle=Associated+Press+via+The+Guardian&amp;rft.atitle=More+than+63+million+women+%27missing%27+in+India%2C+statistics+show&amp;rft.date=2018-01-30&amp;rft&#95;id=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.theguardian.com%2Fworld%2F2018%2Fjan%2F30%2Fmore-than-63-million-women-missing-in-india-statistics-show&amp;rfr&#95;id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3AIndia" class="Z3988"></span> Quote: "More than 63 million women are "missing" statistically across India, and more than 21 million girls are unwanted by their families, government officials say. The skewed ratio of men to women is largely the result of sex-selective abortions, and better nutrition and medical care for boys, according to the government's annual economic survey, which was released on Monday. In addition, the survey found that "families where a son is born are more likely to stop having children than families where a girl is born".</span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-Foreign&#95;Policy&#95;November&#95;17&#95;2019c-330"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-Foreign_Policy_November_17_2019c_330-0">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite id="CITEREFTrivedi2019" class="citation web cs1">Trivedi, Ira (15 August 2019). <a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://foreignpolicy.com/2019/08/15/a-generation-of-girls-is-missing-in-india/">"A Generation of Girls Is Missing in India – Sex-selective abortion fuels a cycle of patriarchy and abuse"</a>. <i><a href="/wiki/Foreign_Policy" title="Foreign Policy">Foreign Policy</a></i><span class="reference-accessdate">. Retrieved <span class="nowrap">17 November</span> 2019</span>.</cite><span title="ctx&#95;ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft&#95;val&#95;fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Ajournal&amp;rft.genre=unknown&amp;rft.jtitle=Foreign+Policy&amp;rft.atitle=A+Generation+of+Girls+Is+Missing+in+India+%E2%80%93+Sex-selective+abortion+fuels+a+cycle+of+patriarchy+and+abuse.&amp;rft.date=2019-08-15&amp;rft.aulast=Trivedi&amp;rft.aufirst=Ira&amp;rft&#95;id=https%3A%2F%2Fforeignpolicy.com%2F2019%2F08%2F15%2Fa-generation-of-girls-is-missing-in-india%2F&amp;rfr&#95;id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3AIndia" class="Z3988"></span> Quote: "Although it has been illegal nationwide for doctors to disclose the sex of a fetus since the 1994 Pre-Conception and Pre-Natal Diagnostic Techniques Act, the ease of ordering cheap and portable ultrasound machines, especially online, has kept the practice of sex-selective abortions alive."</span>

<li id="cite&#95;note-jolad-agarwal-336"><span class="mw-cite-backlink">^ <a href="#cite_ref-jolad-agarwal_336-0"><sup><i><b>a</b></i></sup></a> <a href="#cite_ref-jolad-agarwal_336-1"><sup><i><b>b</b></i></sup></a> <a href="#cite_ref-jolad-agarwal_336-2"><sup><i><b>c</b></i></sup></a> <a href="#cite_ref-jolad-agarwal_336-3"><sup><i><b>d</b></i></sup></a></span> <span class="reference-text"><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite id="CITEREFJoladAgarwal2024" class="citation book cs1">Jolad, Shivakumar; Agarwal, Aayush (2024). </li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-Moseley2008-338"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-Moseley2008_338-0">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite id="CITEREFMoseley2008" class="citation book cs1">Moseley, Christopher (10 March 2008). <a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://books.google.com/books?id=p-7ON7Rvx_AC&amp;pg=PT528"><i>Encyclopedia of the World's Endangered Languages</i></a>. Routledge. <a href="/wiki/ISBN_(identifier)" class="mw-redirect" title="ISBN (identifier)">ISBN</a>&#160;<a href="/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-1-135-79640-2" title="Special:BookSources/978-1-135-79640-2"><bdi>978-1-135-79640-2</bdi></a>.</cite><span title="ctx&#95;ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft&#95;val&#95;fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Abook&amp;rft.genre=book&amp;rft.btitle=Encyclopedia+of+the+World%27s+Endangered+Languages&amp;rft.pub=Routledge&amp;rft.date=2008-03-10&amp;rft.isbn=978-1-135-79640-2&amp;rft.aulast=Moseley&amp;rft.aufirst=Christopher&amp;rft&#95;id=https%3A%2F%2Fbooks.google.com%2Fbooks%3Fid%3Dp-7ON7Rvx&#95;AC%26pg%3DPT528&amp;rfr&#95;id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3AIndia" class="Z3988"></span></span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-lerner-Hindi-339"><span class="mw-cite-backlink">^ <a href="#cite_ref-lerner-Hindi_339-0"><sup><i><b>a</b></i></sup></a> <a href="#cite_ref-lerner-Hindi_339-1"><sup><i><b>b</b></i></sup></a> <a href="#cite_ref-lerner-Hindi_339-2"><sup><i><b>c</b></i></sup></a> <a href="#cite_ref-lerner-Hindi_339-3"><sup><i><b>d</b></i></sup></a></span> <span class="reference-text"><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite id="CITEREFLerner2016" class="citation cs1">Lerner, Hanna (2016). <a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://books.google.com/books?id=d0knDAAAQBAJ&amp;pg=PA64">"The Indian Founding: A Comparative Perspective"</a>. In Choudhry, Sujit; Khosla, Madhav; Mehta, Pratap Bhanu (eds.). <i>The Oxford Handbook of the Indian Constitution</i>. Oxford University Press. pp.&#160;<span class="nowrap">63–</span>64. <a href="/wiki/ISBN_(identifier)" class="mw-redirect" title="ISBN (identifier)">ISBN</a>&#160;<a href="/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-0-19-870489-8" title="Special:BookSources/978-0-19-870489-8"><bdi>978-0-19-870489-8</bdi></a>. <q>Ultimately, it was the pragmatic consensus-seeking approach that triumphed. On 14 September 1949, after three years of debate, the assembly overwhelmingly approved a compromise resolution, known as the Munshi—Ayyangar formula, which later became Articles 343-51 of the Indian Constitution. Instead of declaring a 'national language', Hindi was labelled the 'official language of the Union', while English was to continue to be used 'for all official purposes'. It was decided that this arrangement would apply for a period of fifteen years, during which time Hindi was to be progressively introduced into official use. What would happen at the end of this interim period was left undetermined, with the Constitution providing for the establishment of a parliamentary committee to examine the issue in the future. In addition, the Constitution recognised fourteen other languages for official use (listed in the Eighth Schedule of the Constitution). ... Fifteen years after the enactment of the Constitution, Hindi was still not widely used by the Union government. Following a series of violent riots in non-Hindi-speaking States in the 1960s, Parliament renounced the ideal of an Indian national language. In 1965, when the fifteen-year interim period prescribed by the Constitution elapsed, the government announced that English would remain the de facto formal language of India.</q></cite><span title="ctx&#95;ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft&#95;val&#95;fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Abook&amp;rft.genre=bookitem&amp;rft.atitle=The+Indian+Founding%3A+A+Comparative+Perspective&amp;rft.btitle=The+Oxford+Handbook+of+the+Indian+Constitution&amp;rft.pages=63-64&amp;rft.pub=Oxford+University+Press&amp;rft.date=2016&amp;rft.isbn=978-0-19-870489-8&amp;rft.aulast=Lerner&amp;rft.aufirst=Hanna&amp;rft&#95;id=https%3A%2F%2Fbooks.google.com%2Fbooks%3Fid%3Dd0knDAAAQBAJ%26pg%3DPA64&amp;rfr&#95;id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3AIndia" class="Z3988"></span></span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-isaka-Hindi-340"><span class="mw-cite-backlink">^ <a href="#cite_ref-isaka-Hindi_340-0"><sup><i><b>a</b></i></sup></a> <a href="#cite_ref-isaka-Hindi_340-1"><sup><i><b>b</b></i></sup></a> <a href="#cite_ref-isaka-Hindi_340-2"><sup><i><b>c</b></i></sup></a> <a href="#cite_ref-isaka-Hindi_340-3"><sup><i><b>d</b></i></sup></a></span> <span class="reference-text"><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite id="CITEREFIsaka2021" class="citation cs1">Isaka, Riho (2021). <a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://books.google.com/books?id=3F5CEAAAQBAJ&amp;pg=PA127"><i>Language, Identity, and Power in Modern India: Gujarat, c. 1850–1960</i></a>. Routledge New Horizons in South Asian Studies. Routledge. pp.&#160;<span class="nowrap">126–</span>197. <a href="/wiki/ISBN_(identifier)" class="mw-redirect" title="ISBN (identifier)">ISBN</a>&#160;<a href="/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-1-000-46858-8" title="Special:BookSources/978-1-000-46858-8"><bdi>978-1-000-46858-8</bdi></a>. <q>Partition may have 'killed' Hindustani, but it had a marked effect on the debates regarding the position of English and provincial languages in the Constitution. The Hindi protagonists became even more insistent on establishing Hindi as the sole national language and imposing it on the non-Hindi-speaking regions to enhance 'national unity'. In addition, these leaders even began to argue that the Devanagari form of numerals should be used instead of the international form. This was firmly opposed by members from South India. To solve the continuing dispute among the Assembly members, (K. M.) Munshi and N. Gopalaswami Ayyangar, a Tamil member of the Assembly, drew up detailed language provisions. These, in the words of the latter, represented a 'compromise between opinions not easily reconcilable' (Constituent Assembly Debates 1X 1966: 1319). The provisions were proposed to the Congress on 2nd September 1949 and engendered a heated discussion. It was eventually decided that they would be proposed in the Assembly by Munshi, Ayyangar, and Bhimrao Ambedkar (the Chairman of the Drafting Committee) in their personal capacities, not as an official proposal on behalf of the Drafting Committee.</q></cite><span title="ctx&#95;ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft&#95;val&#95;fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Abook&amp;rft.genre=book&amp;rft.btitle=Language%2C+Identity%2C+and+Power+in+Modern+India%3A+Gujarat%2C+c.+1850%E2%80%931960&amp;rft.series=Routledge+New+Horizons+in+South+Asian+Studies&amp;rft.pages=126-197&amp;rft.pub=Routledge&amp;rft.date=2021&amp;rft.isbn=978-1-000-46858-8&amp;rft.aulast=Isaka&amp;rft.aufirst=Riho&amp;rft&#95;id=https%3A%2F%2Fbooks.google.com%2Fbooks%3Fid%3D3F5CEAAAQBAJ%26pg%3DPA127&amp;rfr&#95;id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3AIndia" class="Z3988"></span></span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-KachruKachru2008-1-341"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-KachruKachru2008-1_341-0">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite id="CITEREFAnnamalai2008" class="citation book cs1">Annamalai, E. (2008). <a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://books.google.com/books?id=O2n4sFGDEMYC&amp;pg=PA223">"Contexts of multilingualism"</a>. In Braj B. Kachru; Yamuna Kachru; S. N. Sridhar (eds.). <i>Language in South Asia</i>. Cambridge University Press. pp.&#160;223–. <a href="/wiki/ISBN_(identifier)" class="mw-redirect" title="ISBN (identifier)">ISBN</a>&#160;<a href="/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-1-139-46550-2" title="Special:BookSources/978-1-139-46550-2"><bdi>978-1-139-46550-2</bdi></a>. <q>Some of the migrated languages ... such as Sanskrit and English, remained primarily as a second language, even though their native speakers were lost.</q></cite><span title="ctx&#95;ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft&#95;val&#95;fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Abook&amp;rft.genre=bookitem&amp;rft.atitle=Contexts+of+multilingualism&amp;rft.btitle=Language+in+South+Asia&amp;rft.pages=223-&amp;rft.pub=Cambridge+University+Press&amp;rft.date=2008&amp;rft.isbn=978-1-139-46550-2&amp;rft.aulast=Annamalai&amp;rft.aufirst=E.&amp;rft&#95;id=https%3A%2F%2Fbooks.google.com%2Fbooks%3Fid%3DO2n4sFGDEMYC%26pg%3DPA223&amp;rfr&#95;id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3AIndia" class="Z3988"></span></span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-GazzolaWickström2016-342"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-GazzolaWickström2016_342-0">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite id="CITEREFGazzolaWickström2016" class="citation book cs1">Gazzola, Michele; Wickström, Bengt-Arne (2016). <a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://books.google.com/books?id=C4snDQAAQBAJ&amp;pg=PA469"><i>The Economics of Language Policy</i></a>. MIT Press. pp.&#160;469–. <a href="/wiki/ISBN_(identifier)" class="mw-redirect" title="ISBN (identifier)">ISBN</a>&#160;<a href="/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-0-262-03470-8" title="Special:BookSources/978-0-262-03470-8"><bdi>978-0-262-03470-8</bdi></a>. <q>The Eighth Schedule recognizes India's national languages as including the major regional languages as well as others, such as Sanskrit and Urdu, which contribute to India's cultural heritage. ... The original list of fourteen languages in the Eighth Schedule at the time of the adoption of the Constitution in 1949 has now grown to twenty-two.</q></cite><span title="ctx&#95;ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft&#95;val&#95;fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Abook&amp;rft.genre=book&amp;rft.btitle=The+Economics+of+Language+Policy&amp;rft.pages=469-&amp;rft.pub=MIT+Press&amp;rft.date=2016&amp;rft.isbn=978-0-262-03470-8&amp;rft.aulast=Gazzola&amp;rft.aufirst=Michele&amp;rft.au=Wickstr%C3%B6m%2C+Bengt-Arne&amp;rft&#95;id=https%3A%2F%2Fbooks.google.com%2Fbooks%3Fid%3DC4snDQAAQBAJ%26pg%3DPA469&amp;rfr&#95;id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3AIndia" class="Z3988"></span></span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-Groff2017-lead-343"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-Groff2017-lead_343-0">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite id="CITEREFGroff2017" class="citation book cs1">Groff, Cynthia (2017). <a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://books.google.com/books?id=qLc7DwAAQBAJ&amp;pg=PA58"><i>The Ecology of Language in Multilingual India: Voices of Women and Educators in the Himalayan Foothills</i></a>. Palgrave Macmillan UK. pp.&#160;58–. <a href="/wiki/ISBN_(identifier)" class="mw-redirect" title="ISBN (identifier)">ISBN</a>&#160;<a href="/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-1-137-51961-0" title="Special:BookSources/978-1-137-51961-0"><bdi>978-1-137-51961-0</bdi></a>. <q>As Mahapatra says: "It is generally believed that the significance for the Eighth Schedule lies in providing a list of languages from which Hindi is directed to draw the appropriate forms, style and expressions for its enrichment" ... Being recognized in the Constitution, however, has had significant relevance for a language's status and functions.</q></cite><span title="ctx&#95;ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft&#95;val&#95;fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Abook&amp;rft.genre=book&amp;rft.btitle=The+Ecology+of+Language+in+Multilingual+India%3A+Voices+of+Women+and+Educators+in+the+Himalayan+Foothills&amp;rft.pages=58-&amp;rft.pub=Palgrave+Macmillan+UK&amp;rft.date=2017&amp;rft.isbn=978-1-137-51961-0&amp;rft.aulast=Groff&amp;rft.aufirst=Cynthia&amp;rft&#95;id=https%3A%2F%2Fbooks.google.com%2Fbooks%3Fid%3DqLc7DwAAQBAJ%26pg%3DPA58&amp;rfr&#95;id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3AIndia" class="Z3988"></span></span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-rupee-15lang-344"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-rupee-15lang_344-0">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite id="CITEREFGautam2024" class="citation cs1">Gautam, Vanya (5 January 2024). <a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://www.indiatimes.com/worth/news/did-you-know-indian-rupee-notes-feature-15-languages-heres-the-list/articleshow/122444265.html"><i>Did You Know? <li id="cite&#95;note-358"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-358">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite id="CITEREFN.&#95;Jayapalan2005" class="citation book cs1">N. Jayapalan (2005). <a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://books.google.com/books?id=IDNeW78fedkC"><i>History of Education in India</i></a>. Atlantic Publishers &amp; Distributors. <a href="/wiki/ISBN_(identifier)" class="mw-redirect" title="ISBN (identifier)">ISBN</a>&#160;<a href="/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-81-7156-922-9" title="Special:BookSources/978-81-7156-922-9"><bdi>978-81-7156-922-9</bdi></a>.</cite><span title="ctx&#95;ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft&#95;val&#95;fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Abook&amp;rft.genre=book&amp;rft.btitle=History+of+Education+in+India&amp;rft.pub=Atlantic+Publishers+%26+Distributors&amp;rft.date=2005&amp;rft.isbn=978-81-7156-922-9&amp;rft.au=N.+Jayapalan&amp;rft&#95;id=https%3A%2F%2Fbooks.google.com%2Fbooks%3Fid%3DIDNeW78fedkC&amp;rfr&#95;id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3AIndia" class="Z3988"></span></span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-hedrick-thomason-359"><span class="mw-cite-backlink">^ <a href="#cite_ref-hedrick-thomason_359-0"><sup><i><b>a</b></i></sup></a> <a href="#cite_ref-hedrick-thomason_359-1"><sup><i><b>b</b></i></sup></a></span> <span class="reference-text"><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite id="CITEREFHeadrick1988" class="citation cs1">Headrick, Daniel R. (1988). <i>The Tentacles of Progress: Technology Transfer in the Age of Imperialism, 1850–1940</i>. New York: Oxford University Press. p.&#160;317. <a href="/wiki/ISBN_(identifier)" class="mw-redirect" title="ISBN (identifier)">ISBN</a>&#160;<a href="/wiki/Special:BookSources/0-19-505115-7" title="Special:BookSources/0-19-505115-7"><bdi>0-19-505115-7</bdi></a>. <q>The first engineering college was an outgrowth of the Ganges Canal.  Named after the lieutenant governor of the North-Western Provinces who founded it in 1847, the Thomason Engineering College at Roorkee trained employees for the irrigation branch of the Public Works Department. It offered different curricula for different types of students: an engineering class for domiciled Europeans and a few Indians, an upper subordinates class to train British noncommissioned officers as construction foremen, and a lower subordinates class to train Indian surveyors. By the mid-1880s, the school has a hundred students, substantial buildings, and a reputation as an important center for the study of hydraulic engineering.</q></cite><span title="ctx&#95;ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft&#95;val&#95;fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Abook&amp;rft.genre=book&amp;rft.btitle=The+Tentacles+of+Progress%3A+Technology+Transfer+in+the+Age+of+Imperialism%2C+1850%E2%80%931940&amp;rft.place=New+York&amp;rft.pages=317&amp;rft.pub=Oxford+University+Press&amp;rft.date=1988&amp;rft.isbn=0-19-505115-7&amp;rft.aulast=Headrick&amp;rft.aufirst=Daniel+R.&amp;rfr&#95;id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3AIndia" class="Z3988"></span></span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-subram-thomason-360"><span class="mw-cite-backlink">^ <a href="#cite_ref-subram-thomason_360-0"><sup><i><b>a</b></i></sup></a> <a href="#cite_ref-subram-thomason_360-1"><sup><i><b>b</b></i></sup></a></span> <span class="reference-text"><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite id="CITEREFSubramanian2019" class="citation cs1">Subramanian, Ajantha (2019). <i>The Caste of Merit: Engineering Education in India</i>. Cambridge, MA and London: Harvard University Press. pp.&#160;<span class="nowrap">30–</span>31. <a href="/wiki/ISBN_(identifier)" class="mw-redirect" title="ISBN (identifier)">ISBN</a>&#160;<a href="/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-0-674-98788-3" title="Special:BookSources/978-0-674-98788-3"><bdi>978-0-674-98788-3</bdi></a>. <q>Before the 1854 despatch, there was already one engineering college in operation: the Thomason College of Civil Engineering at Roorkee. The college was founded in 1847 and was affiliated to the <a href="/wiki/University_of_Calcutta" title="University of Calcutta">University of Calcutta</a>, in response to the demand for civil engineers to aid the construction of the Ganges Canal in the North-west Provinces.</q></cite><span title="ctx&#95;ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft&#95;val&#95;fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Abook&amp;rft.genre=book&amp;rft.btitle=The+Caste+of+Merit%3A+Engineering+Education+in+India&amp;rft.place=Cambridge%2C+MA+and+London&amp;rft.pages=30-31&amp;rft.pub=Harvard+University+Press&amp;rft.date=2019&amp;rft.isbn=978-0-674-98788-3&amp;rft.aulast=Subramanian&amp;rft.aufirst=Ajantha&amp;rfr&#95;id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3AIndia" class="Z3988"></span></span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-who-chan-14-feb-2014-361"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-who-chan-14-feb-2014_361-0">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite id="CITEREFChan2014" class="citation cs1">Chan, Margaret (11 February 2014). <a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://www.who.int/director-general/speeches/detail/who-director-general-celebrates-polio-free-india"><i>Address at the 'India celebrates triumph over polio' event</i></a>. New Delhi, India: <a href="/wiki/World_Health_Organization" title="World Health Organization">World Health Organization</a><span class="reference-accessdate">. <li id="cite&#95;note-364"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-364">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite class="citation news cs1"><a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://ig.ft.com/india-pollution/">"Dirty air: how India became the most polluted country on earth"</a>. <i>Financial Times</i>. 11 December 2018. <a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://web.archive.org/web/20190930130506/https://ig.ft.com/india-pollution/">Archived</a> from the original on 30 September 2019<span class="reference-accessdate">. <li id="cite&#95;note-caste-lead-365"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-caste-lead_365-0">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text">
<ul><li><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite id="CITEREFLagasse,&#95;Paul2007" class="citation cs1">Lagasse, Paul, ed. (2007). <a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="http://www.encyclopedia.com/topic/caste.aspx#3">"Caste"</a>. <i>The Columbia Encyclopedia</i>. New York, NY: Columbia University Press. <a href="/wiki/ISBN_(identifier)" class="mw-redirect" title="ISBN (identifier)">ISBN</a>&#160;<a href="/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-0-231-14446-9" title="Special:BookSources/978-0-231-14446-9"><bdi>978-0-231-14446-9</bdi></a><span class="reference-accessdate">. Retrieved <span class="nowrap">24 September</span> 2012</span>.</cite><span title="ctx&#95;ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft&#95;val&#95;fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Abook&amp;rft.genre=bookitem&amp;rft.atitle=Caste&amp;rft.btitle=The+Columbia+Encyclopedia&amp;rft.place=New+York%2C+NY&amp;rft.pub=Columbia+University+Press&amp;rft.date=2007&amp;rft.isbn=978-0-231-14446-9&amp;rft&#95;id=http%3A%2F%2Fwww.encyclopedia.com%2Ftopic%2Fcaste.aspx%233&amp;rfr&#95;id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3AIndia" class="Z3988"></span> Quote: "<b>caste</b> [Port., casta=basket], ranked groups based on heredity within rigid systems of social stratification, especially those that constitute Hindu India. Some scholars, in fact, deny that true caste systems are found outside India. The caste is a closed group whose members are severely restricted in their choice of occupation and degree of social participation. Marriage outside the caste is prohibited. Social status is determined by the caste of one's birth and may only rarely be transcended."</li>
<li><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite id="CITEREFMadan2012" class="citation cs1"><a href="/wiki/T._N._Madan" title="T. N. Madan">Madan, T. N.</a> (2012). <a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://www.britannica.com/EBchecked/topic/98395/caste"><i>caste</i></a>. Encyclopædia Britannica Online.</cite><span title="ctx&#95;ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft&#95;val&#95;fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Abook&amp;rft.genre=book&amp;rft.btitle=caste&amp;rft.pub=Encyclop%C3%A6dia+Britannica+Online&amp;rft.date=2012&amp;rft.aulast=Madan&amp;rft.aufirst=T.+N.&amp;rft&#95;id=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.britannica.com%2FEBchecked%2Ftopic%2F98395%2Fcaste&amp;rfr&#95;id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3AIndia" class="Z3988"></span> Quote: "<b>caste</b>, any of the ranked, hereditary, endogamous social groups, often linked with occupation, that together constitute traditional societies in South Asia, particularly among Hindus in India. Although sometimes used to designate similar groups in other societies, the "caste system" is uniquely developed in Hindu societies."</li>
<li><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite id="CITEREFGupta2008" class="citation cs1">Gupta, Dipankar (2008). <a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://books.google.com/books?id=YMUola6pDnkC&amp;pg=PA246">"Caste"</a>. In Schaefer, Richard T. (ed.). <i>Encyclopedia of Race, Ethnicity, and Society</i>. Thousand Oaks: SAGE. pp.&#160;<span class="nowrap">246–</span>250. <a href="/wiki/ISBN_(identifier)" class="mw-redirect" title="ISBN (identifier)">ISBN</a>&#160;<a href="/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-1-4129-2694-2" title="Special:BookSources/978-1-4129-2694-2"><bdi>978-1-4129-2694-2</bdi></a><span class="reference-accessdate">. Retrieved <span class="nowrap">5 August</span> 2012</span>.</cite><span title="ctx&#95;ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft&#95;val&#95;fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Abook&amp;rft.genre=bookitem&amp;rft.atitle=Caste&amp;rft.btitle=Encyclopedia+of+Race%2C+Ethnicity%2C+and+Society&amp;rft.place=Thousand+Oaks&amp;rft.pages=246-250&amp;rft.pub=SAGE&amp;rft.date=2008&amp;rft.isbn=978-1-4129-2694-2&amp;rft.aulast=Gupta&amp;rft.aufirst=Dipankar&amp;rft&#95;id=https%3A%2F%2Fbooks.google.com%2Fbooks%3Fid%3DYMUola6pDnkC%26pg%3DPA246&amp;rfr&#95;id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3AIndia" class="Z3988"></span> Quote: "<b>Caste</b>: What makes Indian society unique is the phenomenon of caste. Economic, religious, and linguistic differentiations, even race-based discrimination, are known elsewhere, but nowhere else does one see caste but in India."</li>
<li><a href="#CITEREFBéteille2002">Béteille 2002</a>, pp.&#160;136–137. Quote: "<b>Caste</b>: Caste has been described as the fundamental social institution of India. Sometimes the term is used metaphorically to refer to rigid social distinctions or extreme social exclusiveness wherever found, and some authorities have used the term 'colour-caste system' to describe the stratification based on race in the United States and elsewhere. But it is among the Hindus in India that we find the system in its most fully developed form although analogous forms exist among Muslims, Christians. Sikhs and other religious groups in South Asia. It is an ancient institution, having existed for at least 2,000 years among the Hindus who developed not only elaborate caste practices hut also a complex theory to explain and justify those practices (Dumont 1970). The theory has now lost much of its force although many of the practices continue."</li>
<li><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite id="CITEREFMitchell2006" class="citation cs1">Mitchell, Geoffrey Duncan (2006). <a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://books.google.com/books?id=-a4vvdBrSVgC&amp;pg=PA194">"Castes (part of SOCIAL STRATIFICATION)"</a>. <i>A New Dictionary of the Social Sciences</i>. New Brunswick, NJ: Aldine Transaction Publishers. pp.&#160;<span class="nowrap">194–</span>195. <a href="/wiki/ISBN_(identifier)" class="mw-redirect" title="ISBN (identifier)">ISBN</a>&#160;<a href="/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-0-202-30878-4" title="Special:BookSources/978-0-202-30878-4"><bdi>978-0-202-30878-4</bdi></a><span class="reference-accessdate">. Retrieved <span class="nowrap">10 August</span> 2012</span>.</cite><span title="ctx&#95;ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft&#95;val&#95;fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Abook&amp;rft.genre=bookitem&amp;rft.atitle=Castes+%28part+of+SOCIAL+STRATIFICATION%29&amp;rft.btitle=A+New+Dictionary+of+the+Social+Sciences&amp;rft.place=New+Brunswick%2C+NJ&amp;rft.pages=194-195&amp;rft.pub=Aldine+Transaction+Publishers&amp;rft.date=2006&amp;rft.isbn=978-0-202-30878-4&amp;rft.aulast=Mitchell&amp;rft.aufirst=Geoffrey+Duncan&amp;rft&#95;id=https%3A%2F%2Fbooks.google.com%2Fbooks%3Fid%3D-a4vvdBrSVgC%26pg%3DPA194&amp;rfr&#95;id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3AIndia" class="Z3988"></span> Quote:"<b>Castes</b> A pure caste system is rooted in the religious order and may be thought of as a hierarchy of hereditary, endogamous, occupational groups with positions fixed and mobility barred by ritual distances between each caste. Empirically, the classical Hindu system of India approximated most closely to pure caste. The system existed for some 3,000 years and continues today despite many attempts to get rid of some of its restrictions. It is essentially connected with Hinduism."</li>
<li><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite class="citation cs1"><a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="http://www.oed.com/view/Entry/28546">"caste, n."</a>. <i>Oxford English Dictionary, Second edition; online version June 2012</i>. Oxford, UK: Oxford University Press. 1989<span class="reference-accessdate">. Retrieved <span class="nowrap">5 August</span> 2019</span>.</cite><span title="ctx&#95;ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft&#95;val&#95;fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Abook&amp;rft.genre=bookitem&amp;rft.atitle=caste%2C+n.&amp;rft.btitle=Oxford+English+Dictionary%2C+Second+edition%3B+online+version+June+2012&amp;rft.place=Oxford%2C+UK&amp;rft.pub=Oxford+University+Press&amp;rft.date=1989&amp;rft&#95;id=http%3A%2F%2Fwww.oed.com%2Fview%2FEntry%2F28546&amp;rfr&#95;id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3AIndia" class="Z3988"></span> Quote: "<b>caste, n.</b> 2a. spec. One of the several hereditary classes into which society in India has from time immemorial been divided; ... <i>This is now the leading sense, which influences all others</i>."</li></ul>
</span></li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-atrey1-367"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-atrey1_367-0">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite id="CITEREFAtrey2019" class="citation book cs1">Atrey, Shreya (2019). <i>Intersectional Discrimination</i>. Oxford, UK and New York, NY: Oxford University Press. p.&#160;63&#8211;64. <a href="/wiki/ISBN_(identifier)" class="mw-redirect" title="ISBN (identifier)">ISBN</a>&#160;<a href="/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-0-19-884895-0" title="Special:BookSources/978-0-19-884895-0"><bdi>978-0-19-884895-0</bdi></a>.</cite><span title="ctx&#95;ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft&#95;val&#95;fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Abook&amp;rft.genre=book&amp;rft.btitle=Intersectional+Discrimination&amp;rft.place=Oxford%2C+UK+and+New+York%2C+NY&amp;rft.pages=63-64&amp;rft.pub=Oxford+University+Press&amp;rft.date=2019&amp;rft.isbn=978-0-19-884895-0&amp;rft.aulast=Atrey&amp;rft.aufirst=Shreya&amp;rfr&#95;id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3AIndia" class="Z3988"></span></span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-nyt-india-constit-assmb-caste-368"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-nyt-india-constit-assmb-caste_368-0">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite class="citation news cs1">"Indians Outlaw 'Untouchability' In Formal Action by Assembly; UNTOUCHABILITY' FORMALLY BANNED". Reuters/New York Times. 30 April 1947. <q>NEW DELHI, India, April 29 -- India's Constituent Assembly, discussing the Fundamental Rights Committee's report, today adopted this provision: "Untouchability in any form is abolished and the imposition of any disability on that account shall be an offense.</q></cite><span title="ctx&#95;ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft&#95;val&#95;fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Ajournal&amp;rft.genre=article&amp;rft.atitle=Indians+Outlaw+%27Untouchability%27+In+Formal+Action+by+Assembly%3B+UNTOUCHABILITY%27+FORMALLY+BANNED&amp;rft.date=1947-04-30&amp;rfr&#95;id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3AIndia" class="Z3988"></span></span>

<li id="cite&#95;note-369"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-369">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite id="CITEREFTeltumbde2010" class="citation book cs1">Teltumbde, Anand (2010). <i>The Persistence of Caste: The Khairlanji Murders and India's Hidden Apartheid</i>.
