<li id="cite&#95;note-374"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-374">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite id="CITEREFBiswas2016" class="citation news cs1">Biswas, Soutik (29 September 2016). <a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/world-asia-india-37481054">"What divorce and separation tell us about modern India"</a>. <a href="/wiki/BBC_News" title="BBC News">BBC News</a><span class="reference-accessdate">. Retrieved <span class="nowrap">18 October</span> 2021</span>.</cite><span title="ctx&#95;ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft&#95;val&#95;fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Ajournal&amp;rft.genre=article&amp;rft.atitle=What+divorce+and+separation+tell+us+about+modern+India&amp;rft.date=2016-09-29&amp;rft.aulast=Biswas&amp;rft.aufirst=Soutik&amp;rft&#95;id=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.bbc.co.uk%2Fnews%2Fworld-asia-india-37481054&amp;rfr&#95;id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3AIndia" class="Z3988"></span></span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-FOOTNOTECullen-Dupont200996-375"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-FOOTNOTECullen-Dupont200996_375-0">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><a href="#CITEREFCullen-Dupont2009">Cullen-Dupont 2009</a>, p.&#160;96.</span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-Agarwal1994-376"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-Agarwal1994_376-0">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite id="CITEREFAgarwal1994" class="citation cs1"><a href="/wiki/Bina_Agarwal" title="Bina Agarwal">Agarwal, Bina</a> (1994). <a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://books.google.com/books?id=Z3pdP30OnEUC&amp;pg=PA264"><i>A Field of One's Own: Gender and Land Rights in South Asia</i></a>. Cambridge University Press. p.&#160;264. <a href="/wiki/ISBN_(identifier)" class="mw-redirect" title="ISBN (identifier)">ISBN</a>&#160;<a href="/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-0-521-42926-9" title="Special:BookSources/978-0-521-42926-9"><bdi>978-0-521-42926-9</bdi></a>. <q>Mayer's (1960: 219) observation for central India would not be inaccurate for most communities in the subcontinent: "A man's tie with his sister is accounted very close. The two have grown up together, at an age when there is no distinction made between the sexes. And later, when the sister marries, the brother is seen as her main protector, for when her father has died to whom else can she turn if there is trouble in her conjugal household.(Adrian C. Mayer, <i>Caste and kinship in Central India</i> (1960)"  The parental home, and after the parents' death the brother's home, often offers the only possibility of temporary or longer-term support in case of divorce, desertion, and even widowhood, especially for a woman without adult sons. Her dependence on this support is directly related to economic and social vulnerability.</q></cite><span title="ctx&#95;ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft&#95;val&#95;fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Abook&amp;rft.genre=book&amp;rft.btitle=A+Field+of+One%27s+Own%3A+Gender+and+Land+Rights+in+South+Asia&amp;rft.pages=264&amp;rft.pub=Cambridge+University+Press&amp;rft.date=1994&amp;rft.isbn=978-0-521-42926-9&amp;rft.aulast=Agarwal&amp;rft.aufirst=Bina&amp;rft&#95;id=https%3A%2F%2Fbooks.google.com%2Fbooks%3Fid%3DZ3pdP30OnEUC%26pg%3DPA264&amp;rfr&#95;id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3AIndia" class="Z3988"></span></span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-Coleman2017-377"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-Coleman2017_377-0">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite id="CITEREFColeman2017" class="citation cs1">Coleman, Leo (2017). <a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://books.google.com/books?id=6YDTDgAAQBAJ&amp;pg=PT127"><i>A Moral Technology: Electrification as Political Ritual in New Delhi</i></a>. Cornell University Press. p.&#160;127. <a href="/wiki/ISBN_(identifier)" class="mw-redirect" title="ISBN (identifier)">ISBN</a>&#160;<a href="/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-1-5017-0791-9" title="Special:BookSources/978-1-5017-0791-9"><bdi>978-1-5017-0791-9</bdi></a>.</cite><span title="ctx&#95;ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft&#95;val&#95;fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Abook&amp;rft.genre=book&amp;rft.btitle=A+Moral+Technology%3A+Electrification+as+Political+Ritual+in+New+Delhi&amp;rft.pages=127&amp;rft.pub=Cornell+University+Press&amp;rft.date=2017&amp;rft.isbn=978-1-5017-0791-9&amp;rft.aulast=Coleman&amp;rft.aufirst=Leo&amp;rft&#95;id=https%3A%2F%2Fbooks.google.com%2Fbooks%3Fid%3D6YDTDgAAQBAJ%26pg%3DPT127&amp;rfr&#95;id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3AIndia" class="Z3988"></span> Quote: Rakhi and its local performances in Kishan Garhi were part of a festival in which connections between out-marrying sisters and village-resident brothers were affirmed. In the "traditional" form of this rite, according to Marriott, sisters exchanged with their brothers to ensure their ability to have recourse—at a crisis, or during childbearing—to their natal village and their relatives there even after leaving for their husband's home. For their part, brothers engaging in these exchanges affirmed the otherwise hard-to-discern moral solidarity of the natal family, even after their sister's marriage.</span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-FOOTNOTERowland1970185–198,&#95;252,&#95;385–466-378"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-FOOTNOTERowland1970185–198,_252,_385–466_378-0">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><a href="#CITEREFRowland1970">Rowland 1970</a>, pp.&#160;185–198, 252, 385–466.</span>

<li id="cite&#95;note-FOOTNOTECraven1997243-405"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-FOOTNOTECraven1997243_405-0">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><a href="#CITEREFCraven1997">Craven 1997</a>, p.&#160;243.</span>

<li id="cite&#95;note-FOOTNOTEMichell2000210-406"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-FOOTNOTEMichell2000210_406-0">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><a href="#CITEREFMichell2000">Michell 2000</a>, p.&#160;210.</span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-FOOTNOTEMichell2000210–211-407"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-FOOTNOTEMichell2000210–211_407-0">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><a href="#CITEREFMichell2000">Michell 2000</a>, pp.&#160;210–211.</span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-FOOTNOTEBlurton1993211-408"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-FOOTNOTEBlurton1993211_408-0">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><a href="#CITEREFBlurton1993">Blurton 1993</a>, p.&#160;211.</span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-hayashi2005-p363-409"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-hayashi2005-p363_409-0">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><a href="#CITEREFHayashi2005">Hayashi 2005</a>, p.&#160;363</span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-plofker-410"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-plofker_410-0">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><a href="#CITEREFPlofker2007">Plofker 2007</a>, p.&#160;1</span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-filliozat-p140to143-411"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-filliozat-p140to143_411-0">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><a href="#CITEREFFilliozat2004">Filliozat 2004</a>, pp.&#160;140–143</span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-hayashi95-413"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-hayashi95_413-0">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><a href="#CITEREFHayashi1995">Hayashi 1995</a></span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-plofker-brit6-414"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-plofker-brit6_414-0">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><a href="#CITEREFPlofker2007">Plofker 2007</a>, p.&#160;6</span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-irfah346-415"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-irfah346_415-0">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><a href="#CITEREFIfrah2000">Ifrah 2000</a>, p.&#160;346: "The measure of the genius of Indian civilisation, to which we owe our modern (number) system, is all the greater in that it was the only one in all history to have achieved this triumph. Some cultures succeeded, earlier than the Indian, in discovering one or at best two of the characteristics of this intellectual feat. But none of them managed to bring together into a complete and coherent system the necessary and sufficient conditions for a number-system with the same potential as our own."</span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-416"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-416">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><a href="#CITEREFPlofker2009">Plofker 2009</a>, pp.&#160;44–47</span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-bourbaki46-417"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-bourbaki46_417-0">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><a href="#CITEREFBourbaki1998">Bourbaki 1998</a>, p.&#160;46: "...our decimal system, which (by the agency of the Arabs) is derived from Hindu mathematics, where its use is attested already from the first centuries of our era. It must be noted moreover that the conception of zero as a number and not as a simple symbol of separation) and its introduction into calculations, also count amongst the original contribution of the Hindus."</span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-bourbaki49-418"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-bourbaki49_418-0">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><a href="#CITEREFBourbaki1998">Bourbaki 1998</a>, p.&#160;49: Modern arithmetic was known during medieval times as "Modus Indorum" or method of the Indians. <a href="/wiki/Leonardo_of_Pisa" class="mw-redirect" title="Leonardo of Pisa">Leonardo of Pisa</a> wrote that compared to method of the Indians all other methods is a mistake. This method of the Indians is none other than our very simple arithmetic of addition, subtraction, multiplication and division. Rules for these four simple procedures was first written down by <a href="/wiki/Brahmagupta" title="Brahmagupta">Brahmagupta</a> during the 7th century AD. "On this point, the Hindus are already conscious of the interpretation that negative numbers must have in certain cases (a debt in a commercial problem, for instance). In the following centuries, as there is a diffusion into the West (by intermediary of the Arabs) of the methods and results of Greek and Hindu mathematics, one becomes more used to the handling of these numbers, and one begins to have other "representation" for them which are geometric or dynamic."</span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-concise-britannica-419"><span class="mw-cite-backlink">^ <a href="#cite_ref-concise-britannica_419-0"><sup><i><b>a</b></i></sup></a> <a href="#cite_ref-concise-britannica_419-1"><sup><i><b>b</b></i></sup></a></span> <span class="reference-text">"algebra" 2007. <a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://www.britannica.com/ebc/article-231064"><i>Britannica Concise Encyclopedia</i></a> <a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://web.archive.org/web/20070929134632/http://www.britannica.com/ebc/article-231064">Archived</a> 29 September 2007 at the <a href="/wiki/Wayback_Machine" title="Wayback Machine">Wayback Machine</a>. Encyclopædia Britannica Online. 16 May 2007. Quote: "A full-fledged decimal, positional system certainly existed in India by the 9th century (AD), yet many of its central ideas had been transmitted well before that time to China and the Islamic world. Indian arithmetic, moreover, developed consistent and correct rules for operating with positive and negative numbers and for treating zero like any other number, even in problematic contexts such as division. Several hundred years passed before European mathematicians fully integrated such ideas into the developing discipline of algebra."</span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-420"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-420">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><a href="#CITEREFPingree2003">Pingree 2003</a>, p.&#160;45 Quote: "Geometry, and its branch trigonometry, was the mathematics Indian astronomers used most frequently. Greek mathematicians used the full chord and never imagined the half chord that we use today. Half chord was first used by Aryabhata which made trigonometry much more simple. In fact, the Indian astronomers in the third or fourth century, using a pre-Ptolemaic Greek table of chords, produced tables of sines and versines, from which it was trivial to derive cosines. This new system of trigonometry, produced in India, was transmitted to the Arabs in the late eighth century and by them, in an expanded form, to the Latin West and the Byzantine East in the twelfth century."</span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-421"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-421">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><a href="#CITEREFBourbaki1998">Bourbaki 1998</a>, p.&#160;126: "As for trigonometry, it is disdained by geometers and abandoned to surveyors and astronomers; it is these latter (<a href="/wiki/Aristarchus_of_Samos" title="Aristarchus of Samos">Aristarchus</a>, <a href="/wiki/Hipparchus" title="Hipparchus">Hipparchus</a>, <a href="/wiki/Ptolemy" title="Ptolemy">Ptolemy</a>) who establish the fundamental relations between the sides and angles of a right angled triangle (plane or spherical) and draw up the first tables (they consist of tables giving the <i>chord</i> of the arc cut out by an angle <span class="mwe-math-element mwe-math-element-inline"><span class="mwe-math-mathml-inline mwe-math-mathml-a11y" style="display: none;"><math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"  alttext="{\displaystyle \theta &lt;\pi }">
  <semantics>
    <mrow class="MJX-TeXAtom-ORD">
      <mstyle displaystyle="true" scriptlevel="0">
        <mi>&#x03B8;<!-- θ --></mi>
        <mo>&lt;</mo>
        <mi>&#x03C0;<!-- π --></mi>
      </mstyle>
    </mrow>
    <annotation encoding="application/x-tex">{\displaystyle \theta &lt;\pi }</annotation>
  </semantics>
</math></span><img src="https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/053c2f5cb3d9460d5fe43fcc48920484fb2ce4c2" class="mwe-math-fallback-image-inline mw-invert skin-invert" aria-hidden="true" style="vertical-align: -0.338ex; width:5.521ex; height:2.176ex;" alt="{\displaystyle \theta &lt;\pi }"></span> on a circle of radius <i>r</i>, in other words the number <span class="mwe-math-element mwe-math-element-inline"><span class="mwe-math-mathml-inline mwe-math-mathml-a11y" style="display: none;"><math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"  alttext="{\displaystyle 2r\sin \left(\theta /2\right)}">

          </mrow>
          <mo>)</mo>
        </mrow>
      </mstyle>
    </mrow>
    <annotation encoding="application/x-tex">{\displaystyle 2r\sin \left(\theta /2\right)}</annotation>
  </semantics>
</math></span><img src="https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/4ca756adcc10a403716e769f6214640ed6802c32" class="mwe-math-fallback-image-inline mw-invert skin-invert" aria-hidden="true" style="vertical-align: -0.838ex; width:10.678ex; height:2.843ex;" alt="{\displaystyle 2r\sin \left(\theta /2\right)}"></span>; the introduction of the sine, more easily handled, is due to Hindu mathematicians of the Middle Ages)."</span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-423"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-423">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><a href="#CITEREFStillwell2004">Stillwell 2004</a>, p.&#160;173</span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-hardy-ramanumajan-notice-424"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-hardy-ramanumajan-notice_424-0">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite id="CITEREFHardy2015" class="citation book cs1"><a href="/wiki/G._H._Hardy" title="G. H. Hardy">Hardy, G. H.</a> (2015) [1927]. "Notice". <i>Collected papers of Srinivasa Ramanujan</i>. Cambridge University Press. pp.&#160;<span class="nowrap">xxi–</span>xxxv. <a href="/wiki/ISBN_(identifier)" class="mw-redirect" title="ISBN (identifier)">ISBN</a>&#160;<a href="/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-1-107-53651-7" title="Special:BookSources/978-1-107-53651-7"><bdi>978-1-107-53651-7</bdi></a>. <q>It was his insight into algebraical formulae, transformation of infinite series, ... that was most amazing.  On this side most certainly I have never met his equal, and I can compare him only with <a href="/wiki/Leonhard_Euler" title="Leonhard Euler">Euler</a> and <a href="/wiki/Carl_Gustav_Jacob_Jacobi" title="Carl Gustav Jacob Jacobi">Jacobi</a>.  He worked, far more than the majority of modern mathematicians, by induction from numerical examples. ... But with his memory, his patience, and his power of calculation, he combined a power of generalisation, a feeling for form, and a capacity for rapid modification of his hypotheses, that were often really startling, and made him, in his own peculiar field, without a rival in his day.</q></cite><span title="ctx&#95;ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft&#95;val&#95;fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Abook&amp;rft.genre=bookitem&amp;rft.atitle=Notice&amp;rft.btitle=Collected+papers+of+Srinivasa+Ramanujan&amp;rft.pages=xxi-xxxv&amp;rft.pub=Cambridge+University+Press&amp;rft.date=2015&amp;rft.isbn=978-1-107-53651-7&amp;rft.aulast=Hardy&amp;rft.aufirst=G.+H.&amp;rfr&#95;id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3AIndia" class="Z3988"></span></span>
</li>

<li id="cite&#95;note-phanek-443"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-phanek_443-0">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite id="CITEREFHaripriya2024" class="citation book cs1">Haripriya, Soibam (2024). <li id="cite&#95;note-Davidson2014-p409-444"><span class="mw-cite-backlink">^ <a href="#cite_ref-Davidson2014-p409_444-0"><sup><i><b>a</b></i></sup></a> <a href="#cite_ref-Davidson2014-p409_444-1"><sup><i><b>b</b></i></sup></a></span> <span class="reference-text"><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite id="CITEREFDavidson2014" class="citation cs1">Davidson, Alan (2014). <a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://books.google.com/books?id=RL6LAwAAQBAJ&amp;pg=PA409"><i>The Oxford Companion to Food</i></a>. <a href="/wiki/Oxford_University_Press" title="Oxford University Press">Oxford University Press</a>. p.&#160;409. <a href="/wiki/ISBN_(identifier)" class="mw-redirect" title="ISBN (identifier)">ISBN</a>&#160;<a href="/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-0-19-967733-7" title="Special:BookSources/978-0-19-967733-7"><bdi>978-0-19-967733-7</bdi></a>.</cite><span title="ctx&#95;ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft&#95;val&#95;fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Abook&amp;rft.genre=book&amp;rft.btitle=The+Oxford+Companion+to+Food&amp;rft.pages=409&amp;rft.pub=Oxford+University+Press&amp;rft.date=2014&amp;rft.isbn=978-0-19-967733-7&amp;rft.aulast=Davidson&amp;rft.aufirst=Alan&amp;rft&#95;id=https%3A%2F%2Fbooks.google.com%2Fbooks%3Fid%3DRL6LAwAAQBAJ%26pg%3DPA409&amp;rfr&#95;id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3AIndia" class="Z3988"></span></span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-Davidson2014-p161-445"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-Davidson2014-p161_445-0">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite id="CITEREFDavidson2014" class="citation cs1">Davidson, Alan (2014). <a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://books.google.com/books?id=RL6LAwAAQBAJ&amp;pg=PA161"><i>The Oxford Companion to Food</i></a>. <a href="/wiki/Oxford_University_Press" title="Oxford University Press">Oxford University Press</a>. p.&#160;161. <a href="/wiki/ISBN_(identifier)" class="mw-redirect" title="ISBN (identifier)">ISBN</a>&#160;<a href="/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-0-19-967733-7" title="Special:BookSources/978-0-19-967733-7"><bdi>978-0-19-967733-7</bdi></a>. <q>Chapatis are made from finely milled whole-wheat flour, called chapati flour or atta, and water.  The dough is rolled into thin rounds which vary in size from region to region and then cooked without fat or oil on a slightly curved griddle called a tava.</q></cite><span title="ctx&#95;ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft&#95;val&#95;fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Abook&amp;rft.genre=book&amp;rft.btitle=The+Oxford+Companion+to+Food&amp;rft.pages=161&amp;rft.pub=Oxford+University+Press&amp;rft.date=2014&amp;rft.isbn=978-0-19-967733-7&amp;rft.aulast=Davidson&amp;rft.aufirst=Alan&amp;rft&#95;id=https%3A%2F%2Fbooks.google.com%2Fbooks%3Fid%3DRL6LAwAAQBAJ%26pg%3DPA161&amp;rfr&#95;id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3AIndia" class="Z3988"></span></span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-tamang-yeast-idlidosa-446"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-tamang-yeast-idlidosa_446-0">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite id="CITEREFTamangFleet2009" class="citation cs1">Tamang, J. P.; Fleet, G. H. (2009). <a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://books.google.com/books?id=jLFmiervaqMC&amp;pg=PA180">"Yeasts Diversity in Fermented Foods and Beverages"</a>. In Satyanarayana, T.; Kunze, G. (eds.). <i>Yeast Biotechnology: Diversity and Applications</i>. Springer. p.&#160;180. <a href="/wiki/ISBN_(identifier)" class="mw-redirect" title="ISBN (identifier)">ISBN</a>&#160;<a href="/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-1-4020-8292-4" title="Special:BookSources/978-1-4020-8292-4"><bdi>978-1-4020-8292-4</bdi></a>. <q>Idli is an acid-leavened and steamed cake made by bacterial fermentation of a thick batter made from coarsely ground rice and dehulled black gram. Idli cakes are soft, moist and spongy, have desirable sour flavour, and is eaten as breakfast in South India. Dosa batter is very similar to idli batter, except that both the rice and black gram are finely grounded. The batter is thinner than that of idli and is fried as a thin, crisp pancake and eaten directly in South India.</q></cite><span title="ctx&#95;ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft&#95;val&#95;fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Abook&amp;rft.genre=bookitem&amp;rft.atitle=Yeasts+Diversity+in+Fermented+Foods+and+Beverages&amp;rft.btitle=Yeast+Biotechnology%3A+Diversity+and+Applications&amp;rft.pages=180&amp;rft.pub=Springer&amp;rft.date=2009&amp;rft.isbn=978-1-4020-8292-4&amp;rft.aulast=Tamang&amp;rft.aufirst=J.+P.&amp;rft.au=Fleet%2C+G.+H.&amp;rft&#95;id=https%3A%2F%2Fbooks.google.com%2Fbooks%3Fid%3DjLFmiervaqMC%26pg%3DPA180&amp;rfr&#95;id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3AIndia" class="Z3988"></span></span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-jhala-princely-biryani-447"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-jhala-princely-biryani_447-0">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite id="CITEREFJhala2015" class="citation cs1">Jhala, Angma Day (2015). <a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://books.google.com/books?id=WGpECgAAQBAJ&amp;pg=PA70"><i>Royal Patronage, Power and Aesthetics in Princely India</i></a>. Routledge. p.&#160;70. <a href="/wiki/ISBN_(identifier)" class="mw-redirect" title="ISBN (identifier)">ISBN</a>&#160;<a href="/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-1-317-31657-2" title="Special:BookSources/978-1-317-31657-2"><bdi>978-1-317-31657-2</bdi></a>. <q>With the ascent of the Mughal Empire in sixteenth-century India, Turkic, Persian and Afghan traditions of dress, 'architecture and cuisine' were adopted by non-Muslim indigenous elites in South Asia. In this manner, Central Asian cooking merged with older traditions within the subcontinent, to create such signature dishes as biryani (a fusion of the Persian pilau and the spice-laden dishes of Hindustan),  and the Kashmiri meat stew of Rogan Josh. It not only generated new dishes and entire cuisines, but also fostered novel modes of eating. Such newer trends included the consumption of Persian condiments, which relied heavily on almonds, pastries and quince jams, alongside Indian achars made from sweet limes, green vegetables and curds as side relishes during Mughlai meals.</q></cite><span title="ctx&#95;ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft&#95;val&#95;fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Abook&amp;rft.genre=book&amp;rft.btitle=Royal+Patronage%2C+Power+and+Aesthetics+in+Princely+India&amp;rft.pages=70&amp;rft.pub=Routledge&amp;rft.date=2015&amp;rft.isbn=978-1-317-31657-2&amp;rft.aulast=Jhala&amp;rft.aufirst=Angma+Day&amp;rft&#95;id=https%3A%2F%2Fbooks.google.com%2Fbooks%3Fid%3DWGpECgAAQBAJ%26pg%3DPA70&amp;rfr&#95;id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3AIndia" class="Z3988"></span></span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-Davidson2014-p410-448"><span class="mw-cite-backlink">^ <a href="#cite_ref-Davidson2014-p410_448-0"><sup><i><b>a</b></i></sup></a> <a href="#cite_ref-Davidson2014-p410_448-1"><sup><i><b>b</b></i></sup></a></span> <span class="reference-text"><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite id="CITEREFDavidson2014" class="citation cs1">Davidson, Alan (2014). <a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://books.google.com/books?id=RL6LAwAAQBAJ&amp;pg=PA410"><i>The Oxford Companion to Food</i></a>. <a href="/wiki/Oxford_University_Press" title="Oxford University Press">Oxford University Press</a>. p.&#160;410. <a href="/wiki/ISBN_(identifier)" class="mw-redirect" title="ISBN (identifier)">ISBN</a>&#160;<a href="/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-0-19-967733-7" title="Special:BookSources/978-0-19-967733-7"><bdi>978-0-19-967733-7</bdi></a>.</cite><span title="ctx&#95;ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft&#95;val&#95;fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Abook&amp;rft.genre=book&amp;rft.btitle=The+Oxford+Companion+to+Food&amp;rft.pages=410&amp;rft.pub=Oxford+University+Press&amp;rft.date=2014&amp;rft.isbn=978-0-19-967733-7&amp;rft.aulast=Davidson&amp;rft.aufirst=Alan&amp;rft&#95;id=https%3A%2F%2Fbooks.google.com%2Fbooks%3Fid%3DRL6LAwAAQBAJ%26pg%3DPA410&amp;rfr&#95;id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3AIndia" class="Z3988"></span></span>
</li>

<li id="cite&#95;note-sengupta-74-454"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-sengupta-74_454-0">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite id="CITEREFSengupta2014" class="citation cs1">Sengupta, Jayanta (2014). <a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://books.google.com/books?id=SNQkDQAAQBAJ&amp;pg=PA74">"India"</a>. In Freedman, Paul; Chaplin, Joyce E.; Albala, Ken (eds.). <i>Food in Time and Place: The American Historical Association Companion to Food History</i>. <a href="/wiki/University_of_California_Press" title="University of California Press">University of California Press</a>. p.&#160;74. <a href="/wiki/ISBN_(identifier)" class="mw-redirect" title="ISBN (identifier)">ISBN</a>&#160;<a href="/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-0-520-27745-8" title="Special:BookSources/978-0-520-27745-8"><bdi>978-0-520-27745-8</bdi></a>.</cite><span title="ctx&#95;ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft&#95;val&#95;fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Abook&amp;rft.genre=bookitem&amp;rft.atitle=India&amp;rft.btitle=Food+in+Time+and+Place%3A+The+American+Historical+Association+Companion+to+Food+History&amp;rft.pages=74&amp;rft.pub=University+of+California+Press&amp;rft.date=2014&amp;rft.isbn=978-0-520-27745-8&amp;rft.aulast=Sengupta&amp;rft.aufirst=Jayanta&amp;rft&#95;id=https%3A%2F%2Fbooks.google.com%2Fbooks%3Fid%3DSNQkDQAAQBAJ%26pg%3DPA74&amp;rfr&#95;id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3AIndia" class="Z3988"></span></span>

<li id="cite&#95;note-nandy2004-455"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-nandy2004_455-0">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite id="CITEREFNandy2004" class="citation cs1"><a href="/wiki/Ashis_Nandy" title="Ashis Nandy">Nandy, Ashis</a> (2004). <ul><li><span class="flagicon"><span class="mw-image-border" typeof="mw:File"><span><img alt="" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/Flag_of_Belarus.svg/40px-Flag_of_Belarus.svg.png" decoding="async" width="23" height="12" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/Flag_of_Belarus.svg/60px-Flag_of_Belarus.svg.png 2x" data-file-width="1200" data-file-height="600" /></span></span>&#160;</span><a href="/wiki/Belarus" title="Belarus">Belarus</a></li>
